title: Opalytics inference cost sensitivity discovery
preview: Initial discovery with Opalytics exploring per-token economics and hosting options. Customer provided rough traffic and tight budget constraints; discussed batching, prefix/KV caching, quantization, reserved capacity vs hosted burst, and security controls (SOC2, SSO, VPC). Agreed next steps: send detailed pricing worksheet, share SOC2 and network diagram, scope a 1-week perf pilot on Dedicated + hosted fallback.
text: Initial discovery with Opalytics exploring per-token economics and hosting options. Customer provided rough traffic and tight budget constraints; discussed batching, prefix/KV caching, quantization, reserved capacity vs hosted burst, and security controls (SOC2, SSO, VPC). Agreed next steps: send detailed pricing worksheet, share SOC2 and network diagram, scope a 1-week perf pilot on Dedicated + hosted fallback.

title: Large Context & High TPS Backpressure Workshop - NovaFin
preview: Demo/workshop focused on handling ~100k-token contexts, sustaining high TPS while avoiding streaming backpressure and timeouts. Covered runtime behavior, KV/prefix caching strategies, batching vs latency tradeoffs, graceful fallback to smaller models, and Console/Optimize suggestions. Agreed to run a Dedicated pilot and share a long-context benchmark spreadsheet.
text: Demo/workshop focused on handling ~100k-token contexts, sustaining high TPS while avoiding streaming backpressure and timeouts. Covered runtime behavior, KV/prefix caching strategies, batching vs latency tradeoffs, graceful fallback to smaller models, and Console/Optimize suggestions. Agreed to run a Dedicated pilot and share a long-context benchmark spreadsheet.

title: Quantization regression workshop - KV aging, batching, and VPC constraints
preview: Deep technical triage of a post-quantize accuracy regression observed on Aurelia's internal clinical summarization workload. Focus areas: INT8/AWQ vs FP8 behavior, GPTQ layer sensitivity, KV cache staleness and affinity, batching-induced tokenization edge-cases, and constraints from VPC/on-prem networking. Agreed to run targeted bisect with layer probes, share benchmark artifacts and security checklist; Redwood to provide a canary config and a small script to replay failing seeds in a private VPC test cluster.

Agreed to run targeted bisect with layer probes, share benchmark artifacts and security checklist; Redwood to provide a canary config and a small script to replay failing seeds in a private VPC test cluster.

title: Pilot Sprint 4 — latency & throughput show-and-tell (Solis Dynamics)
preview: Quick sync to review Sprint 4 benchmark results vs incumbent, validate p50/p95 deltas, discuss tail latency incidents and batching/quantization tuning. Agreed to run 24h traffic trace, try dedicated slot for short window, and share raw traces and benchmark sheet.
text: Quick sync to review Sprint 4 benchmark results vs incumbent, validate p50/p95 deltas, discuss tail latency incidents and batching/quantization tuning. Agreed to run 24h traffic trace, try dedicated slot for short window, and share raw traces and benchmark sheet.

title: StrataShield LLM cost playbook kickoff
preview: Kickoff to scope a POC to validate per-token unit economics for an insurance claims triage LLM feature. Main goals: define success criteria (latency/cost/SLA), capture workload size and token distribution, agree on benchmarks and timeline for a 48-hour cost + perf run. Customer emphasized budget ceiling and on-prem constraints for sensitive data.

title: Elysium Labs — Dedicated capacity discovery
preview: Discovery focused on sizing a dedicated reserved GPU footprint for Elysium's generative biology inference workloads. Main concerns: tenant isolation, burst handling (warm pools vs. prewarm), eviction tolerance, data residency and KMS integrations. Agreed to run a 2-week pilot with a small reserved pool and benchmark synthetic bursts; Redwood to send pricing and SOC2/KMS docs.

Action items assigned for benchmark plan and SSO test account.

title: Orchid Edge - Open model eval & private deployment scoping
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-27
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Liam O'Connor (Redwood SE), Samira Holt (Orchid Edge - CTO), Declan Wu (Orchid Edge - ML Eng), Tanya Ruiz (Orchid Edge - Head of Security), Miguel Reyes (Orchid Edge - Product)

Summary: High-level discovery to understand Orchid Edge's inference constraints: context lengths up to 100k tokens for legal doc summarization, strict data residency and KMS requirements, multilingual (EN/ES/PT) needs, and tool-calling to link to internal search and billing systems. Customer is evaluating hosted vs dedicated vs private deployment; wants benchmark numbers and security pack.

Topics covered: model family comparisons (Mistral-style, Llama variants, Redmond-verified/open quantized options), KV/prefix caching, batching tradeoffs, network egress and VPC peering, on-prem packaging, and pilot scope.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: All right, welcome everyone. Thanks for taking the time. I'm Jordan with Redwood, and with me are Priya and Liam on the engineering side. How are you folks doing?
[00:06] Samira: Doing well, thanks. Appreciate you fitting us in. We've got a tight window to decide infra for the Q3 pilot.
[00:12] Declan: Hey, quick note — we want concrete perf numbers with our own docs, not just generic bench marks.
[00:18] Priya: Totally, that's our plan. We'll run a bench with your sample corpus. Quick pre-qualify q: what volumes and latencies are you expecting for the pilot?
[00:28] Miguel: For the prototype, maybe 20 requests per minute, but each request can be a 30k to 100k token context — legal contracts, sometimes multi-doc. Latency target is a bit fuzzy but sub-8s for a 32k context would be ideal. For 100k, we expect higher but want to understand costs.
[00:46] Priya: Got it. So workload is heavy on context length. That steers model choice and runtime — we can do 32k easily on a bunch of open models, 100k is more specialized and depends on model architecture and KV cache support.
[00:58] Tanya: Security is the other big piece — we need KMS integration, audit logs, SSO (SAML/Okta), and the option to host in VPC or full on-prem airgapped for production.
[01:09] Liam: That's standard — Redwood Private provides an on-prem package and VPC with customer-managed network. We integrate with KMS/HSM and ship audit logs. We can share the SOC2 and the compliance pack after the call.
[01:19] Tanya: Please do — and also a note on data retention controls. Our legal needs ability to TTL or purge conversation data on demand.
[01:26] Jordan: We have retention controls and per-customer toggles. We'll include details in the security pack.
[01:32] Declan: Back to models — we're trying to choose between a few open families. Our list is Mistral-instruct-ish models, Llama-derivatives, and MPT for longer contexts. Any guidance?
[01:44] Priya: Sure. High-level: Mistral style models tend to be cost-efficient at shorter contexts and good instruction-following. Llama 2/3 family variants are flexible but you pay in memory for longer context. MPT and some newer long-context models are purpose-built for 100k+, but they often trade off generation quality on instruction tasks. Also quantization plays a role — int8/4 options reduce memory but can slightly degrade quality for complex legal language.
[02:05] Samira: Do you have Redwood-validated profiles for these? We don't want to pick and then realize latency doubles.
[02:12] Priya: We do — we have a model catalog with performance profiles. We'll run your samples across a short list: a Mistral variant, a Llama-ish 13B, and a long-context MPT-like model. For 100k we'll test a chunked retrieval+KV cache strategy vs full context model to compare quality and cost.
[02:28] Declan: Chunked retrieval meaning we send retrieval hits and then use tool-calling to pull in snippets? We're currently using an internal elasticsearch store.
[02:35] Liam: Exactly — two approaches: 1) heavy context model ingesting everything; 2) retrieval-augmented with tool-calling or inlined passages and KV cache to avoid reprocessing large prefixes. For your use case, retrieval + tools often wins cost-wise and can maintain quality if the prompt engineering is tight.
[02:52] Miguel: We're leaning to tool-calling because we already have a search endpoint and prefer to keep the corpus in our control. How mature is Redwood's tool-calling? Auth, sandboxing?
[03:02] Priya: We support function/tool calling and can wire up authenticated calls via short-lived tokens. SE note: there is a sandboxing layer for outbound calls and you can restrict destinations by policy. We'll include the integration guide.
[03:15] Tanya: One security caveat — any outbound calls must be signed and ideally go through our egress proxy. Can Redwood support that?
[03:22] Liam: Yes, we can route tool calls through a customer-managed egress point in Private or Dedicated setups. Hosted can do webhooks but if you need egress proxy and VPC peering that's Private.
[03:33] Samira: Pricing — we need hosted to compare to dedicated reserved capacity and private licensing. For initial bench and pilot which do you recommend?
[03:40] Jordan: For speed-to-value, Hosted is fastest. For predictable latency and data isolation, Dedicated (reserved GPU pools) is a middle ground. Private is for strict compliance and on-prem. For your 100k context legal workloads and egress controls I'd recommend a Private pilot for production, but we can start with Dedicated for the POC to speed up. We'll provide comparative pricing.
[03:58] Declan: Any wildcard costs? Tokenization of long docs, KV cache storage, retrieval calls?
[04:04] Priya: We break down costs per token generation, embedding units, and storage for cache. Retrieval and tool calls are usually billed as small addons or included in higher tiers — we'll enumerate. For 100k contexts, costs can be dominated by tokenization and generation unless you use retrieval + chunking.
[04:20] Miguel: Regarding latency, what's realistic for a 32k summarization on a 13B vs 70B type model on Dedicated?
[04:26] Priya: On Dedicated with optimized kernels, a 13B quantized can hit ~2-4s for a 32k-ish effective context depending on batching and KV reuse. 70B is higher memory, likely double latency unless you tune and use larger GPU instances. These are rough, we'll run your real docs to give concrete numbers.
[04:44] Tanya: About compliance — do you support per-customer key management and HSM-backed KMS? Also ISO certs?
[04:51] Liam: We integrate with customer KMS providers and support HSMs for Private. We have SOC2 Type II and are pursuing ISO27001; we'll include current cert status in the pack.
[04:59] Samira: Great. A couple of nitty-gritty things: multilingual output — EN/ES/PT — any model recommendations and how do you measure regression risk when moving from EN to multilingual tasks?
[05:10] Priya: Some open models have been fine-tuned for multi-lingual but they vary. We recommend baseline multilingual models for retrieval+RAG; for high-precision legal content, it's often better to fine-tune or instruction-tune on your labeled examples. We can surface per-language perplexity and ROUGE-like evals in our eval dashboard when we run the bench.
[05:28] Declan: Fine. For the bench, include quality metrics (ROUGE/EM) plus latency and cost per request.
[05:35] Jordan: Will do. Anything else from the security side?
[05:39] Tanya: We'll want a network diagram and the minimal trust boundary for Private deployment. Also need to see SLA language around data isolation.
[05:47] Jordan: We'll attach network diagrams and the SLA excerpt.
[05:50] Miguel: One more question — fallback strategies. If a model variant is overloaded, can Redwood route to a cheaper fallback? We need graceful degradation.
[05:58] Liam: Yes, smart routing can fall back to compatible variants by policy (latency/cost/region). You can configure canaries and automatic fallback policies in the Redwood Console. We'll show that in the next demo.
[06:10] Samira: OK sounds good. Let's talk about pilot scope — timeline wise we want a pilot in June for internal validation with maybe 2-3k docs. Is that feasible?
[06:20] Jordan: Yes, feasible. We'll follow the steps: 1) you send sample docs and access constraints, 2) we run bench and share results, 3) review and scope pilot. We'll aim for a June pilot start.
[06:30] Declan: Who will run the actual benchmark on our data — Redwood or do we provision something for us to run?
[06:35] Priya: We can run it on Redwood Dedicated infra with your dataset under an NDA, or we can give you a sandbox package to run locally for Private. For speed, Redwood-run Dedicated bench tends to be fastest.
[06:47] Tanya: If data sensitivity is high, we'd prefer Redwood-run Dedicated under a strict DPA and limited retention.
[06:53] Jordan: Noted — we'll include DPA language and retention options.
[06:57] Samira: Cool. Any blockers on your side to produce the bench?
[06:59] Priya: We need sample docs (Declan), desired quality metrics, and an hour of alignment on which model variants to test. We'll deliver results in about a week after we receive data.
[07:10] Jordan: I'll send calendar invites and the security pack. Priya will own the benchmark.
[07:15] Miguel: Thanks everyone, that's helpful.
[07:18] Jordan: Great, recap next steps in the notes and we'll follow up. Thanks all.

End of transcript.

References shared in call: Redwood security summary (link placeholder), Benchmark template (link placeholder), Model catalog snapshot (link placeholder).

Note: transcription includes occasional mis-hearings and filler words; speaker labels are consolidated where Fireflies auto-attributed multiple short utterances.

title: 2026-04-27-fallback-coverage-gap-closure-and-runbook-transfer
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-04-27 15:30 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Liam O'Connor (Redwood CSM); Maya Patel (Solstice VP Eng), Tom Bowers (SRE), Hana Kim (Security), Evan Ruiz (Product)
Meeting ID: ff-20260427-8732

Summary:
Quick bridgecall to close the coverage gaps found in last week's incident postmortem. Primary goals: (1) agree on deterministic fallback ordering and coverage (hosted -> dedicated -> private?); (2) transfer the designed runbook to Solstice SREs; (3) validate that KMS/encryption answers satisfy legal; (4) schedule a canary that proves fallthrough works end-to-end.

Topics:
- Fallback ordering and partial-response handling
- Runbook ownership and transfer checklist
- KMS / data residency questions outstanding
- Canary test plan and success criteria

Action items (discussed): see metadata above

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: All right, good morning / afternoon everyone, thanks for joining. Uh this is Jordan from Redwood, I'm the AE on the account, we wanted to run through the remediation items from last week's outage and make sure we've got a clean handoff of the fallback runbook. Priya will drive the technical pieces.
[00:18] Maya: Hey Jordan, thanks. We had a pretty... yeah, rough day last week. The postmortem called out that our fallback coverage missed the dedicated pool evictions and then we had a cascade to our downstream aggregator. So main ask is: can we guarantee a non‑surprising behavior when Dedicated capacity stars to throttle?
[00:36] Priya: Mm hm. So recap from our side — the root was a combination of a very long sequence length and aggressive autoscale shed that caused G P U pool reclaims. The runtime currently prefers local KV cache warms, and when the KV cache was lost we didn't have an immediate lower‑cost variant to route to because the routing policy wasn't covering the compact quantized variant.
[00:55] Tom: just to clarify, when you say "compact quantized variant" you mean the Q4/INT8 variant that we tested in the bench spreadsheet? I think we saw different perf numbers there.
[01:06] Priya: Right, sorry — the q4_0 style variant, the smaller memory footprint model. The perf delta is about 20–30% latency increase but 3x throughput headroom; we should route to that when full capacity can't be maintained.
[01:20] Maya: Our concern is user‑impact: we don't want degraded hallucinations or higher token cost unexpectedly; product will be upset if accuracy changes mid‑flight.
[01:28] Evan: yeah product wants explicit signal when a fallback happens so UX can show a degraded mode.
[01:34] Jordan: That's reasonable. Proposal: one, explicit route policy that includes a tokenized header in the response indicating fallback reason; two, canary a 2% traffic cutover to quantized model with monitoring on top‑k completions and perplexity on a reduced prompt set.
[01:51] Hana: Quick security question — when you talk about fallbacks across private and dedicated, where does the K M S key live and does the tokenization header leak PII? Our legal has to sign off.
[02:05] Priya: Good question. For Private deployments the customer‑managed KMS remains in your VPC; Redwood does not hold private keys. For Dedicated and Hosted, encryption at rest uses our managed KMS but we offer customer‑managed keys via BYOK for enterprise customers — we've documented this in the security FAQ. The token header would be low‑entropy (e.g., "fallback=quantized-v2") and contains no PII.
[02:27] Hana: okay need to confirm the BYOK flow with our KMS team, I'll add that as an action.
[02:33] Jordan: Noted. Priya — can you walk through the fallback ordering you recommend?
[02:38] Priya: Sure. Short version: prefer non‑blocking fallbacks that maintain semantic parity. Order: 1) Same model family, quantized variant on same node pool (q1) 2) Different instance type in same region with warm prefix cache (q2) 3) Region fallback to a cold cache variant with shorter max tokens (q3) 4) As last resort, degrade to structured‑response mode (e.g., return a compact summary with confidence score) rather than partial stream. The key is to avoid silent truncation or mid‑stream replacement.
[03:05] Tom: For step 1, how fast is the runtime at swapping to q1? We need SLA for failover < 500ms or we risk user timeouts.
[03:12] Priya: The model switch itself is about 120–300ms for warm KV cache cases; cold start can be multiple seconds. But with prewarmed pool and automated eviction hooks (we'll push an autoscale annotation), we can keep the critical path under ~400ms for targeted flows. The numbers vary by sequence length.
[03:30] Jordan: We'll include explicit SLOs in the runbook. Liam: anything from your CSM angle?
[03:36] Liam: Just that we should agree on the notification flow. When fallback occurs, Redwood will emit an event to the customer's webhook and put an entry in the console tracing with the request id. That event will also include the fallback reason code. Customer must subscribe to the "fallback" channel.
[03:51] Maya: Good. Two more cross‑cutting items: (a) cost plumbing — we need a clear commercial estimate if 10% of traffic hits the dedicated fallback variant; (b) observability — we want token‑level cost breakdown and a delta report week over week.
[04:05] Jordan: Jordan will do cost estimate for 10% fallback and provide a delta worksheet.
[04:10] Evan: we already have an internal benchmark sheet; can Redwood ingest the test vectors and run the canary against a mirrored cluster?
[04:18] Priya: Yes, we can mirror with a shadow traffic config. We'll need a small set (1000 requests) for the first sanity canary. Please upload the vector set to the shared drive link — can you confirm?
[04:28] Tom: I'll upload by end of day tomorrow.
[04:31] Jordan: Great. Now runbook transfer: Priya has put together a draft fallback runbook and a checklist. Priya, talk us through the ownership handoff.
[04:40] Priya: The runbook contains: 1) detection heuristics (throttled errors, queue length thresholds), 2) automated remediation (route rewrite rules + prewarm hooks), 3) manual escalation steps (how to pin to specific model versions), 4) rollback play (how to revert to previous routing policy). We'll hand it over as a Google doc and we also have the machine‑readable policy snippets for your infra team.
[04:57] Maya: We prefer the machine‑readable snippets. Our infra is Terraform based and we'd rather avoid copy/pasting.
[05:04] Priya: Understood — we'll provide Terraform‑style examples and a JSON policy blob. Note: some customers had parsing issues if they tried to apply the blob without the proper RBAC in Redwood Console, so we'll include the RBAC checklist.
[05:18] Hana: On RBAC, can we restrict fallback toggles to a small admin group? We don't want product engineers accidentally flipping fallbacks.
[05:25] Jordan: Yes, access controls are role‑based; we'll document recommended roles and least‑privilege mapping.
[05:31] Tom: One point from the SRE side — last time logs were sparse during the spike. We need longer retention for fallback traces at least for two weeks. Is that doable?
[05:41] Liam: We can bump retention for the account as an add‑on; I'll propose a retention tier and cost in the followup.
[05:47] Maya: Also, the postmortem recommended we simulate a mid‑stream model degrade — does Redwood support stream replacement without reconnect? We saw partial outputs previously.
[05:58] Priya: We do support streaming fallback but we don't switch models mid‑stream by default. Instead we recommend switching between messages (i.e., last completed chunk then new stream). That avoids inconsistent context. For UX you can show a single combined response with a flag.
[06:12] Evan: Will that require SDK changes?
[06:14] Priya: Minimal — we have a client flag for "allow_stream_handoff" but it's off by default. We'll instrument it in the canary.
[06:20] Jordan: Ok let's talk canary success criteria: Priya?
[06:24] Priya: Success = (1) no user‑facing errors during 1000 mirrored requests, (2) latency under 2x baseline for 95th percentile, (3) quality regression < 2% on our eval set (we'll share the prompt set), (4) fallback telemetry events correlate with expected triggers and are visible in the console traces within 30s.
[06:44] Tom: We can accept those. We'll also add a business rule: if hallucination metric > 1.5x baseline, halt the canary.
[06:52] Hana: Security followup — a note on BYOK: we need the exact flow for key rotation and whether Redwood can request rewrapping rights. Please add that to the security FAQ.
[07:03] Priya: Will do. We'll add a KMS rotation appendix and explicitly note that Redwood cannot rotate customer keys without permission.
[07:11] Jordan: Perfect. Closing recap — I'll read back the next steps: Priya uploads annotated runbook and terraform snippets by 2026‑05‑01, Jordan sends the 10% fallback cost estimate by 2026‑05‑02, Hana shares BYOK/legal constraints by 2026‑05‑03, Tom runs a synthetic test and uploads vectors by 2026‑05‑06, and we schedule a 60‑minute canary session week of 2026‑05‑04. Does that align?
[07:34] Maya: Yep. Also please share the event webhook sample and the console trace example so our infra can wire it.
[07:41] Liam: I'll include the webhook sample in the same bundle as the runbook.
[07:45] Evan: One last thing — can Redwood provide a brief exec one‑pager we can share with our legal and ledger teams summarizing the mitigation? They want a non‑technical summary.
[07:55] Jordan: Yes, we'll attach an executive summary with the runbook package.
[07:59] Hana: Thanks, that helps.
[08:02] Jordan: Great, thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the links in an email and create a calendar invite for the canary kickoff.
[08:09] Maya: Thanks all.
[08:12] (call ends)

References / shared artifacts mentioned:

- Canary vectors spreadsheet (customer) — to be uploaded to same folder by Tom: https://shared.company/solstice/canary-vectors

Meeting ID: ff-20260427-8732

Summary:
Quick bridgecall to close the coverage gaps found in last week's incident postmortem. Primary goals: (1) agree on deterministic fallback ordering and coverage (hosted -> dedicated -> private?); (2) transfer the designed runbook to Solstice SREs; (3) validate that KMS/encryption answers satisfy legal; (4) schedule a canary that proves fallthrough works end-to-end.

Topics:
- Fallback ordering and partial-response handling
- Runbook ownership and transfer checklist
- KMS / data residency questions outstanding
- Canary test plan and success criteria

Action items (discussed): see metadata above

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: All right, good morning / afternoon everyone, thanks for joining. Uh this is Jordan from Redwood, I'm the AE on the account, we wanted to run through the remediation items from last week's outage and make sure we've got a clean handoff of the fallback runbook. Priya will drive the technical pieces.
[00:18] Maya: Hey Jordan, thanks. We had a pretty... yeah, rough day last week. The postmortem called out that our fallback coverage missed the dedicated pool evictions and then we had a cascade to our downstream aggregator. So main ask is: can we guarantee a non‑surprising behavior when Dedicated capacity stars to throttle?
[00:36] Priya: Mm hm. So recap from our side — the root was a combination of a very long sequence length and aggressive autoscale shed that caused G P U pool reclaims. The runtime currently prefers local KV cache warms, and when the KV cache was lost we didn't have an immediate lower‑cost variant to route to because the routing policy wasn't covering the compact quantized variant.
[00:55] Tom: just to clarify, when you say "compact quantized variant" you mean the Q4/INT8 variant that we tested in the bench spreadsheet? I think we saw different perf numbers there.
[01:06] Priya: Right, sorry — the q4_0 style variant, the smaller memory footprint model. The perf delta is about 20–30% latency increase but 3x throughput headroom; we should route to that when full capacity can't be maintained.
[01:20] Maya: Our concern is user‑impact: we don't want degraded hallucinations or higher token cost unexpectedly; product will be upset if accuracy changes mid‑flight.
[01:28] Evan: yeah product wants explicit signal when a fallback happens so UX can show a degraded mode.
[01:34] Jordan: That's reasonable. Proposal: one, explicit route policy that includes a tokenized header in the response indicating fallback reason; two, canary a 2% traffic cutover to quantized model with monitoring on top‑k completions and perplexity on a reduced prompt set.
[01:51] Hana: Quick security question — when you talk about fallbacks across private and dedicated, where does the K M S key live and does the tokenization header leak PII? Our legal has to sign off.
[02:05] Priya: Good question. For Private deployments the customer‑managed KMS remains in your VPC; Redwood does not hold private keys. For Dedicated and Hosted, encryption at rest uses our managed KMS but we offer customer‑managed keys via BYOK for enterprise customers — we've documented this in the security FAQ. The token header would be low‑entropy (e.g., "fallback=quantized-v2") and contains no PII.
[02:27] Hana: okay need to confirm the BYOK flow with our KMS team, I'll add that as an action.
[02:33] Jordan: Noted. Priya — can you walk through the fallback ordering you recommend?
[02:38] Priya: Sure. Short version: prefer non‑blocking fallbacks that maintain semantic parity. Order: 1) Same model family, quantized variant on same node pool (q1) 2) Different instance type in same region with warm prefix cache (q2) 3) Region fallback to a cold cache variant with shorter max tokens (q3) 4) As last resort, degrade to structured‑response mode (e.g., return a compact summary with confidence score) rather than partial stream. The key is to avoid silent truncation or mid‑stream replacement.
[03:05] Tom: For step 1, how fast is the runtime at swapping to q1? We need SLA for failover < 500ms or we risk user timeouts.
[03:12] Priya: The model switch itself is about 120–300ms for warm KV cache cases; cold start can be multiple seconds. But with prewarmed pool and automated eviction hooks (we'll push an autoscale annotation), we can keep the critical path under ~400ms for targeted flows. The numbers vary by sequence length.
[03:30] Jordan: We'll include explicit SLOs in the runbook. Liam: anything from your CSM angle?
[03:36] Liam: Just that we should agree on the notification flow. When fallback occurs, Redwood will emit an event to the customer's webhook and put an entry in the console tracing with the request id. That event will also include the fallback reason code. Customer must subscribe to the "fallback" channel.
[03:51] Maya: Good. Two more cross‑cutting items: (a) cost plumbing — we need a clear commercial estimate if 10% of traffic hits the dedicated fallback variant; (b) observability — we want token‑level cost breakdown and a delta report week over week.
[04:05] Jordan: Jordan will do cost estimate for 10% fallback and provide a delta worksheet.
[04:10] Evan: we already have an internal benchmark sheet; can Redwood ingest the test vectors and run the canary against a mirrored cluster?
[04:18] Priya: Yes, we can mirror with a shadow traffic config. We'll need a small set (1000 requests) for the first sanity canary. Please upload the vector set to the shared drive link — can you confirm?
[04:28] Tom: I'll upload by end of day tomorrow.
[04:31] Jordan: Great. Now runbook transfer: Priya has put together a draft fallback runbook and a checklist. Priya, talk us through the ownership handoff.
[04:40] Priya: The runbook contains: 1) detection heuristics (throttled errors, queue length thresholds), 2) automated remediation (route rewrite rules + prewarm hooks), 3) manual escalation steps (how to pin to specific model versions), 4) rollback play (how to revert to previous routing policy). We'll hand it over as a Google doc and we also have the machine‑readable policy snippets for your infra team.
[04:57] Maya: We prefer the machine‑readable snippets. Our infra is Terraform based and we'd rather avoid copy/pasting.
[05:04] Priya: Understood — we'll provide Terraform‑style examples and a JSON policy blob. Note: some customers had parsing issues if they tried to apply the blob without the proper RBAC in Redwood Console, so we'll include the RBAC checklist.
[05:18] Hana: On RBAC, can we restrict fallback toggles to a small admin group? We don't want product engineers accidentally flipping fallbacks.
[05:25] Jordan: Yes, access controls are role‑based; we'll document recommended roles and least‑privilege mapping.
[05:31] Tom: One point from the SRE side — last time logs were sparse during the spike. We need longer retention for fallback traces at least for two weeks. Is that doable?
[05:41] Liam: We can bump retention for the account as an add‑on; I'll propose a retention tier and cost in the followup.
[05:47] Maya: Also, the postmortem recommended we simulate a mid‑stream model degrade — does Redwood support stream replacement without reconnect? We saw partial outputs previously.
[05:58] Priya: We do support streaming fallback but we don't switch models mid‑stream by default. Instead we recommend switching between messages (i.e., last completed chunk then new stream). That avoids inconsistent context. For UX you can show a single combined response with a flag.
[06:12] Evan: Will that require SDK changes?
[06:14] Priya: Minimal — we have a client flag for "allow_stream_handoff" but it's off by default. We'll instrument it in the canary.
[06:20] Jordan: Ok let's talk canary success criteria: Priya?
[06:24] Priya: Success = (1) no user‑facing errors during 1000 mirrored requests, (2) latency under 2x baseline for 95th percentile, (3) quality regression < 2% on our eval set (we'll share the prompt set), (4) fallback telemetry events correlate with expected triggers and are visible in the console traces within 30s.
[06:44] Tom: We can accept those. We'll also add a business rule: if hallucination metric > 1.5x baseline, halt the canary.
[06:52] Hana: Security followup — a note on BYOK: we need the exact flow for key rotation and whether Redwood can request rewrapping rights. Please add that to the security FAQ.
[07:03] Priya: Will do. We'll add a KMS rotation appendix and explicitly note that Redwood cannot rotate customer keys without permission.
[07:11] Jordan: Perfect. Closing recap — I'll read back the next steps: Priya uploads annotated runbook and terraform snippets by 2026‑05‑01, Jordan sends the 10% fallback cost estimate by 2026‑05‑02, Hana shares BYOK/legal constraints by 2026‑05‑03, Tom runs a synthetic test and uploads vectors by 2026‑05‑06, and we schedule a 60‑minute canary session week of 2026‑05‑04. Does that align?
[07:34] Maya: Yep. Also please share the event webhook sample and the console trace example so our infra can wire it.
[07:41] Liam: I'll include the webhook sample in the same bundle as the runbook.
[07:45] Evan: One last thing — can Redwood provide a brief exec one‑pager we can share with our legal and ledger teams summarizing the mitigation? They want a non‑technical summary.
[07:55] Jordan: Yes, we'll attach an executive summary with the runbook package.
[07:59] Hana: Thanks, that helps.
[08:02] Jordan: Great, thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the links in an email and create a calendar invite for the canary kickoff.
[08:09] Maya: Thanks all.
[08:12] (call ends)

title: POC Decision Criteria & Acceptance Report Template
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date/time: 2026-04-15 08:30 PT / 16:30 GMT
- Duration: ~62 minutes
- Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Michael (Kestrel Head of Data Platforms), Sana (ML Infra), Evan (Security)

Summary: quick sign-off on go/no-go thresholds and the reporting template for the POC acceptance. Agreed on three buckets: perf (latency/throughput), quality (accuracy/regression), and security/compliance. Action items assigned and schedule for first benchmark run in staging.

Topics: POC acceptance criteria, runbook for benchmarks, reporting format (CSV + PDF + slides), timeline for execution, security gating (KMS, VPC peering, audit logs).

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: hey everyone, thanks for joining, can you hear me okay?
[00:04] Michael: yeah loud and clear, thanks Jordan.
[00:07] Priya: hi all, Priya from Redwood, I'll run through the acceptance template and the concrete thresholds.
[00:12] Sana: mm-hm, quick note, we want to avoid long windows where PII could leak, so encryption at rest and transit is a must.
[00:18] Jordan: absolutely, we'll cover the KMS integration in the security section. Evan, anything to add on your side?
[00:22] Evan: uh yeah, we'll need audit log retention at least 90 days, SSO/SAML, and a list of required signoff steps. Also curious about data residency for EU customers.
[00:31] Jordan: noted. Ok Priya, over to you.

[00:36] Priya: Alright, so the acceptance report we propose has three core sections: executive pass/fail summary, detailed benchmark results (CSV attached), and an issues/regressions appendix. For each test we capture: config, seed, request shape, p50/p90/p99 latency, tokens per request, error rate, and cost per million tokens.
[00:55] Michael: when you say "cost per million tokens" — is that host-only? we need to compare to our current baseline model running on prem.
[01:02] Priya: good Q. We'll give you both: Redwood hosted baseline and a Dedicated/Private projected cost given the GPU profile you choose. The cost numbers in the report are normalized to 1M generated tokens.
[01:13] Sana: okay, for quality metrics what are you planning? We'll run both synthetic and replay tests.
[01:18] Priya: we recommend two quality checks: 1) funcional correctness / rubric pass rate on a labeled sample (target >= 90% pass rate), and 2) regression detection via n-gram similarity + semantic similarity thresholds — for us practical go/no-go is: no more than 3% drop in pass rate vs baseline and no >0.05 drop in average semantic similarity (embedding cosine) on critical prompts.
[01:39] Michael: spelling — you mean "functional" sorry — and is 90% firm? For some of our risk-sensitive flows we need 95%.
[01:44] Priya: we can make it tiered: critical flows need 95% pass, non-critical 90%. We'll mark which prompts are critical in the shared prompt set.

[01:51] Evan: For perf, what's the p90 target? we see a lot of variance with long contexts.
[01:56] Priya: baseline target for the POC: p90 under 250ms for short responses (<=128 tokens) on Dedicated capacity; for Private (on-prem) we accept p90 under 400ms depending on GPU. Throughput goal: sustain 120 qps at 99% service availability for a 1 minute window during load test.
[02:14] Michael: 120 qps seems high — our current peak average is closer to 60 qps, but we want headroom.
[02:19] Priya: it's configurable, the POC will include a sizing exercise to pick target QPS. We'll run two load steps: 1) nominal at 60 qps and 2) burst at 120 qps, both for 10-minute steady windows in staging.

[02:30] Jordan: let's talk go/no-go rules concretely — what constitutes fail?
[02:34] Priya: propose these go/no-go thresholds for the POC:
- Perf: p90 latency for nominal workload must be <= 300ms (configurable by customer), error rate < 0.5% over the run.
- Throughput: must sustain nominal QPS for 10 minutes with no more than 2 scaling errors.
- Quality: critical flows >= 95% pass; overall prompts >= 90% pass; no >3% regression vs baseline.
- Security: KMS integration validated, VPC peering established for Private runs, and audit log collection confirmed.
- Ops: must be able to reproduce a failing request from stored traces within 24 hours.

[03:09] Michael: if a single critical flow drops to 92% during the test but average is 94%, do we auto-fail?
[03:15] Priya: we treat per-critical-flow failures as blocking — they'd be a go/no-go fail unless a compensating mitigation is agreed (e.g., prompt engineering or a rollback to previous model). We document mitigations in the appendix and can run an immediate fix window.

[03:28] Sana: about the reporting format — we prefer machine-readable outputs. Can you produce CSVs, JSON summaries, and a slide deck?
[03:33] Priya: yes, deliverables are: 1) raw CSV (per-request rows), 2) summary JSON with p50/p90/p99 and pass rates, 3) pdf slides with executive summary and recommended next steps. We'll attach the raw traces and the jmeter script.
[03:47] Jordan: example names for artifacts will be in the template. We'll put links in the final email: drive link for raw CSV, confluence for the runbook, and the slides in PDF.

[03:57] Michael: about cost accounting — we need token-level cost split by prompt vs generation. Is that doable?
[04:01] Priya: yes we can break down input vs output tokens and show cost per token bucket; also provide token distribution histogram.

[04:07] Evan: security run: when you do Private runs in our staging VPC, we need your collector IPs to be whitelisted and mutual TLS for control plane. Also KMS must be customer-managed keys.
[04:16] Jordan: that's standard — Redwood Private supports customer KMS and we can do mTLS for control plane. We'll share the IP ranges and the VPN instructions.

[04:25] Sana: another question, for the replay tests, will Redwood replay user timing (think inter-arrival times) or just concurrent QPS?
[04:30] Priya: we support both. For acceptance we typically do both: a time-series replay (realistic burstiness) and a steady-state concurrency test. We'll provide the replay harness.

[04:39] Michael: timeline — what's realistic for us to get first run results?
[04:42] Jordan: if you can send logs by 2026-04-20, we can stand up the staging run by 2026-04-24 and have initial results by 2026-04-25. Then a review on 2026-04-29.

[04:52] Priya: we'll include the exact commands and the expected environment variables in the runbook. Also note, seed randomness can cause minor variance; we include seeds in all runs to ensure reproducibility.

[05:00] Michael: what's your definition of "regression" for embeddings / semantic sims? a delta in cosine of 0.05 — is that significant?
[05:06] Priya: we flag deltas >0.05 as notable; if it affects downstream metrics (like rerank precision), it's blocking. We'll report both raw cosine deltas and per-query impact.

[05:18] Evan: compliance: do you support log redaction at collection time? Some of our logs include masked PII but we want an extra layer.
[05:25] Jordan: yes, we have a pluggable redaction stage before logs leave your VPC for Private. For hosted, we offer configurable retention and redaction rules. We'll attach the security whitepaper link.

[05:36] Michael: ok, let's role-play the acceptance report header so we know what gets stapled to the deck: pass/fail, owner, timestamp, total tests run, issues raised, recommended next step.
[05:45] Priya: exactly. Example header: "POC Acceptance: PASS/FAIL", owner: Michael, date, baseline id, run id, environment (staging-vpc-01), and summary cells for perf/quality/security.

[05:56] Jordan: one more thing — reporting cadence: we recommend hourly aggregation during the 10-minute tests and full per-request export for forensics. Do you want more frequent?
[06:03] Sana: hourly is fine for long runs; for the 10-minute steady windows, 1-minute aggregation would be ideal.

[06:10] Priya: noted. We'll provide both 1-minute and 5-minute rollups plus raw rows.

[06:16] Michael: any thoughts on A/B or canary within the POC?
[06:19] Priya: we can do a split test: 50/50 traffic for 30 minutes to compare outputs and track delta. But to keep the POC simple we suggested sequential runs first. If you want, we can add a brief A/B on the second day.

[06:30] Evan: operationally, how do we validate fallback behavior if the model becomes unavailable?
[06:34] Priya: we include a chaos test: simulate a model failure and verify automatic fallback to a lighter model variant within 15s and no more than 1% degraded responses. That's in the runbook.

[06:44] Jordan: ok, so I'll summarize the final acceptance criteria and the report template in an email. Before we wrap, anything else?

[06:49] Michael: can you include an explicit timeline row in the template: who signs off on each gate? We need to know if a product PM from Kestrel must approve.

[06:55] Jordan: yes — template will have gate owners. We'll include PM signoff, infra signoff, and security signoff fields.

[07:00] Sana: last ask: please include a sample CSV format with columns: timestamp, request_id, model_variant, input_tokens, output_tokens, p50_ms, p90_ms, p99_ms, error_code, pass_label, embed_cosine, cost_tokens.

[07:11] Priya: will do. We'll attach a sample CSV and a sample JSON summary.

[07:15] Jordan: great. Recap of next steps and owners — I'll send draft template and the runbook links by EOD 2026-04-17. Priya will prep load scripts. Sana provides logs by 4/20. Evan confirms KMS details by 4/21. Follow-up call 4/29 to review results.

[07:32] Michael: thanks all.
[07:34] Priya: thanks, bye.
[07:35] Jordan: bye.

Attachments/links mentioned:
- Drive: https://drive.redwood.ai/kestrel/poc-acceptance-samples (placeholder link)
- Runbook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INFR/POC+Runbook (placeholder)
- Security whitepaper: https://redwood.ai/security-whitepaper.pdf (placeholder)

End of transcript.

[03:09] Michael: if a single critical flow drops to 92% during the test but average is 94%, do we auto-fail?
[03:15] Priya: we treat per-critical-flow failures as blocking — they'd be a go/no-go fail unless a compensating mitigation is agreed (e.g., prompt engineering or a rollback to previous model). We document mitigations in the appendix and can run an immediate fix window.

[03:28] Sana: about the reporting format — we prefer machine-readable outputs. Can you produce CSVs, JSON summaries, and a slide deck?
[03:33] Priya: yes, deliverables are: 1) raw CSV (per-request rows), 2) summary JSON with p50/p90/p99 and pass rates, 3) pdf slides with executive summary and recommended next steps. We'll attach the raw traces and the jmeter script.
[03:47] Jordan: example names for artifacts will be in the template. We'll put links in the final email: drive link for raw CSV, confluence for the runbook, and the slides in PDF.

[03:57] Michael: about cost accounting — we need token-level cost split by prompt vs generation. Is that doable?
[04:01] Priya: yes we can break down input vs output tokens and show cost per token bucket; also provide token distribution histogram.

[04:07] Evan: security run: when you do Private runs in our staging VPC, we need your collector IPs to be whitelisted and mutual TLS for control plane. Also KMS must be customer-managed keys.
[04:16] Jordan: that's standard — Redwood Private supports customer KMS and we can do mTLS for control plane. We'll share the IP ranges and the VPN instructions.

[04:25] Sana: another question, for the replay tests, will Redwood replay user timing (think inter-arrival times) or just concurrent QPS?
[04:30] Priya: we support both. For acceptance we typically do both: a time-series replay (realistic burstiness) and a steady-state concurrency test. We'll provide the replay harness.

[04:39] Michael: timeline — what's realistic for us to get first run results?
[04:42] Jordan: if you can send logs by 2026-04-20, we can stand up the staging run by 2026-04-24 and have initial results by 2026-04-25. Then a review on 2026-04-29.

[04:52] Priya: we'll include the exact commands and the expected environment variables in the runbook. Also note, seed randomness can cause minor variance; we include seeds in all runs to ensure reproducibility.

[05:00] Michael: what's your definition of "regression" for embeddings / semantic sims? a delta in cosine of 0.05 — is that significant?
[05:06] Priya: we flag deltas >0.05 as notable; if it affects downstream metrics (like rerank precision), it's blocking. We'll report both raw cosine deltas and per-query impact.

[05:18] Evan: compliance: do you support log redaction at collection time? Some of our logs include masked PII but we want an extra layer.
[05:25] Jordan: yes, we have a pluggable redaction stage before logs leave your VPC for Private. For hosted, we offer configurable retention and redaction rules. We'll attach the security whitepaper link.

[05:36] Michael: ok, let's role-play the acceptance report header so we know what gets stapled to the deck: pass/fail, owner, timestamp, total tests run, issues raised, recommended next step.
[05:45] Priya: exactly. Example header: "POC Acceptance: PASS/FAIL", owner: Michael, date, baseline id, run id, environment (staging-vpc-01), and summary cells for perf/quality/security.

[05:56] Jordan: one more thing — reporting cadence: we recommend hourly aggregation during the 10-minute tests and full per-request export for forensics. Do you want more frequent?
[06:03] Sana: hourly is fine for long runs; for the 10-minute steady windows, 1-minute aggregation would be ideal.

[06:10] Priya: noted. We'll provide both 1-minute and 5-minute rollups plus raw rows.

[06:16] Michael: any thoughts on A/B or canary within the POC?
[06:19] Priya: we can do a split test: 50/50 traffic for 30 minutes to compare outputs and track delta. But to keep the POC simple we suggested sequential runs first. If you want, we can add a brief A/B on the second day.

[06:30] Evan: operationally, how do we validate fallback behavior if the model becomes unavailable?
[06:34] Priya: we include a chaos test: simulate a model failure and verify automatic fallback to a lighter model variant within 15s and no more than 1% degraded responses. That's in the runbook.

[06:44] Jordan: ok, so I'll summarize the final acceptance criteria and the report template in an email. Before we wrap, anything else?

[06:49] Michael: can you include an explicit timeline row in the template: who signs off on each gate? We need to know if a product PM from Kestrel must approve.

[06:55] Jordan: yes — template will have gate owners. We'll include PM signoff, infra signoff, and security signoff fields.

[07:00] Sana: last ask: please include a sample CSV format with columns: timestamp, request_id, model_variant, input_tokens, output_tokens, p50_ms, p90_ms, p99_ms, error_code, pass_label, embed_cosine, cost_tokens.

[07:11] Priya: will do. We'll attach a sample CSV and a sample JSON summary.

[07:15] Jordan: great. Recap of next steps and owners — I'll send draft template and the runbook links by EOD 2026-04-17. Priya will prep load scripts. Sana provides logs by 4/20. Evan confirms KMS details by 4/21. Follow-up call 4/29 to review results.

[07:32] Michael: thanks all.
[07:34] Priya: thanks, bye.
[07:35] Jordan: bye.

Attachments/links mentioned:
- Drive: https://drive.redwood.ai/kestrel/poc-acceptance-samples (placeholder link)
- Runbook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INFR/POC+Runbook (placeholder)
- Security whitepaper: https://redwood.ai/security-whitepaper.pdf (placeholder)

End of transcript.

title: Paladin Systems - Portout Feasibility Discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date/time: 2026-04-14 09:30 PT / 16:30 UTC
- Duration: ~57 minutes
- Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Sofia Chen (Redwood SE), Maya Ortiz (Redwood CSM); Mark Rivera (Paladin CTO), Priya Nair (Paladin Head of ML), Liam O'Connor (Paladin Infra), Dana Park (Paladin SRE)

Auto-generated summary (brief, may be incomplete):
Paladin is evaluating options to port away from vendor X (mix of OpenAI + in-house vLLM/TGI) to a provider that offers either Hosted for fast time-to-value, Dedicated for predictable throughput, or Private for strict data residency and KMS control. Main concerns: eval parity, inference latency at scale, KV/prefix cache compatibility, SSO/KMS, SOC2, cost comparisons. Agreed next steps: Redwood to send security docs and propose a 2-week benchmark/pilot using Paladin's prompts.

Topics discussed:
- Current stack and pain points
- Model/serving compatibility (vLLM/TGI parity)
- Hosting modes: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private
- Security/compliance (SOC2, SSO, KMS, audit logs)
- Performance benchmarks and pilot scope
- Commercial/pricing expectations and procurement timeline

Transcript body (timestamps approx every 20-40s, transcription imperfect):
00:00 Jordan (AE): All right good morning everyone thanks for making time. quick intro, im Jordan from Redwood — AE, i handle Paladin. uh with me is Sofia, our SE, and Maya who will be supporting during pilot. could you do quick intros on your side?
00:12 Mark (CTO): yeah hi, Mark Rivera here CTO at Paladin. we run a combination of OpenAI for some user-facing stuff and a self-hosted vllm cluster for higher-volume internal tasks.
00:22 Priya (Head of ML): Priya Nair, I lead ML. most of our prompts are tuned for instruct style models and we also run a bunch of rerank/embedding workloads.
00:30 Liam (Infra): Liam O'Connor, infra lead. i look after kubernetes, network and our KMS.
00:36 Jordan: awesome, thanks. high level — what are the primary drivers for evaluating a port-out? is it cost, latency, vendor risk?
00:45 Mark: a bit of all of that. cost spiked after we scaled, and the OpenAI bill's getting unpredictable. also we want better control over data residency and audit trails.
00:55 Priya: and eval parity — we have strict acceptance gating. when we moved some flows to vLLM internally we saw a slight drop in reply quality on edge cases, so parity with what our product teams expect is key.
01:10 Sofia (SE): that's helpful. quick Q on vLLM/TGI — are you running TGI as the serving layer on top of vllm or is it a separate stack? i want to understand compatibility concerns.
01:22 Priya: we actually use both. for some models we spin up TGI endpoints, others are direct vLLM serving. there's also a small custom kernel for our quantized weights, so compatibility with quantization/4-bit kernels matters.
01:36 Jordan: got it. so three axes: API parity (responses), model weights/quantization compatibility, and runtime semantics like KV cache and streaming.
01:50 Mark: exactly. another thing — we have a handful of regulated customers, so SOC2 plus the ability to bring your own KMS and SSO/SAML are table stakes. also audit logs retention for 1 year.
02:05 Sofia: understoood. Redwood supports SSO and we can integrate with IdP via SAML/OIDC; Private deployments include customer-managed KMS (HSM integrations) and we capture detailed request traces for auditing. i'll send the doc after this call.
02:20 Mark: great, that helps.
02:25 Maya (CSM): just to set expectations — for pilots we usually do a 2-week bench with a small set of canonical prompts; SEs will instrument latencies and token counts and we can show cost delta across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.
02:40 Liam: what about latency — our SLO is 120ms p50 for short completions and sub-300ms p95 for interactive builds. will Redwood meet that on Hosted?
02:50 Sofia: for short seqs Hosted can meet low latency for smaller models; for predictable p95 tails usually Dedicated or Private pinned capacity helps because you avoid noisy neighbors. there are also batching knobs and prefix/KV cache that can reduce per-token work.
03:08 Dana (SRE): noise — i'm curious about autoscaling. if we burst, do you automatically route to a regional fallback or degrade to smaller model? what's the behavior?
03:18 Jordan: good q. Redwood's smart routing can fallback to compatible model variants or route across regions per policy; we can configure auto-bursting to Dedicated pools or allow temporary Hosted overage. we'll include config examples.
03:34 Priya: on the eval parity topic — how do you validate model output parity? we run acceptance tests and evals tied to prompts; can we bring our eval harness?
03:44 Sofia: absolutely. we can run your evaluation suite during the benchmark and compare rouge/bleu/embedding similarity, plus we tie quality regressions to alerting. we also support structured output and function-calling equivalence for your wrappers.
03:58 Mark: have you onboarded other customers porting off OpenAI or Anthropic? curious about common blockers we should expect.
04:05 Jordan: yes, we've migrated several customers away from hosted vendors; common blockers are prompt compatibility, model family mismatches, and edge-case tokenization differences. often we need small prompt rewrites or setting different generation parameters to match behavior.
04:22 Priya: tokenization is a pain. if we quantize we worry about model weights — will Redwood accept our custom quantized artifacts or do you want float32?
04:32 Sofia: Private deployments can accept customer-provided artifacts, including quantized checkpoints, but we need to validate kernel compatibility; Dedicated pools can be configured with specific quantized runtimes. for Hosted we typically use curated artifacts for stability.
04:50 Liam: and networking — if we do Private in our VPC, what egress is required? can we keep everything internal?
04:58 Jordan: Private offers a customer-controlled control plane with optional air-gapped on-prem package. some control-plane calls (optional) can be routed via a control plane proxy; we can detail a no-egress pattern in the checklist.
05:12 Dana: KMS — do you support BYOK with HSM rotations and audit hooks?
05:18 Sofia: yes, BYOK with major KMS vendors and HSM is supported in Private. we capture CMK rotation events and can forward key audit logs to your SIEM.
05:28 Mark: procurement timeline — we're targeting a pilot in May and production in June if the pilot looks good. any blockers on Redwood side for a 2-week pilot?
05:36 Maya: timeline looks fine. we'll need rights to spin up a Dedicated trial or a Private sandbox depending on what you want to test. usually for parity checks we recommend the Dedicated pool + your prompt set first.
05:48 Priya: cost — can you give a rough delta vs our current OpenAI spend? we pay a lot for embeddings and davinci-style completions.
05:56 Jordan: rough numbers are ... (laughter) hard to pin without workload. but typical customers see 20–60% unit cost savings moving to Dedicated or Private with batching and quantization. we'll provide a more tailored estimate after we get token distributions and model sizes.
06:15 Mark: one more technical — streaming behavior. we do streaming with chunked deltas and rely on partial hydration of KV cache. will there be parity with TGI/vLLM streaming?
06:28 Sofia: streaming semantics are configurable; we emulate chunked delta streaming and support KV cache prefetching. there may be differences at microsecond granularity but functionally we can replicate the same behavior.
06:42 Priya: are there known regressions with function-calling or structured JSON outputs? we've had issues where structured outputs change slightly between infra.
06:51 Sofia: we include structured output enforcement tools and can run your structured tests during the bench. sometimes you need to tweak temperature/top-p and sampling seeds, but we try to make it non-invasive.
07:07 Liam: about benchmarks — what's the recursion: do we run on your curated models or can we run our exact model artifacts?
07:16 Jordan: for Hosted we run against our curated model variants. for Dedicated/Private we can use customer artifacts subject to validation. recommended path: run parity checks on Dedicated with your model artifact.
07:30 Mark: any compliance reports we should see? SOC2 type II, penetration test reports, ISO?
07:36 Jordan: SOC2 Type II is available, we can share a redacted report, plus pen test summary. Private customers can also get tailored attestations. we'll send links after.
07:48 Priya: regarding the tokenization/parity — could we run a quick smoke test in the next 2 weeks? maybe 2-3 prompts that are representative.
07:56 Sofia: yes i can queue a smoke run: we need your prompt files, expected outputs (or metrics), and a small dataset for embeddings if used.
08:06 Mark: i'm worried about vendor lock — what happens if we want to move again? how portable are the prompts and tokenization?
08:14 Jordan: portability is a core thesis — we support standard model artifacts and export tools. if you keep your prompts and model weights, you can move across providers. we document differences and help create a migration runbook.
08:28 Dana: operationally, who owns the logs in a Dedicated scenario? are they shipped to our ELK?
08:34 Sofia: logs can be forwarded to your logging stack; by default we retain request traces for N days per config, and Dedicated/Private customers can control retention and export.
08:44 Priya: procurement/legal question — do you accept DPAs, custom security addendums, and can you do SOC2 mapping?
08:52 Jordan: yes on DPAs; we have a standard DPA and are used to custom clauses for enterprise. we map controls to SOC2 and can provide evidence where required.
09:02 Mark: ok cool. proposed pilot then: 2-week Dedicated benchmark using our prompts, include both completions + embedding workloads, run parity evals and latency SLO measurement. is that feasible?
09:12 Maya: totally. Sofia will own the bench; Jordan will send security artifact pack; Paladin provides prompt set and expected metrics.
09:20 Priya: sounds good. quick nit — if we detect a 3–5% drop in quality on some critical tests, what's the rollback?
09:28 Sofia: we'd root cause whether it's model artifact mismatch, decoding params, or tokenization. if it's not resolvable quickly we'll fallback the flow to your existing endpoint or use a compatible model variant. we also support A/B canary and automatic fallback policies.
09:44 Mark: any costs for the pilot?
09:47 Jordan: pilots are typically credit-backed; we'll share specifics but usually no large upfront commitment for a standard 2-week bench.
09:56 Liam: ok last infra question — our quantized weights need a custom kernel; do you have prebuilt kernels for Q4_0 and Q8 variants?
10:06 Sofia: we support common quant kernels and have a process to validate new kernels; Private/Dedicated can accept kernel additions subject to testing. for Hosted we restrict kernels for stability.
10:20 Dana: any caveats for embedding parity — vector dimensionality differences?
10:25 Priya: we saw in previous portouts that embedding dimensionality and tokenization shifts cause nearest-neighbor degradation; do you allow exact embedding model families?
10:34 Jordan: yes, we have embedding families and we can host the same dimensionality sets. if you bring a model with a unique embedding dimension we can host it but you'll need to validate similarity thresholds.
10:46 Mark: ok let's recap and finalize next steps.
10:50 Jordan: recap — Redwood to deliver: SOC2/pen-test links, SSO/KMS integration doc, Private deployment checklist; Sofia to propose benchmark plan for Dedicated and infra prerequisites; Paladin to share prompt sets, expected eval metrics, and confirm timeline for a pilot in early May.
11:10 Priya: i'll get prompts over by monday and a baseline metric export.
11:15 Jordan: perfect. any final Qs?
11:20 Mark: none here, thanks everyone.
11:24 Jordan: thanks all — we'll follow up with the docs and calendar invite for the deep-dive. bye.

Action item summary (auto):
- Jordan: send security + deployment docs (due 2026-04-16)
- Sofia: benchmark plan + infra runbook (due 2026-04-20)
- Priya: provide prompt sets and eval metrics (due 2026-04-19)
- Liam: confirm KMS/egress constraints (due 2026-04-21)

End of transcript.

Discussed burst vs steady capacity, cost tradeoffs, SOC2/KMS requirements, and a short benchmark plan.

title: Horizon Health - Safety-net Oncall Handshake and Postmortem Follow-up
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-03-21
Time: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake, Priya Singh, Luis Marten, Ava Romero (Redwood); Alex Navarro, Mei Chen, Sam Ortiz, Rina Patel (Horizon Health)

Summary:
Quick sync to close out the postmortem actions from last week's outage, confirm safety-net/oncall handover for the pilot, review outstanding security/compliance blockers, and plan a low-risk benchmark in Horizon staging. Tone: pragmatic, a little tense but cooperative.

Topics:
- Root-cause recap and lingering unknowns
- Safety-net (fallbacks + graceful degrade) responsibilities
- Data residency and KMS questions from compliance
- Staging benchmark plan + KPIs
- Next steps and owners

Action items (brief): see metadata list above

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. We wanted to keep this tight — half hour-ish, plus followups. I'm Jordan, account lead. Priya and Luis from our side. Alex, Mei — thanks for making time.

[00:18] Alex Navarro: Yeah, thanks. We had a rough week so appreciate the off hours help. We're mostly looking to make sure the oncall handoff is clear and the mitigation you suggested is actually codified.

[00:32] Priya Singh: Hi all. I'll start with the quick recap — from Redwood's perspective, the primary failure mode was a KV cache eviction storm combined with an autoscale thrash during a traffic spike; some requests fell back to a heavier model path that wasn't warmed, which increased latency and caused retries. There were also a couple of LT-establishing network blips between zones.

[00:58] Mei Chen: Right, and from our logs we saw the tail latency blow up around 03:14 UTC; there's a pattern where a prefix cache miss cascades because many of our prompts share the same cold prefix — so the cache churn was worse than we'd modeled.

[01:22] Jordan Blake: That's helpful. I want to be explicit about responsibilities for the safety-net flow: Redwood will maintain the fallback policy and the canary route definitions; Horizon needs to set the priority on model variants and confirm which prompts are allowed to use the cached prefix optimization.

[01:40] Sam Ortiz: Quick clarifying question — when you say "fallback policy", is that run-time within Redwood Dedicated, or do we need to add client-side checks that preemptively choose a lighter model? We tried a client-side guard but it complicated metrics.

[02:01] Priya Singh: Good question. We recommend server-side routing, primarily because it preserves a single source of truth for capacity and fallback. Client-side guards are an option but they make tracing harder. For the pilot we'll do server-side policy and we'll expose a header so you can observe decisions in your traces.

[02:20] Rina Patel: On compliance, can you confirm that the fallback logs won't include raw prompt text? We need to be careful about PII leaving our VPC — last time some audit tooling caught free text in a debug trace.

[02:39] Ava Romero: Short answer: we can mask or hash prompt strings in logs; by default debug traces include snippets but for enterprise pilots we flip a mask-on policy. We'll include an audit example in the security pack.

[02:55] Alex Navarro: Excellent. Also, we need to talk SLA-wise — if tail latency breaches we want a documented escalation path. Who's first responder on Redwood's side?

[03:10] Luis Marten: For the pilot we'll provide a temporary oncall rotation: Luis and Ava will be primary weekday coverage; Priya will be on-call for deep infra escalations. We'll share a dedicated Slack channel and an incident bridging doc.

[03:27] Mei Chen: Can we get the oncall roster in writing? We need pager policy for our team to match.

[03:35] Jordan Blake: Yep, action item for Luis to share the rota.

[03:42] Sam Ortiz: Two technical items: 1) we want to test the prefix cache tuning you recommended (larger cache, different eviction), and 2) quantify cost impact if we stick to the warmed model path vs allow fallback — our finance team is worried.

[04:02] Priya Singh: For (1) we'll provide a param set — cache size, TTL, batching window — and a test harness to run in staging. For (2) we can run a cost delta using your traffic profile; we did a rough sim in the trial doc but we'll do a more conservative estimate with your exact sequence lengths.

[04:28] Alex Navarro: There's also one lingering unknown from the postmortem: the cross-zone network jitter. Was that AWS, or was it something in the customer routing?

[04:41] Priya Singh: Our tracing indicates packet retransmits at the edge managed by your cloud provider; we saw higher SRTT in one AZ. We're not ruling out provider-level transient issues. We'll include the packet-level logs in the handover doc.

[04:58] Mei Chen: Okay. If it's provider, we'll raise with our cloud TAM. But we still want Redwood to handle degraded performance gracefully.

[05:11] Jordan Blake: Absolutely. Let's run through a concrete safety-net play:
- Step A: Redwood notices rising tail latency and triggers fallback routing to a smaller model variant in the same family.
- Step B: Redwood reduces batch windows and disables speculative prefetch for cold prefixes.
- Step C: If latency still high, Redwood returns a short-circuit response with a 10 token summary and a warning header; Horizon can choose to requeue for async processing.

[05:45] Sam Ortiz: That short-circuit behavior — is that implemented now or is this an as-of-pilot change?

[05:53] Priya Singh: We have a proto that does short-circuits; for the pilot we'll enable it behind a feature flag and provide telemetry so you can see how often it fires.

[06:05] Rina Patel: We need the short-circuit not to expose any PII; treat it like a synthesized summary, not an excerpt.

[06:12] Ava Romero: Confirmed, summarize-only policy when short-circuiting.

[06:20] Alex Navarro: Let's talk about the security blockers — KMS integration, SSO access for our ops, and data residency. Rina's team is watching the KMS keys very closely.

[06:33] Rina Patel: We require customer-managed keys (CMKs) for any model logs that persist outside the VPC. Does Redwood support bring-your-own-key (BYOK)?

[06:42] Priya Singh: Yes, we support KMS integration and HSM for Private deployments. For the hosted pilot we can accept customer-managed keys for storage encryption and for audit logs; if you need HSM-backed signing we can discuss Private options.

[06:58] Mei Chen: Will the key operations add latency to inference? Our compliance team is concerned about chattiness to KMS for every request.

[07:07] Priya Singh: We don't call KMS per inference token — keys are used for at-rest encryption and for signing audit artifacts. For per-request auth we use short-lived service tokens, so runtime impact is minimal. We'll include an architecture diagram in the security pack to show call flow.

[07:23] Jordan Blake: On data residency, Horizon required that logs from the pilot remain in EU region — I think your main cluster is in us-west, correct?

[07:33] Alex Navarro: Correct, primary is us-west but we need EU for PHI concern.

[07:39] Priya Singh: For the pilot we can provision a regional endpoint in eu-west-1. It'll be a small dedicated pool; there will be a modest add-on for regional isolation. We'll outline costs in the pricing doc.

[07:52] Sam Ortiz: Good. Also want to lock down audit retention — how long will you keep obfuscated logs vs raw debug?

[07:59] Ava Romero: By default we keep obfuscated audit logs for 90 days; raw debug is disabled for enterprise pilots or retained only with explicit opt-in for a short period. We'll make retention configurable.

[08:14] Mei Chen: Moving onto the benchmark — can you run a staging job that simulates our peak burst: 18k rps over 3 minutes with mixed sequence lengths (2k short, 30% medium, 5% long)?

[08:28] Priya Singh: Yes. We'll use Redwood Dedicated parity to emulate capacity: set up the same batch windows and cache settings. We may need a small harness agent deployed in your VPC to replay traffic without sending PHI to Redwood's control plane.

[08:45] Alex Navarro: We'd prefer a replay agent to reduce data leaving our environment. Is that supported?

[08:50] Priya Singh: Yep, we have a replay agent (sometimes auto-called 'firewall-agent' in previous docs — transcription note: might be 'fairwall') that runs in customer VPC, signs requests with short-lived creds and only sends tokens to the Redwood endpoint. We'll include deployment steps.

[09:06] Sam Ortiz: If we run the replay agent, will Redwood still see prompt contents for caching decisions? We need to ensure the cache keys are hashed and not stored raw.

[09:18] Priya Singh: Cache keys are hashed and salted at the edge; we never store raw prompt text in the global cache snapshot that's visible to Redwood multitenant layers. For a dedicated/private pool, keys remain in your dedicated cache instances.

[09:35] Mei Chen: There's one more earlier item: last postmortem called out a runaway retry loop from our client SDK when it saw a 503; that created extra pressure. Have you got a recommended client-side backoff?

[09:48] Priya Singh: We recommend exponential backoff with jitter and a max retry budget per token. Also set client-side max inflight to avoid compounding. We'll share suggested defaults for our SDKs.

[10:02] Alex Navarro: Can you put those defaults in writing — our SDK team wants exact numbers.

[10:06] Priya Singh: Yep, action item.

[10:12] [Brief silence and note about screen share where Priya opened the runbook]

[10:22] Priya Singh: I'm going to walk through the runbook bits we updated: detect-level thresholds, automatic fallbacks, alert routing, and runbook links. The thresholds are adaptive by default but for the pilot we'll set fixed SLO thresholds you can change.

[10:40] Jordan Blake: Do you have a sample alert play? We want to ensure our oncall doesn't escalate too early.

[10:47] Priya Singh: Yes — three-tier alerts: 1) Warning (5m average tail > 500ms), 2) Critical (5m tail > 1.5s or error rate > 2%), 3) Outage (service unavailable > 2m). Warning goes to Slack ops channel, Critical pages Redwood oncall and Horizon primary, Outage triggers bridge.

[11:10] Mei Chen: That seems sensible. How about throttling? Can Redwood throttle downstream consumers gracefully?

[11:17] Priya Singh: We can apply per-customer rate-limits and also prioritize internal critical routes. For client throttling, we return 429 with a Retry-After; our SDKs handle backoff automatically if used.

[11:31] Alex Navarro: Competitor callout — we evaluated Megacloud AI earlier; they offered a cheaper failover but didn't have a private key option. NimbusServe had a more complex oncall integration. We're trying to pick a partner that can do the hybrid fit.

[11:47] Jordan Blake: Understood. We'll make sure to highlight BYOK and regional pool for EU in the proposal.

[11:55] Sam Ortiz: One more operational ask — can Redwood provide a weekly 'health snapshot' for the pilot that includes cache hit ratios, tail latency P95/P99, error budget burn and short-circuit counts?

[12:08] Luis Marten: Yes, the console already has dashboards and we can provide an export to your SIEM. We'll include a sample CSV and also a dashboard share link.

[12:20] Mei Chen: Export to our SIEM is mandatory — we ingest into Splunk and run custom alerts there.

[12:27] Ava Romero: We'll confirm the log schema.

[12:34] [Noise/background: someone typing; mild crosstalk]

[12:45] Jordan Blake: Alright let's walkthrough the timeline we proposed for the next two weeks: security pack by 24th, traffic/profile by 25th, pricing and commitment by 26th, staging benchmark week of 30th, and pilot kickoff the week after if all good.

[13:05] Alex Navarro: That timeline works but we need to ensure procurement reviews the pricing by 26th — if we don't have that, the pilot won't start.

[13:12] Jordan Blake: We'll prioritize it.

[13:20] Rina Patel: On procurement, any standard contract terms that are blockers? We need data processing addendum (DPA) and breach notification timelines.

[13:30] Jordan Blake: We have a standard DPA and commit to 72-hour breach notification for severity-one. We'll include DPA and an escrow clause for the pilot.

[13:42] Sam Ortiz: Back to the technicals — during the outage we noticed a memory spike in the inference worker that correlated with the long-sequence jobs; have you implemented a soft-limit that will reject very long sequences to protect the pool?

[13:58] Priya Singh: We do. There's a per-tenant maximum token limit and an optional queue for large jobs. For the pilot we recommend allowing long jobs but routing them to a separate gradual pool to avoid interfering with low-latency traffic.

[14:14] Mei Chen: So separate pool for L long sequences. Is there autoscale there?

[14:19] Priya Singh: Yes, separate autoscale group with different burst parameters. We'll size it conservatively for the pilot.

[14:28] Alex Navarro: Cost question — if we route 5% long sequences to the separate pool, what's rough incremental cost?

[14:34] Priya Singh: We'll model that with your exact sequence length distribution. Rough guess: 12-18% uplift depending on quantization and batch efficiency. We'll run a sim and include it in the pricing doc.

[14:47] [Longer pause while people read shared doc; Jordan prompts next topic]

[14:58] Jordan Blake: Anything else on the postmortem items we haven't closed? I know we had a doc item about a telemetry gap — who owns closing that?

[15:06] Mei Chen: That was the telemetry agent crash for 30 minutes; we need a fix and a followup test to ensure metrics don't drop during restarts.

[15:13] Priya Singh: Priya: we'll add a heartbeat to the agent and replay missed metrics into the pipeline; we'll test it in staging.

[15:22] Alex Navarro: Good. Also, the PM asked that we get an RCA note that is customer-friendly — no raw logs, just timeline and actions. Can Redwood produce that?

[15:31] Jordan Blake: Yes, we'll produce a customer-ready RCA and link to the raw artifacts behind a signed access for your security team.

[15:40] Rina Patel: And confirm: will the raw artifacts require a signed NDA or special access?

[15:47] Jordan Blake: We'll gate raw debug artifacts behind a short NDA and restrict to named engineers.

[15:54] [Short crosstalk; Alex affirms]

[16:02] Priya Singh: I want to call out one engineering caveat — the short-circuit summary can change model quality; we recommend adding a small human-in-the-loop step for high sensitivity calls where accuracy matters. For non-critical UX, auto short-circuiting is fine.

[16:22] Sam Ortiz: We'll flag high sensitivity endpoints in the request header so you can skip short-circuit.

[16:30] Priya Singh: Perfect.

[16:35] Jordan Blake: Let's document the acceptance criteria for pilot success — suggests: sustained 99th percentile latency below 1.2s for first wave, cache hit ratio > 85% for identified prefixes, and no unrecoverable outages in 2 weeks. Thoughts?

[16:52] Mei Chen: That feels reasonable. Maybe tweak P99 to 1.5s for the first week to be realistic.

[17:01] Alex Navarro: Agree to 1.5s first week, then ramp to 1.2s after tuning.

[17:09] Jordan Blake: Noted.

[17:12] [Participants make small jokes; lighter tone]

[17:25] Luis Marten: On the ops side, we'll schedule a 30-minute dry run with your oncall to walk through the runbook triggers before the benchmark. Suggested time: 2026-03-29.

[17:37] Mei Chen: That works. Put it on calendar.

[17:42] Jordan Blake: Final round — any blocking items that would stop the pilot?

[17:48] Rina Patel: The main blocker is DPA signoff and BYOK confirmation. If we get those done by 24th, we're green.

[17:56] Jordan Blake: We'll prioritize DPA + BYOK.

[18:03] Alex Navarro: One administrative question: will Redwood accept SOC2 evidence as-of last audit or do you need live attestations?

[18:12] Ava Romero: We can provide the latest SOC2 report and our compliance contact for any attestation questions. For some customers we also offer a SOC2+ supplemental letter.

[18:25] Jordan Blake: Good. 

[18:30] [Wrap-up begins]

[18:32] Jordan Blake: Recap — Redwood to share security pack (SOC2, ISO, KMS diagrams), Priya to send benchmark runbook and backoff defaults, Luis to share oncall rota, Horizon to share traffic/profile and PII prompt examples. We'll schedule the dry run 3/29 and tentatively run the staging benchmark week of 3/30. Pricing proposal to Alex by 3/26. Anything to add?

[18:58] Mei Chen: No, that's it. Thank you.

[19:02] Alex Navarro: Thanks — appreciate the concrete plan.

[19:06] Jordan Blake: Thanks everyone. We'll send notes and action items after this.

[19:12] [Meeting ends, approximate total duration 54 minutes — transcript contains occasional misrecognitions: 'firewall-agent' transcribed as 'fairwall' in one excerpt, product 'Redwood Dedicated' sometimes appears as 'Redwood Dedicate' in auto text)
