[15:47] Jordan Blake: We'll gate raw debug artifacts behind a short NDA and restrict to named engineers.

[15:54] [Short crosstalk; Alex affirms]

[16:02] Priya Singh: I want to call out one engineering caveat — the short-circuit summary can change model quality; we recommend adding a small human-in-the-loop step for high sensitivity calls where accuracy matters. For non-critical UX, auto short-circuiting is fine.

[16:22] Sam Ortiz: We'll flag high sensitivity endpoints in the request header so you can skip short-circuit.

[16:30] Priya Singh: Perfect.

[16:35] Jordan Blake: Let's document the acceptance criteria for pilot success — suggests: sustained 99th percentile latency below 1.2s for first wave, cache hit ratio > 85% for identified prefixes, and no unrecoverable outages in 2 weeks. Thoughts?

[16:52] Mei Chen: That feels reasonable. Maybe tweak P99 to 1.5s for the first week to be realistic.

[17:01] Alex Navarro: Agree to 1.5s first week, then ramp to 1.2s after tuning.

[17:09] Jordan Blake: Noted.

[17:12] [Participants make small jokes; lighter tone]

[17:25] Luis Marten: On the ops side, we'll schedule a 30-minute dry run with your oncall to walk through the runbook triggers before the benchmark. Suggested time: 2026-03-29.

[17:37] Mei Chen: That works. Put it on calendar.

[17:42] Jordan Blake: Final round — any blocking items that would stop the pilot?

[17:48] Rina Patel: The main blocker is DPA signoff and BYOK confirmation. If we get those done by 24th, we're green.

[17:56] Jordan Blake: We'll prioritize DPA + BYOK.

[18:03] Alex Navarro: One administrative question: will Redwood accept SOC2 evidence as-of last audit or do you need live attestations?

[18:12] Ava Romero: We can provide the latest SOC2 report and our compliance contact for any attestation questions. For some customers we also offer a SOC2+ supplemental letter.

[18:25] Jordan Blake: Good. 

[18:30] [Wrap-up begins]

[18:32] Jordan Blake: Recap — Redwood to share security pack (SOC2, ISO, KMS diagrams), Priya to send benchmark runbook and backoff defaults, Luis to share oncall rota, Horizon to share traffic/profile and PII prompt examples. We'll schedule the dry run 3/29 and tentatively run the staging benchmark week of 3/30. Pricing proposal to Alex by 3/26. Anything to add?

[18:58] Mei Chen: No, that's it. Thank you.

[19:02] Alex Navarro: Thanks — appreciate the concrete plan.

[19:06] Jordan Blake: Thanks everyone. We'll send notes and action items after this.

[19:12] [Meeting ends, approximate total duration 54 minutes — transcript contains occasional misrecognitions: 'firewall-agent' transcribed as 'fairwall' in one excerpt, product 'Redwood Dedicated' sometimes appears as 'Redwood Dedicate' in auto text)

title: Inference Capacity Staircase & Rollbacks Playbook
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-03-18
Start: 11:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Mateo Alvarez, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Jordan Blake, Elena Park, Noah Wright, Sana Mir, Greg Foster

Summary:
Quick internal workshop to align on a concrete 'capacity staircase' (phased ramps), explicit rollback criteria for model and capacity regressions, and a short playbook for dry-run/drill execution. Focus was on launch readiness for the next private deployment wave and a plan to avoid cost surprises while keeping SLOs.

Topics:
- Capacity staircase definition (levels, triggers)
- Canary and dry-run plan
- Rollback criteria: quality regressions vs infra regressions
- Observability needs and runbook edits
- Security/audit notes for private deployments

Action items (captured live): see metadata section above.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: alright, uh thanks everyone for squeezing this in, I know the calendars are tight. the goal is to leave with a concrete, like, staircase thresholds doc and the first dry-run scheduled.
[00:18] Elena: yep, from exec side want clear guardrails — if latency crosses 220ms p95 for more than 5min we need an automatic step down, or at least an alert that triggers rollback.
[00:30] Noah: I think 220 as a hard number might be tough on the 8k contexts, we should scope by model size. maybe per-model thresholds, and then a global SLO budget.
[00:45] Mateo: agreed. we have telemetry split by model+region already but we need a staircase with 3 levels: baseline, stress, and surge. baseline is normal autoscale, stress is reserved burst, surge is emergency cap (manual). each level has triggers: latency, error rate, token throughput.
[01:05] Priya: can we call them v1/v2/v3? the names matter less than the actions — for v2 the plan is increase batch window slightly and enable KV caching for prefixes, for v3 we should auto-fallback to a quantized variant or smaller model.
[01:25] Liam: caution on auto-fallback — there's user-affecting quality regressions. we need rollback criteria both for infra failures and for quality regressions. that means an observable that's not just latency: e.g. eval prompt set failure rate or hallucination proxy.
[01:40] Sana: on the security side, any autoscaling or rollbacks in private deployments must be logged with the instance id and KMS key usage. audit trail is mandatory for some customers. also, VPC egress events need to be captured.
[02:00] Jordan: ok capturing, also note that cost must be part of the staircase. if burst > X token-minutes we trigger a cost review window and possibly step-down. otherwise finance will freak.
[02:15] Greg: for dry-run we should pick a low-traffic weekend slot and run a canary that ramps traffic using replay plus synthetic tails to exercise long-context paths. the sim should include KV cache warmup so we don't get skewed results.
[02:35] Ava: agreed — Mateo you own the sim plan?
[02:40] Mateo: yep, i'll do a 4-hour sim with three ramps, I'll include latency, p95/p99, tail lat, token throughput, and error rate. spreadsheet will have columns for thresholds and pass/fail criteria.
[02:55] Elena: what's the rollback mechanism we're comfortable with? full traffic cut or model variant fallback?
[03:05] Priya: prefer staged: first fallback model variant (quantized), if QA metric drops below threshold then step to capacity step-down (move traffic to smaller region or dedicated pool), last resort cut traffic. incorporate vote from oncall.
[03:25] Liam: that needs clear criteria: e.g., quality proxy > 2% fail on eval set OR latency p99 > 500ms for 10 minutes leads to vote. we should automate the vote if it's infra-exit (OOMs, GPU errors).
[03:45] Noah: on capacity accounting: the staircase should expose reserved burst buckets per customer so we can bill and also stop accidental overrun. we should include a strap on the console that shows current staircase stage and remaining burst minutes.
[04:05] Priya: we talked about KV cache being mis-heard in prior calls as 'KV cash' — note: caching warms cost but helps latency; we'll measure cost per token with cache on/off.
[04:20] Jordan: heh yep, recorded.
[04:25] Ava: operational playbook edits — Liam, can you propose amendments to runbook for staged rollback, including who has the unilateral authority to move to v2/v3?
[04:40] Liam: i'll propose that oncall can move to v2 (stress) but v3 (surge) requires approval from capacity lead + product (Noah + Elena). final traffic cut needs VP ok.
[04:55] Greg: and we'll add a checklist for the dry-run: prechecks (metrics sane), smoke test endpoints, KMS audit hook enabled, and roll-forward plan.
[05:10] Ava: timeline check: draft stairs doc by Monday (Ava), sim by Wednesday (Mateo), rollback hook prototype by next Friday (Priya), dry-run targeted end of next week (Greg).
[05:25] Elena: quick question — how do we handle private deployments where customers want full manual control? do we still enforce staircases?
[05:35] Sana: for privacy customers we ship the scaffolding; by default it's opt-in for automatic steps, but we still require audit logs and a manual approval path. the defaults will be conservative.
[05:50] Mateo: technical gotcha — when we do model variant fallback we need to ensure KV cache format compatibility or else cache miss storms will happen. we should add a cache compatibility check in the rollback hook.
[06:05] Priya: yep, that's on my prototype scope. i'll include a quick latency-safe migration that invalidates prefixes older than N seconds to avoid cold-miss avalanche.
[06:25] Jordan: can we add a short 'blast radius' diagram to the staircase doc that maps components affected (frontend, router, runtime, kv cache, billing)?
[06:40] Ava: good idea, Jordan — that'll help execs understand risk.
[06:50] (crosstalk)
[07:00] Ava: ok smaller notes: include specific SLO windows, escalation path, and the exact telemetry dashboards we'll need. Mateo, please list dashboard IDs so we can pair them to thresholds.
[07:18] Mateo: will do — i'm also going to include an 'emergency capacity token' concept that is separate from reserved burst and requires manual approval.
[07:30] Noah: one more ask — instrument the console to show last rollback reason: quality/infrastructure/cost. that's critical for postmortems.
[07:45] Priya: agreed. building that into trace logs is straightforward but we need UX work to surface it. I'll coordinate with Jordan for the wire.
[07:58] Ava: wrapping — any final blockers?
[08:05] Elena: none here. just want the doc to be crisp.
[08:10] Liam: need to validate SSO and audit hooks for private customers before dry-run. Sana, can you sign off once done?
[08:20] Sana: yes i'll sign off once I see KMS audit logs and VPC egress hooks in place.
[08:30] Ava: cool. recap next steps in chat and i'll own the doc.
[08:35] (meeting ends)

Post-call quick notes (auto):
- Some speech mis-recognition around 'KV cache' and 'canary sim' replaced with 'KV cash' and 'candy sim' in earlier auto transcript; corrected above.
- A few timestamps drift because of overlapping talkers; manual cleanup recommended if used for legal/audit purposes.

Recap (final):
- Deliverables: staircase thresholds doc (Ava), canary sim results spreadsheet (Mateo), rollback hook prototype (Priya), dry-run schedule and checklist (Greg), runbook edits (Liam).
- Dry-run target: weekend slot week of 2026-03-29.
- Acceptance criteria: per-model thresholds pass in sim, KV cache compatibility verified, security audit hooks present, and rollback behavior tested with postmortem template filled.

References shared during call:
- \"staircase-spec-v0\" doc link (to be attached in calendar)
- canary-spreadsheet template (sheet link)
- runbook PR template (repo link)

End of transcript.

title: Launch gating and incident ownership sprint sync
preview: Planning sync to finalize launch gating criteria for next beta, review last weekend incident postmortem, assign incident ownership changes, and finalize preflight checklist for model onboarding.
text: Planning sync to finalize launch gating criteria for next beta, review last weekend incident postmortem, assign incident ownership changes, and finalize preflight checklist for model onboarding.

title: Renewal check-in: usage + spend forecast (Fabrikam)
preview: Fabrikam walked through their March–May spend forecast for Redwood Hosted API and highlighted two pain points: (1) day-to-day volatility (model mix changes, retries/fallback, and caching behavior) and (2) internal budgeting requirements that prefer commit + predictable overages. Redwood shared recent metering hardening work (idempotency/dedupe on retries, billed vs raw tokens, cache-hit attribution fields, and a short shadow-billing compare period) and clarified what will and will not change in invoicing. The group discussed packaging options: staying pure PAYG with better alerting and daily exports, moving to an annual commit with discounted overage, or adding Dedicated capacity for the steady baseline and bursting to Hosted for peaks. Redwood agreed to send a proposal with two options (Hosted commit-only vs hybrid Dedicated+Hosted), plus updated guidance explaining billed tokens, cache hits, and retry accounting for procurement and finance review.

title: Security review: data retention + billing/metering logs (Contoso Financial)
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Security review: data retention + billing/metering logs (Contoso Financial)
Date: 2026-02-19
Start time: 4:00 PM PT
Duration: 47 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees:
- Jordan Blake (Redwood Inference)
- Mei Lin (Redwood Inference)
- Michael Grant (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Contoso Financial)
- Mark Ellison (Contoso Financial)
- Hannah Cho (Contoso Financial)
- Diego Ramirez (Contoso Financial)

Summary (auto-generated):
Contoso asked how Redwood retains metering/billing data, who can access it, and what audit trail exists for access to billing and usage logs. Redwood walked through high-level retention tiers (request logs vs usage events vs billing ledger), access control (RBAC + least privilege, restricted Finance tables), and audit logging. Contoso also asked about export availability for audits and dispute windows. Redwood pointed to policy documentation and agreed to follow up with a security packet and specifics for their deployment mode.

Topics:
- Data retention: request logs, usage events, billing ledger, customer exports
- Access controls and audit trails for billing/metering datasets
- Encryption and key management options
- Evidence for invoice disputes and audit readiness

Questions (auto-generated):
- How long are billing and metering datasets retained by default?
- Who inside Redwood can view customer-level billing ledger data?
- Are queries to billing tables audited and exportable?
- Can Contoso request a shorter/longer retention period?

Action items:
- Redwood: Send retention/access-control policy pointers and security packet.
- Contoso: Confirm retention requirements and preferred evidence format.
- Redwood/Contoso: Schedule audit log + RBAC deep dive.


Transcript

[00:00] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Alright, thanks everyone for making time. This is Jordan from Redwood. We’ve got Mei here who runs a lot of the reconciliation datasets and access patterns, and Michael from Legal to help with the, you know, usage definitions and dispute language if it comes up. Priya, Mark, Hannah, Diego — thanks for joining.

[00:18] Priya Desai (Contoso): Thanks. Yeah, we’re basically trying to close out security and compliance questions. Our biggest open items are around data retention and access. Specifically billing logs, metering data, audit trails. Because… we’re a financial org so we have retention requirements.

[00:35] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Makes sense. Maybe we start with what you mean by “billing logs” on your side. Is that invoice artifacts, or the event-level usage that leads to the invoice, or request logs?

[00:46] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): All of it, but the sensitive part is the event-level usage. Like tokens, request metadata, and any identifiers. We need to know what you store, for how long, and who can access it.

[01:01] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yep. We generally separate it into three layers:
1) request/serving logs (high-volume, operational)
2) usage events (the metering stream; less data than raw logs)
3) billing ledger (the finance-grade aggregation used to generate invoices)
And retention + access controls are different for each.

[01:20] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Can you give numbers? Like 30 days, 90 days, 7 years? Because our auditors will ask.

[01:28] Mei Lin (Redwood): I can give you the defaults at a high level, and we’ll follow up with the exact policy language for the package you’re buying. Typically:
- Operational request logs are retained shorter (think weeks), mostly for debugging and incident response.
- Usage events and reconciliation exports are retained longer (think months-plus) because customers want trend reporting, and Finance needs continuity across billing cycles.
- Billing ledger and invoice artifacts are retained longest (multi-year) because of accounting and audit requirements.
I don’t want to over-commit to specific day counts verbally, but we can provide the written retention schedule.

[02:02] Priya Desai (Contoso): Okay, but we need to know if anything is retained indefinitely. Is there a path to purge?

[02:09] Jordan Blake (Redwood): For hosted, we have defined retention windows and we can support deletion requests for certain categories, subject to legal/accounting obligations. For example, invoice records can’t usually be deleted immediately due to statutory retention. Usage events tied to billing similarly have constraints.

[02:26] Michael Grant (Redwood): And just to clarify — we’ll usually distinguish between “customer content” and “billing evidence.” Billing evidence like invoices, ledger entries, and audit logs have retention obligations. We can talk through what’s configurable in an order form, but we can’t promise to delete records that we’re required to retain.

[02:47] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Right. And who can access those records inside Redwood? We’ve had incidents with vendors where a broad engineering group could see billing dashboards.

[02:57] Mei Lin (Redwood): Good question. Access is role-based, least privilege. The billing ledger tables are restricted; it’s not like every engineer can query them. Generally:
- Support has limited tooling access and usually sees account-level summaries, not raw event streams.
- Engineering sees operational metrics and anonymized aggregates for reliability, unless there’s an approved escalation.
- Finance/RevRec has access to the ledger and reconciliation exports, again with auditing.
And access requests go through an internal approval + ticketing workflow.

[03:28] Mark Ellison (Contoso): When you say “auditing,” do you mean you log when someone looks at our usage data?

[03:34] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes. Access to restricted billing/metering datasets is audit logged. We also audit changes to the metering definitions and pipelines. For example, if we change token counting semantics, there’s change control and evidence retained.

[03:53] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Is that something you can export to us? Like, we want to see a report of who accessed Contoso billing data.

[04:02] Jordan Blake (Redwood): For hosted products, we typically provide an attestation and can provide audit log excerpts under an agreed process. For Private deployments (VPC), there are more options because you can host certain logs in your environment. But I want to be careful: we don’t give customers direct access to internal employee audit logs in an unfiltered way, but we can provide evidence during audits or incident investigations.

[04:28] Priya Desai (Contoso): That’s… a little vague. We need something concrete for our third-party risk assessment.

[04:35] Michael Grant (Redwood): Totally fair. What we can do is share:
- our standard security/compliance packet (SOC 2 report, controls summary)
- the written data retention and access control policy for metering/billing datasets
- and a description of the audit logging controls (what’s logged, retention of those logs, and how we respond to audit requests)
That’s typically what procurement and security want.

[05:03] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Okay. Next: what exactly is in “usage events”? Are prompts included? Are customer IDs included? IP addresses?

[05:13] Mei Lin (Redwood): Usage events are metering-focused. They include the dimensions needed to price correctly and to reconcile: account identifier, timestamp, model, region, meter fields like prompt tokens, completion tokens, billed tokens, request counts, cache hit attribution. They don’t store full prompt content as part of the metering record.

[05:37] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): “Cache hit attribution” — explain that.

[05:41] Mei Lin (Redwood): If caching is enabled, some tokens may be served from cache. We track whether a request benefited from cache so billing and analytics are consistent. That’s actually a reason we keep the metering stream separate from raw logs: it’s structured and scoped.

[05:59] Priya Desai (Contoso): And region — we care about residency. Do you store region in usage data and can we restrict processing to a region?

[06:07] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, region is a first-class dimension in usage and billing. For residency: for hosted, you pick regions where you deploy. For strict constraints, customers often choose Private (VPC) where network boundaries are customer-managed.

[06:26] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Is your billing system centralized in the US? Like do the events flow to a central warehouse?

[06:33] Mei Lin (Redwood): The reconciliation and billing ledger are centralized for finance operations, but we keep the data partitioned by account and apply access controls. Also, the customer-facing usage exports are derived from the same source-of-truth tables used for invoicing, to avoid discrepancies.

[06:56] Priya Desai (Contoso): You said “customer-facing usage exports.” Are those daily? Can we get them in our time zone?

[07:02] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, we support daily usage exports and API access. Time zone handling is something we can align on; we generally report in UTC in the API but can provide guidance for alignment. If you need a specific time zone boundary for your internal cost allocation, we can discuss.

[07:22] Hannah Cho (Contoso): We’ve had issues where tokens on an invoice don’t match what engineering sees in a usage dashboard. How do you prevent that?

[07:30] Mei Lin (Redwood): Two things:
1) We reconcile the ledger against usage events, and we monitor drift. If aggregated events don’t match ledger numbers within thresholds, we alert.
2) We use idempotency and dedupe keys to avoid double-counting from retries/backfills.
And we have a “shadow billing compare” approach when metering logic changes — compute old and new meters in parallel before it impacts invoices.

[08:05] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Retries is big. If your server retries a request, does that double-charge?

[08:11] Mei Lin (Redwood): The intent is no. The metering logic is designed so the customer is billed once for a logical request, even if there are internal retries or fallback routing. There are edge cases we test specifically — streaming disconnects, tool call paths, server-side retries. If we ever identify a discrepancy, we have a documented remediation and credit process.

[08:35] Priya Desai (Contoso): “Fallback routing” means you might send a request to a different model or region? That sounds scary from a compliance standpoint.

[08:43] Jordan Blake (Redwood): For regulated customers we can disable fallback policies or restrict them. In dedicated or private modes, the policy is much tighter. For hosted, there are reliability features, but they’re configurable. We’ll capture your constraints.

[09:05] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): We need to know: if a request goes to another region, would the usage event show that region? Because we’d need evidence.

[09:12] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes, the region dimension is part of the usage event and ledger aggregation. If there’s a policy that pins traffic to a region, then it should never show otherwise; and if it did, it would be visible in the usage export.

[09:29] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Okay. Next — who can see our invoices internally at Redwood? Like can an engineer open up Contoso invoices?

[09:38] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Typically no, not by default. Finance and the account team may see invoice PDFs and summary spend. Engineering access to invoice artifacts is not a normal workflow.

[09:50] Michael Grant (Redwood): Also, contractually, we treat that as confidential customer information. Access is limited to those who need it for support, billing, and legal compliance.

[10:03] Priya Desai (Contoso): What about support? If we open a ticket, do they get broad access to our request logs?

[10:10] Mei Lin (Redwood): Support usually works from customer-provided request IDs and aggregated telemetry. If deeper investigation is required, we have an escalation process. The goal is to avoid “browse all logs” access.

[10:27] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Do you have SSO and RBAC on your console? Because if our users look at usage dashboards, we want to control that.

[10:34] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes — SSO/SAML for enterprise plans, RBAC for roles in the console, and audit logs for user actions. We can share the exact feature set in the security packet.

[10:50] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Can you define “audit logs” there? Like login events? API key usage? Viewing billing data?

[10:56] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Console audit logs include auth events and key administrative actions. For API keys, we track key creation/rotation, and usage can be monitored via usage metrics. For the billing data viewing, we can discuss what is exposed via the console vs what remains internal.

[11:18] Priya Desai (Contoso): Encryption — at rest and in transit. Do you support customer-managed keys?

[11:24] Jordan Blake (Redwood): In transit TLS, at rest encryption is standard. For customer-managed keys, that’s typically available in Private/VPC deployments; for hosted it depends on the tier and the region/provider. We’ll get you the official answer and options.

[11:44] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Switching gears slightly — retention again. We need billing evidence for 7 years. Is that possible? Or would we need to export and store ourselves?

[11:55] Michael Grant (Redwood): Most customers with long retention requirements do export and store in their own systems. Redwood retains what’s required for our accounting and audit obligations, but if you need guaranteed 7-year availability of detailed usage exports in a particular format, we’d specify that in the order form as a deliverable.

[12:19] Mei Lin (Redwood): And practically, we can support periodic exports (monthly, daily) that you store in your data lake. That’s a common pattern for finance teams.

[12:33] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Do those exports include enough to prove the invoice? Like “billed_tokens” versus “raw tokens”? We’ve been burned where the meter changes and we can’t reproduce the number.

[12:44] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes, we’re moving toward always including both “raw” and “billed” fields where relevant, plus dimensions like cache hit and retry attribution. And when we change meters, we keep versioning and run shadow comparisons. So you can see “pre-change vs post-change” if necessary.

[13:07] Priya Desai (Contoso): I’m going to ask directly: if your meter was wrong last month, do you retroactively change invoices?

[13:14] Michael Grant (Redwood): Generally, we avoid retroactive changes unless there’s a clear error and both parties agree, and there’s a documented process. The contract language typically includes a dispute window and a credit/refund mechanism depending on circumstances. We can share our standard terms.

[13:38] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Dispute window — what is it? 30 days?

[13:42] Michael Grant (Redwood): It varies by agreement; we can provide the default and we can negotiate if you have a policy requirement.

[13:52] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Back to who can access — do you have a list of roles? Like “Finance can see X,” “SRE can see Y.”

[14:00] Mei Lin (Redwood): We do, but it’s internal. What we can share is the access control model: least privilege, approvals for elevated access, and audited access to restricted datasets. We can also share a high-level matrix without naming internal individuals.

[14:18] Priya Desai (Contoso): That’s acceptable as long as it’s in writing.

[14:22] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yep, we’ll send the written policy pointer. There’s a specific doc that covers metering data retention and access controls.

[14:33] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Also, do you ever use customer usage data to train models?

[14:38] Jordan Blake (Redwood): No — Redwood is an inference platform. We do not train on customer prompts by default. We can include that in the data processing terms. For certain opt-in eval programs, it would be explicit.

[14:56] Priya Desai (Contoso): Okay.

[15:00] (crosstalk)

[15:02] Hannah Cho (Contoso): For procurement: we need subprocessor list, breach notification timing, and retention schedule.

[15:10] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Understood. We’ll package that up.

[15:14] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Another technical one: for “usage events,” do you store user IDs that we send? Like end-user identifiers in the request metadata.

[15:23] Mei Lin (Redwood): If you pass metadata, best practice is don’t pass PII. We can support customer-provided tags for cost allocation (like project_id), but we recommend you use pseudonymous IDs. Metering requires account-level identifiers, not end-user PII.

[15:43] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Do you enforce that? Or it’s just advice.

[15:47] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Mostly guidance. We can help configure filters or linting in SDK examples, but we can’t prevent someone from sending arbitrary text in prompts. That’s why private deployment is preferred for highly regulated content. But for metering records, we keep the schema limited.

[16:10] Priya Desai (Contoso): If we go with hosted initially, can we later move to VPC/Private without changing usage definitions?

[16:18] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, the goal is consistent metering definitions across hosted/dedicated/private. There can be differences in what logs are available to you depending on deployment, but the meters and invoice semantics stay aligned.

[16:36] Mark Ellison (Contoso): On audit trails again: you said internal access is audited. Is that like Snowflake query logs? Sorry, I’m guessing.

[16:45] Mei Lin (Redwood): That’s the right category. Access to the data warehouse and restricted tables is logged, and we also track access requests. We can provide a controls description rather than the raw warehouse config.

[17:04] Priya Desai (Contoso): I want to make sure: if there’s a billing dispute, can you produce the data to show how you computed billed tokens?

[17:11] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes. We can show the meter spec, the relevant usage exports, and the reconciliation to the invoice. We also maintain change history for meter logic so you know which version applied to a billing period.

[17:31] Hannah Cho (Contoso): That’s important.

[17:34] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Priya, what’s the format your auditors want? A PDF attestation? A CSV export? Both?

[17:41] Priya Desai (Contoso): Typically: (1) policy documents, (2) SOC 2, (3) if there’s an incident, we need audit evidence. For billing, we want the ability to store monthly exports ourselves. CSV is fine.

[17:56] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Great.

[18:00] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Can you confirm: do you have data-quality monitoring for those usage exports? Like completeness, late arriving events.

[18:09] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes. We monitor freshness and completeness, and we have drift checks between usage events and the billing ledger. Late events are tracked; there’s a freeze window at month-end so Finance can close reliably. Late events after the freeze get handled via adjustments/credits depending on policy.

[18:33] Priya Desai (Contoso): Freeze window — explain.

[18:36] Mei Lin (Redwood): Basically, to avoid invoices changing endlessly due to late telemetry, we define a cutoff for a billing period. After that, events can still arrive but they’re handled in a controlled way. That’s part of audit readiness.

[18:56] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): If there’s a cutoff, does that mean your usage API might show something different than the invoice?

[19:03] Mei Lin (Redwood): We aim to keep them consistent by applying the same rules to customer exports, and by labeling data as “finalized” vs “provisional” where appropriate. We can clarify the semantics.

[19:21] Mark Ellison (Contoso): This is fine. We just need it documented.

[19:25] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yep.

[19:27] (brief pause)

[19:29] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Also: do you have a retention policy for audit logs themselves? Like how long do you keep “who accessed what” logs.

[19:37] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, there is retention for audit logs. We’ll provide the policy schedule.

[19:46] Priya Desai (Contoso): Any chance we can get that today? We’re trying to make a go/no-go next week.

[19:53] Jordan Blake (Redwood): We can send the packet today EOD, and if we need to fill in specific retention numbers, we’ll do that in writing tomorrow after confirming with our compliance team.

[20:06] Michael Grant (Redwood): And for contract language, we can propose wording that covers usage definitions (tokens, retries, caching) and dispute/credit process. That should help procurement.

[20:23] Mark Ellison (Contoso): One more: Do you have an incident history for billing correctness? Like have you had billing mistakes.

[20:31] Jordan Blake (Redwood): We’ve had typical early-stage issues like any platform — like edge cases around retries or streaming metering — but we’ve invested heavily in hardening. We run automated meter sanity checks, and we do shadow comparisons before changes roll out. If an issue occurs, we follow an incident process and remediate with credits when appropriate.

[20:55] Priya Desai (Contoso): Good answer. We’ll still want the controls in writing.

[21:00] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Understood.

[21:03] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): From engineering: do you allow us to tag requests with our own cost center and have it show in usage exports?

[21:11] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, within limits. We can support metadata tags and project identifiers. We’ll make sure it doesn’t interfere with the meter.

[21:22] Mei Lin (Redwood): And we can help define a safe tagging scheme so you avoid PII.

[21:31] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Great.

[21:34] Priya Desai (Contoso): Okay I think that covers it. Recap the next steps?

[21:39] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Sure. 1) We’ll email the security packet plus the retention/access controls document pointers today. 2) You’ll confirm your retention requirement — especially the 7-year evidence need — and whether hosted is acceptable short term. 3) We’ll set a follow-up for audit log export format and RBAC mapping. And Michael will send suggested contract language for usage definitions and dispute window.

[22:06] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Works.

[22:08] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Yep.

[22:10] Priya Desai (Contoso): Thanks.

[22:12] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Thanks everyone.

[End of transcript]

Note: Transcription may contain errors. Speaker attribution is automated.
text:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Security review: data retention + billing/metering logs (Contoso Financial)
Date: 2026-02-19
Start time: 4:00 PM PT
Duration: 47 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees:
- Jordan Blake (Redwood Inference)
- Mei Lin (Redwood Inference)
- Michael Grant (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Contoso Financial)
- Mark Ellison (Contoso Financial)
- Hannah Cho (Contoso Financial)
- Diego Ramirez (Contoso Financial)

Summary (auto-generated):
Contoso asked how Redwood retains metering/billing data, who can access it, and what audit trail exists for access to billing and usage logs. Redwood walked through high-level retention tiers (request logs vs usage events vs billing ledger), access control (RBAC + least privilege, restricted Finance tables), and audit logging. Contoso also asked about export availability for audits and dispute windows. Redwood pointed to policy documentation and agreed to follow up with a security packet and specifics for their deployment mode.

Topics:
- Data retention: request logs, usage events, billing ledger, customer exports
- Access controls and audit trails for billing/metering datasets
- Encryption and key management options
- Evidence for invoice disputes and audit readiness

Questions (auto-generated):
- How long are billing and metering datasets retained by default?
- Who inside Redwood can view customer-level billing ledger data?
- Are queries to billing tables audited and exportable?
- Can Contoso request a shorter/longer retention period?

Action items:
- Redwood: Send retention/access-control policy pointers and security packet.
- Contoso: Confirm retention requirements and preferred evidence format.
- Redwood/Contoso: Schedule audit log + RBAC deep dive.


Transcript

[00:00] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Alright, thanks everyone for making time. This is Jordan from Redwood. We’ve got Mei here who runs a lot of the reconciliation datasets and access patterns, and Michael from Legal to help with the, you know, usage definitions and dispute language if it comes up. Priya, Mark, Hannah, Diego — thanks for joining.

[00:18] Priya Desai (Contoso): Thanks. Yeah, we’re basically trying to close out security and compliance questions. Our biggest open items are around data retention and access. Specifically billing logs, metering data, audit trails. Because… we’re a financial org so we have retention requirements.

[00:35] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Makes sense. Maybe we start with what you mean by “billing logs” on your side. Is that invoice artifacts, or the event-level usage that leads to the invoice, or request logs?

[00:46] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): All of it, but the sensitive part is the event-level usage. Like tokens, request metadata, and any identifiers. We need to know what you store, for how long, and who can access it.

[01:01] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yep. We generally separate it into three layers:
1) request/serving logs (high-volume, operational)
2) usage events (the metering stream; less data than raw logs)
3) billing ledger (the finance-grade aggregation used to generate invoices)
And retention + access controls are different for each.

[01:20] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Can you give numbers? Like 30 days, 90 days, 7 years? Because our auditors will ask.

[01:28] Mei Lin (Redwood): I can give you the defaults at a high level, and we’ll follow up with the exact policy language for the package you’re buying. Typically:
- Operational request logs are retained shorter (think weeks), mostly for debugging and incident response.
- Usage events and reconciliation exports are retained longer (think months-plus) because customers want trend reporting, and Finance needs continuity across billing cycles.
- Billing ledger and invoice artifacts are retained longest (multi-year) because of accounting and audit requirements.
I don’t want to over-commit to specific day counts verbally, but we can provide the written retention schedule.

[02:02] Priya Desai (Contoso): Okay, but we need to know if anything is retained indefinitely. Is there a path to purge?

[02:09] Jordan Blake (Redwood): For hosted, we have defined retention windows and we can support deletion requests for certain categories, subject to legal/accounting obligations. For example, invoice records can’t usually be deleted immediately due to statutory retention. Usage events tied to billing similarly have constraints.

[02:26] Michael Grant (Redwood): And just to clarify — we’ll usually distinguish between “customer content” and “billing evidence.” Billing evidence like invoices, ledger entries, and audit logs have retention obligations. We can talk through what’s configurable in an order form, but we can’t promise to delete records that we’re required to retain.

[02:47] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Right. And who can access those records inside Redwood? We’ve had incidents with vendors where a broad engineering group could see billing dashboards.

[02:57] Mei Lin (Redwood): Good question. Access is role-based, least privilege. The billing ledger tables are restricted; it’s not like every engineer can query them. Generally:
- Support has limited tooling access and usually sees account-level summaries, not raw event streams.
- Engineering sees operational metrics and anonymized aggregates for reliability, unless there’s an approved escalation.
- Finance/RevRec has access to the ledger and reconciliation exports, again with auditing.
And access requests go through an internal approval + ticketing workflow.

[03:28] Mark Ellison (Contoso): When you say “auditing,” do you mean you log when someone looks at our usage data?

[03:34] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes. Access to restricted billing/metering datasets is audit logged. We also audit changes to the metering definitions and pipelines. For example, if we change token counting semantics, there’s change control and evidence retained.

[03:53] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Is that something you can export to us? Like, we want to see a report of who accessed Contoso billing data.

[04:02] Jordan Blake (Redwood): For hosted products, we typically provide an attestation and can provide audit log excerpts under an agreed process. For Private deployments (VPC), there are more options because you can host certain logs in your environment. But I want to be careful: we don’t give customers direct access to internal employee audit logs in an unfiltered way, but we can provide evidence during audits or incident investigations.

[04:28] Priya Desai (Contoso): That’s… a little vague. We need something concrete for our third-party risk assessment.

[04:35] Michael Grant (Redwood): Totally fair. What we can do is share:
- our standard security/compliance packet (SOC 2 report, controls summary)
- the written data retention and access control policy for metering/billing datasets
- and a description of the audit logging controls (what’s logged, retention of those logs, and how we respond to audit requests)
That’s typically what procurement and security want.

[05:03] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Okay. Next: what exactly is in “usage events”? Are prompts included? Are customer IDs included? IP addresses?

[05:13] Mei Lin (Redwood): Usage events are metering-focused. They include the dimensions needed to price correctly and to reconcile: account identifier, timestamp, model, region, meter fields like prompt tokens, completion tokens, billed tokens, request counts, cache hit attribution. They don’t store full prompt content as part of the metering record.

[05:37] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): “Cache hit attribution” — explain that.

[05:41] Mei Lin (Redwood): If caching is enabled, some tokens may be served from cache. We track whether a request benefited from cache so billing and analytics are consistent. That’s actually a reason we keep the metering stream separate from raw logs: it’s structured and scoped.

[05:59] Priya Desai (Contoso): And region — we care about residency. Do you store region in usage data and can we restrict processing to a region?

[06:07] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, region is a first-class dimension in usage and billing. For residency: for hosted, you pick regions where you deploy. For strict constraints, customers often choose Private (VPC) where network boundaries are customer-managed.

[06:26] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Is your billing system centralized in the US? Like do the events flow to a central warehouse?

[06:33] Mei Lin (Redwood): The reconciliation and billing ledger are centralized for finance operations, but we keep the data partitioned by account and apply access controls. Also, the customer-facing usage exports are derived from the same source-of-truth tables used for invoicing, to avoid discrepancies.

[06:56] Priya Desai (Contoso): You said “customer-facing usage exports.” Are those daily? Can we get them in our time zone?

[07:02] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, we support daily usage exports and API access. Time zone handling is something we can align on; we generally report in UTC in the API but can provide guidance for alignment. If you need a specific time zone boundary for your internal cost allocation, we can discuss.

[07:22] Hannah Cho (Contoso): We’ve had issues where tokens on an invoice don’t match what engineering sees in a usage dashboard. How do you prevent that?

[07:30] Mei Lin (Redwood): Two things:
1) We reconcile the ledger against usage events, and we monitor drift. If aggregated events don’t match ledger numbers within thresholds, we alert.
2) We use idempotency and dedupe keys to avoid double-counting from retries/backfills.
And we have a “shadow billing compare” approach when metering logic changes — compute old and new meters in parallel before it impacts invoices.

[08:05] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Retries is big. If your server retries a request, does that double-charge?

[08:11] Mei Lin (Redwood): The intent is no. The metering logic is designed so the customer is billed once for a logical request, even if there are internal retries or fallback routing. There are edge cases we test specifically — streaming disconnects, tool call paths, server-side retries. If we ever identify a discrepancy, we have a documented remediation and credit process.

[08:35] Priya Desai (Contoso): “Fallback routing” means you might send a request to a different model or region? That sounds scary from a compliance standpoint.

[08:43] Jordan Blake (Redwood): For regulated customers we can disable fallback policies or restrict them. In dedicated or private modes, the policy is much tighter. For hosted, there are reliability features, but they’re configurable. We’ll capture your constraints.

[09:05] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): We need to know: if a request goes to another region, would the usage event show that region? Because we’d need evidence.

[09:12] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes, the region dimension is part of the usage event and ledger aggregation. If there’s a policy that pins traffic to a region, then it should never show otherwise; and if it did, it would be visible in the usage export.

[09:29] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Okay. Next — who can see our invoices internally at Redwood? Like can an engineer open up Contoso invoices?

[09:38] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Typically no, not by default. Finance and the account team may see invoice PDFs and summary spend. Engineering access to invoice artifacts is not a normal workflow.

[09:50] Michael Grant (Redwood): Also, contractually, we treat that as confidential customer information. Access is limited to those who need it for support, billing, and legal compliance.

[10:03] Priya Desai (Contoso): What about support? If we open a ticket, do they get broad access to our request logs?

[10:10] Mei Lin (Redwood): Support usually works from customer-provided request IDs and aggregated telemetry. If deeper investigation is required, we have an escalation process. The goal is to avoid “browse all logs” access.

[10:27] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Do you have SSO and RBAC on your console? Because if our users look at usage dashboards, we want to control that.

[10:34] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes — SSO/SAML for enterprise plans, RBAC for roles in the console, and audit logs for user actions. We can share the exact feature set in the security packet.

[10:50] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Can you define “audit logs” there? Like login events? API key usage? Viewing billing data?

[10:56] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Console audit logs include auth events and key administrative actions. For API keys, we track key creation/rotation, and usage can be monitored via usage metrics. For the billing data viewing, we can discuss what is exposed via the console vs what remains internal.

[11:18] Priya Desai (Contoso): Encryption — at rest and in transit. Do you support customer-managed keys?

[11:24] Jordan Blake (Redwood): In transit TLS, at rest encryption is standard. For customer-managed keys, that’s typically available in Private/VPC deployments; for hosted it depends on the tier and the region/provider. We’ll get you the official answer and options.

[11:44] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Switching gears slightly — retention again. We need billing evidence for 7 years. Is that possible? Or would we need to export and store ourselves?

[11:55] Michael Grant (Redwood): Most customers with long retention requirements do export and store in their own systems. Redwood retains what’s required for our accounting and audit obligations, but if you need guaranteed 7-year availability of detailed usage exports in a particular format, we’d specify that in the order form as a deliverable.

[12:19] Mei Lin (Redwood): And practically, we can support periodic exports (monthly, daily) that you store in your data lake. That’s a common pattern for finance teams.

[12:33] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Do those exports include enough to prove the invoice? Like “billed_tokens” versus “raw tokens”? We’ve been burned where the meter changes and we can’t reproduce the number.

[12:44] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes, we’re moving toward always including both “raw” and “billed” fields where relevant, plus dimensions like cache hit and retry attribution. And when we change meters, we keep versioning and run shadow comparisons. So you can see “pre-change vs post-change” if necessary.

[13:07] Priya Desai (Contoso): I’m going to ask directly: if your meter was wrong last month, do you retroactively change invoices?

[13:14] Michael Grant (Redwood): Generally, we avoid retroactive changes unless there’s a clear error and both parties agree, and there’s a documented process. The contract language typically includes a dispute window and a credit/refund mechanism depending on circumstances. We can share our standard terms.

[13:38] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Dispute window — what is it? 30 days?

[13:42] Michael Grant (Redwood): It varies by agreement; we can provide the default and we can negotiate if you have a policy requirement.

[13:52] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Back to who can access — do you have a list of roles? Like “Finance can see X,” “SRE can see Y.”

[14:00] Mei Lin (Redwood): We do, but it’s internal. What we can share is the access control model: least privilege, approvals for elevated access, and audited access to restricted datasets. We can also share a high-level matrix without naming internal individuals.

[14:18] Priya Desai (Contoso): That’s acceptable as long as it’s in writing.

[14:22] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yep, we’ll send the written policy pointer. There’s a specific doc that covers metering data retention and access controls.

[14:33] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Also, do you ever use customer usage data to train models?

[14:38] Jordan Blake (Redwood): No — Redwood is an inference platform. We do not train on customer prompts by default. We can include that in the data processing terms. For certain opt-in eval programs, it would be explicit.

[14:56] Priya Desai (Contoso): Okay.

[15:00] (crosstalk)

[15:02] Hannah Cho (Contoso): For procurement: we need subprocessor list, breach notification timing, and retention schedule.

[15:10] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Understood. We’ll package that up.

[15:14] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): Another technical one: for “usage events,” do you store user IDs that we send? Like end-user identifiers in the request metadata.

[15:23] Mei Lin (Redwood): If you pass metadata, best practice is don’t pass PII. We can support customer-provided tags for cost allocation (like project_id), but we recommend you use pseudonymous IDs. Metering requires account-level identifiers, not end-user PII.

[15:43] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Do you enforce that? Or it’s just advice.

[15:47] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Mostly guidance. We can help configure filters or linting in SDK examples, but we can’t prevent someone from sending arbitrary text in prompts. That’s why private deployment is preferred for highly regulated content. But for metering records, we keep the schema limited.

[16:10] Priya Desai (Contoso): If we go with hosted initially, can we later move to VPC/Private without changing usage definitions?

[16:18] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, the goal is consistent metering definitions across hosted/dedicated/private. There can be differences in what logs are available to you depending on deployment, but the meters and invoice semantics stay aligned.

[16:36] Mark Ellison (Contoso): On audit trails again: you said internal access is audited. Is that like Snowflake query logs? Sorry, I’m guessing.

[16:45] Mei Lin (Redwood): That’s the right category. Access to the data warehouse and restricted tables is logged, and we also track access requests. We can provide a controls description rather than the raw warehouse config.

[17:04] Priya Desai (Contoso): I want to make sure: if there’s a billing dispute, can you produce the data to show how you computed billed tokens?

[17:11] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes. We can show the meter spec, the relevant usage exports, and the reconciliation to the invoice. We also maintain change history for meter logic so you know which version applied to a billing period.

[17:31] Hannah Cho (Contoso): That’s important.

[17:34] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Priya, what’s the format your auditors want? A PDF attestation? A CSV export? Both?

[17:41] Priya Desai (Contoso): Typically: (1) policy documents, (2) SOC 2, (3) if there’s an incident, we need audit evidence. For billing, we want the ability to store monthly exports ourselves. CSV is fine.

[17:56] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Great.

[18:00] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Can you confirm: do you have data-quality monitoring for those usage exports? Like completeness, late arriving events.

[18:09] Mei Lin (Redwood): Yes. We monitor freshness and completeness, and we have drift checks between usage events and the billing ledger. Late events are tracked; there’s a freeze window at month-end so Finance can close reliably. Late events after the freeze get handled via adjustments/credits depending on policy.

[18:33] Priya Desai (Contoso): Freeze window — explain.

[18:36] Mei Lin (Redwood): Basically, to avoid invoices changing endlessly due to late telemetry, we define a cutoff for a billing period. After that, events can still arrive but they’re handled in a controlled way. That’s part of audit readiness.

[18:56] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): If there’s a cutoff, does that mean your usage API might show something different than the invoice?

[19:03] Mei Lin (Redwood): We aim to keep them consistent by applying the same rules to customer exports, and by labeling data as “finalized” vs “provisional” where appropriate. We can clarify the semantics.

[19:21] Mark Ellison (Contoso): This is fine. We just need it documented.

[19:25] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yep.

[19:27] (brief pause)

[19:29] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Also: do you have a retention policy for audit logs themselves? Like how long do you keep “who accessed what” logs.

[19:37] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, there is retention for audit logs. We’ll provide the policy schedule.

[19:46] Priya Desai (Contoso): Any chance we can get that today? We’re trying to make a go/no-go next week.

[19:53] Jordan Blake (Redwood): We can send the packet today EOD, and if we need to fill in specific retention numbers, we’ll do that in writing tomorrow after confirming with our compliance team.

[20:06] Michael Grant (Redwood): And for contract language, we can propose wording that covers usage definitions (tokens, retries, caching) and dispute/credit process. That should help procurement.

[20:23] Mark Ellison (Contoso): One more: Do you have an incident history for billing correctness? Like have you had billing mistakes.

[20:31] Jordan Blake (Redwood): We’ve had typical early-stage issues like any platform — like edge cases around retries or streaming metering — but we’ve invested heavily in hardening. We run automated meter sanity checks, and we do shadow comparisons before changes roll out. If an issue occurs, we follow an incident process and remediate with credits when appropriate.

[20:55] Priya Desai (Contoso): Good answer. We’ll still want the controls in writing.

[21:00] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Understood.

[21:03] Diego Ramirez (Contoso): From engineering: do you allow us to tag requests with our own cost center and have it show in usage exports?

[21:11] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Yes, within limits. We can support metadata tags and project identifiers. We’ll make sure it doesn’t interfere with the meter.

[21:22] Mei Lin (Redwood): And we can help define a safe tagging scheme so you avoid PII.

[21:31] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Great.

[21:34] Priya Desai (Contoso): Okay I think that covers it. Recap the next steps?

[21:39] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Sure. 1) We’ll email the security packet plus the retention/access controls document pointers today. 2) You’ll confirm your retention requirement — especially the 7-year evidence need — and whether hosted is acceptable short term. 3) We’ll set a follow-up for audit log export format and RBAC mapping. And Michael will send suggested contract language for usage definitions and dispute window.

[22:06] Hannah Cho (Contoso): Works.

[22:08] Mark Ellison (Contoso): Yep.

[22:10] Priya Desai (Contoso): Thanks.

[22:12] Jordan Blake (Redwood): Thanks everyone.

[End of transcript]

title: Prompt hash segmentation & eviction scenarios - Helix Consulting technical deep dive
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-02-19
Time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~62 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Avery (Redwood SE), Marcus (Helix CTO), Sofia (Helix ML Lead), Ethan (Helix Infra), Lina (Helix Security)
Links shared: deck: https://redwood.example.com/helix/prompt-cache-workshop-deck, bench sheet: https://sheets.example.com/helix-cache-bench, security packet: https://redwood.example.com/security/packet.pdf

Summary (auto-gen, may be rough):
- Technical deep dive on prompt hash segmentation, eviction scenarios, and multi-tenant safety for Helix Consulting's assistant product.
- Focus on hit-rate optimization, safe shared KV cache keys, tenant isolation strategies, and VPC/on-prem constraints.
- Agreed next steps: Helix to share prompt families and traces; Redwood to share eviction policy and pilot configs; schedule pilot.

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan (AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining. we'll do a deep dive into prompt cache keying and evictions. apologies for the slight delay, got pulled in on another thread.
[00:23] Marcus (CTO): No worries. Appreciate you carving time. We're hoping to validate a shared-KV approach without leaking context between clients.
[00:35] Priya (SE): Great, Marcus. quick agenda: we'll cover hash segmentation strategies, deterministic salts, eviction policies, hotset isolation, and then tradeoffs for hosted vs private/VPC.
[00:52] Sofia (ML Lead): Sounds good. we have a bunch of short, repetitive prompts (customer support) and some longer generative flows for summarization; hit rates look different across these families.
[01:10] Ethan (Infra): Also we need to understand network egress and VPC peering overhead if we do private deployment; and KMS latency for tokenization keys.
[01:30] Jordan (AE): Totally. quick calibration: our runtime supports prefix/KV cache at token level and we can shard by hashed prompt signature or by tenant. the question is how you want to balance memory usage vs cross-tenant cache efficiency.
[01:55] Marcus: Right. Our billing model and SLA require tenant isolation if there's any chance of PII cross-contamination. but performance is also a big cost center.
[02:13] Priya: Understood. two patterns we usually see: 1) Strict tenant silo: every tenant gets own cache namespace, zero chance of leakage, but lower global hit rate. 2) Shared cache with keyed namespace + per-key tenant binding or salted keys to avoid collisions — higher hit rate for common prompts, but must be careful with metadata and audit.
[02:40] Sofia: Could you explain salted keys? I saw mention in docs but unclear in implication.
[02:48] Priya: Sure. salted key means we include a tenant-specific salt when hashing the prompt, so even if two tenants ask the same prompt, hashes differ. that preserves isolation but defeats cross-tenant hits. alternatively you can use a shared salt for some prompt classes, or opt-in shared keys for public/common prompts.
[03:10] Marcus: So option three is hybrid: some prompts public-shared, others tenant-siloed. How do we classify prompts safe to share?
[03:23] Lina (Security): from security POV, we need deterministic rules: any prompt that contains customer-provided PII, or references tenant config, must be siloed. generic system prompts or public FAQs could be shared. we also need audit logs linking cache hits to tenant IDs for forensics.
[03:45] Priya: Audit logs are supported — but note there is a perf tradeoff if you log every token hit synchronously; we can do async logging.
[04:00] Avery (SE): Also an important thing: prefix collisions can happen when prompts are similar but not identical; our hash function is robust but collisions are non-zero globally. we recommend a namespace + prefix-length fingerprinting approach to reduce false hot matches.
[04:18] Ethan: What about upstream model KV cache vs runtime prefix cache? I want to understand double-caching behavior.
[04:28] Priya: Good point. RW runtime handles KV caching at the model-attention level; our prefix cache is a higher level that stores repeated conditioning sequences. for most flows the prefix cache avoids re-running the expensive initial decode; both can coexist but you should avoid duplicated caching for the same sequences. we usually align TTLs and eviction policies.
[04:55] Jordan: I'll share a short diagram in the deck showing where prefix cache sits vs model KV.
[05:05] Sofia: Can you walk through hit-rate modeling? we have a traffic histogram with 80% short canonical prompts and a 20% long tail of unique prompts.
[05:18] Priya: Sure. for heavy short canonical families, shared cache boosts hit-rate dramatically. if 80% are identical across tenants, you can save lots. but if the long tail contains business-sensitive text, you must silo that tail. so hybrid classification plus a dynamic promoter system works well.
[05:40] Marcus: Dynamic promoter?
[05:43] Priya: It's a policy that marks keys observed across multiple tenants as "promotable" to the shared namespace after X observations and a safety check (no PII patterns, no tenant-specific markers). basically begin tenant-siloed, promote only after safe threshold.
[06:05] Sofia: Interesting. what's a good X? 10? 50?
[06:10] Priya: Depends on traffic. for a low-volume tenant, 10 might cause false promotion; we'd suggest relative frequency thresholds and time windows — e.g., 50 occurrences across at least 3 distinct tenant accounts within 7 days.
[06:28] Marcus: That makes sense. anything else for safety?
[06:32] Lina: We need cryptographic proofs of non-reversibility for hashing and salt rotation policies. also ability to revoke promoted keys if we later detect leakage.
[06:43] Priya: Key revocation is supported: we can mark key ID revoked and flush corresponding cache entries. but note revocation latency depends on distributed cache invalidation; on a large cluster that can be seconds to tens of seconds.
[06:56] Jordan: quick poll — Helix, are you leaning hosted (Redwood API) or private VPC?
[07:04] Marcus: We're evaluating private VPC for data residency and to integrate with internal KMS. but we might pilot on hosted first.
[07:12] Priya: If you go private, you get tighter network controls and can run on-prem for air-gapped customers. some of the cache internals change: eviction is local to the deployment; if you want global shared cache across regions you need cross-region replication which adds complexity.
[07:33] Ethan: About eviction policies — LRU vs LFU vs segmentation per-tenant. What's Redwood's recommendation for mixed workloads?
[07:43] Priya: For mixed short-hot and long-tail, we recommend a segmented policy: keep a hotset for high-frequency canonical prompts (LFU or TinyLFU works well), plus a per-tenant LRU partition for recent unique prompts. that gives stable hot hits while bounding per-tenant memory.
[08:04] Avery: We also have an adaptive TTL layer: keys that survive multiple reuses get upgraded to longer TTLs; one-off prompts expire quickly.
[08:14] Sofia: Do we have metrics on hit-rate uplift for typical setups?
[08:18] Priya: Bench numbers vary, but for support-bot style workloads we often see hit-rate go from 12% to 62% with a hybrid shared+promote approach, reducing token costs by ~35-50% depending on model. we'll share our bench sheet.
[08:37] Marcus: Cost saving is compelling. but I'm worried about cache poisoning or adversarial prompts causing degenerate keys.
[08:46] Priya: Good concern. We recommend entropy checks on prompt content — e.g., detect prompts that include large free-form dumps, code blobs, or base64 content that artificially changes hash distribution. Those get flagged and either quarantined into tenant-only cache or rejected for promotion.
[09:07] Lina: Also request auth must be strongly tied to cache writes; writes should require client auth with mTLS or signed tokens.
[09:16] Jordan: For hosted mode we use token-based auth with RBAC; for private/VPC you can enforce mTLS between services.
[09:25] Ethan: On the implementation side, how do you store TTL and metadata? I'm concerned about per-key metadata bloat.
[09:35] Avery: We store compact metadata: 1) small fingerprint, 2) tenant-tag bitset (or tenant ID for silo), 3) frequency counter (tiny int), and 4) last-used timestamp. that's ~40-80 bytes overhead per entry depending on config, plus token payload.
[09:52] Marcus: That could be large at 100M keys. What's the recommendation to control memory?
[09:58] Priya: Sharding and eviction boundaries: cap total entries, use approximate counting (e.g., Count-Min sketches) for global LFU counters, and use TTL tiers for older infrequent entries. also consider quantization of model weights to reduce model memory so you can allocate more RAM to cache.
[10:20] Sofia: You mentioned quantization — does that impact cache semantics?
[10:24] Priya: Not directly. quantization affects model compute & memory. the prefix cache is independent. but quantization affects token decoding latency and might change the ideal batching window. we can recommend quantization profiles per model class.
[10:39] Marcus: Any pitfalls with batching + cache? we can't increase latency beyond 350ms P95.
[10:45] Priya: continuous batching gains throughput but adds micro-latency. our smart batching tries to keep fast-path requests below your SLO by using latency corridors and senders: small bursts go immediately, larger batches are delayed by up to X ms to fill batch. integration with cache: if a prefix cache hit completes locally, we avoid model invocation so latency stays low. misses that land in batching may add up.
[11:10] Ethan: Is there a recommended max batch size for low-latency applications?
[11:13] Priya: For sub-350ms P95, we typically keep interactive requests batch size <= 4-8 depending on GPU and model size. for background jobs larger batches are fine. the runtime automatically picks batch size based on queue depth and SLOs.
[11:32] Marcus: What logs will we get for debugging hits/misses?
[11:37] Jordan: We provide per-route dashboards showing hit-rate, per-key histograms (hashed), eviction traces, and per-tenant memory usage. logs can be exported to your SIEM.
[11:50] Lina: Need assurance that hashed keys are irreversible in logs.
[11:53] Priya: We can offer one-way fingerprints (HMAC with rotation off your KMS). logs then store only the HMAC fingerprint not the clear prompt. for debugging we can optionally store redacted prompt snippets under stricter access.
[12:12] Sofia: Let's step through a concrete example; I have a canonical short-form prompt: "Summarize user message" with a 12-token system prompt. How does it flow?
[12:25] Priya: Flow: 1) Request arrives, compute prompt fingerprint (includes system+user context per policy). 2) Lookup prefix cache keyed by fingerprint; if hit return cached state. 3) if miss, model invoked with batching decisions; prefix is materialized and potentially inserted into cache depending on policy. 4) if promoted to shared after thresholds, future cross-tenant hits can be served.
[12:56] Marcus: For the fingerprint, do you include tenant ID by default?
[13:00] Priya: We include tenant ID only in tenant-siloed policy. for shared policy we omit tenant ID and may include a classification tag.
[13:10] Ethan: How is classification done reliably? simple regex? ML classifier?
[13:15] Priya: Hybrid: lightweight heuristics (PII regex, size thresholds) for fast decisions at the edge, plus offline ML classifier to recommend promotions. realtime decisions prioritize safety and latency.
[13:31] Jordan: Quick interjection — as an example, we had a customer with a 30% hit-rate gain using a promote-after-50-occurrences policy and tinyLFU, but they had to tune thresholds to avoid promoting repetitive but sensitive prompts.
[13:52] Sofia: Makes sense. For pilot, we want to start conservative. initial policies: tenant-silo for anything matching PII heuristic, shared for known public FAQ templates; promote threshold 200 occurrences across 5 tenants, quarantine for base64-like payloads.
[14:15] Priya: That's reasonable for a conservative start. we'll include that as a sample config.
[14:22] Marcus: About pilot ops — what's required from our infra team?
[14:27] Priya: We'll need access to anonymized traces, a staging API key, and for VPC pilot either peering info or a private deploy. for hosted pilot, we just need sample traffic replay.
[14:40] Ethan: For replay we'll scrub PII; are there any formats you prefer?
[14:45] Priya: JSONL with fields: timestamp, tenant_id (anonymized), system_prompt_hash, user_prompt_text (scrubbed), response_length. the bench sheet has a template.
[14:58] Lina: One more on compliance: we require audit trails for key promotions and revocations, and an exportable ledger for a 90-day window.
[15:08] Jordan: Redwood can provide audit exports. note that storage retention for logs is an add-on but included in enterprise packages.
[15:18] Sofia: Can we simulate an attack where someone tries to poison promotion by injecting many repeated prompts across synthetic tenants?
[15:27] Priya: Yes. recommended mitigations: rate-limit promotions per tenant, require promotions to come from distinct real tenant accounts (we can exclude suspect accounts), and add anomaly detection on promotion traffic. we'll run such a fuzz test in the pilot.
[15:46] Marcus: Good. can we talk briefly about eviction storm behavior when a large number of keys expire at once?
[15:53] Avery: Eviction storms are handled with staggered expiration (randomized TTL jitter) and async lazy deletion. we also use write-behind removal to avoid thundering herd.
[16:06] Ethan: If we run in private mode with our KMS, does key rotation require downtime?
[16:12] Priya: No downtime. we support KMS-backed HMAC with seamless rotation. old HMACs can be accepted for a grace period while you re-hash entries if you choose to.
[16:25] Marcus: Any recommendations on fingerprint length? shorter=smaller metadata vs collision risk.
[16:31] Priya: 128-bit fingerprint is a good balance; collision risk negligible for your scale. if you need extra safety we can do 256-bit at higher storage cost.
[16:42] Sofia: A question on debugging: if a reply is wrong due to stale prefix in cache, how do we detect and remediate?
[16:50] Priya: We recommend a TTL and a freshness score — if model quality metrics degrade we can trigger a cache flush policy for affected keys and backfill with replays. also we store sampling of served prompts to run automated evals.
[17:05] Marcus: How to handle tenant migrations? if a tenant moves from hosted to private, how to migrate cache entries?
[17:14] Avery: We treat cache as ephemeral — migration typically replays warmup traffic. cross-deployment replication is possible but adds complexity and must preserve keys securely; we usually recommend warmup replay.
[17:30] Sofia: About metrics: what dashboards do you provide out of the box?
[17:35] Jordan: per-route hit-rate, per-model token savings, per-tenant memory and eviction counters, promotion logs, and a promotion audit stream.
[17:46] Lina: Will those dashboards leak prompt content?
[17:49] Priya: No, by default dashboards show only hashed fingerprints and aggregate stats; we can enable redacted snippets under strict RBAC.
[18:00] Marcus: Let's get tactical on pilot timeline. We want a 2-week test with replay and live traffic shadowing.
[18:07] Priya: We recommend a 3-phase pilot: 1) replay (1 week) to validate hit-rates and safety checks, 2) shadow live traffic (few days), 3) limited canary with real responses for a small percent.
[18:21] Marcus: Good. What resources from Redwood do we need?
[18:24] Jordan: AE to coordinate, SE support for config and bench, Solutions Eng for pilot infra, and access to security docs.
[18:33] Sofia: We'll set internal review with legal and security before sharing traces. expect that early next week.
[18:40] Lina: We'll request the security packet and review KMS integration notes.
[18:45] Jordan: We'll send the eviction policy doc and sample config after this call.
[18:50] Priya: Also including a sample promote-quarantine config and sample replay script.
[18:57] Marcus: Anything else to cover?
[19:00] Sofia: One micro question — do you support per-route custom TTLs?
[19:04] Avery: Yes. routes have policy layers: default, per-model override, and explicit per-route TTLs.
[19:13] Ethan: If we want strict tenant enforcement for some routes, can we lock down promotion for that route?
[19:17] Priya: Absolutely — route-level flags to disable promotion and force tenant-siloed writes.
[19:25] Marcus: Sounds good. let's recap next steps.
[19:28] Jordan: Recap: Jordan share eviction policy + sample config; Priya prepare pilot config and cost estimate; Sofia to upload prompt families and scrubbed traces; schedule pilot week of 2026-03-02.
[19:42] Marcus: Confirmed. thanks everyone. that was super helpful.
[19:47] Lina: thanks. we'll review security doc and revert.
[19:50] Jordan: Thanks all. we'll follow up via email with links and config.

End of meeting.
