[12:56] Marcus: For the fingerprint, do you include tenant ID by default?
[13:00] Priya: We include tenant ID only in tenant-siloed policy. for shared policy we omit tenant ID and may include a classification tag.
[13:10] Ethan: How is classification done reliably? simple regex? ML classifier?
[13:15] Priya: Hybrid: lightweight heuristics (PII regex, size thresholds) for fast decisions at the edge, plus offline ML classifier to recommend promotions. realtime decisions prioritize safety and latency.
[13:31] Jordan: Quick interjection — as an example, we had a customer with a 30% hit-rate gain using a promote-after-50-occurrences policy and tinyLFU, but they had to tune thresholds to avoid promoting repetitive but sensitive prompts.
[13:52] Sofia: Makes sense. For pilot, we want to start conservative. initial policies: tenant-silo for anything matching PII heuristic, shared for known public FAQ templates; promote threshold 200 occurrences across 5 tenants, quarantine for base64-like payloads.
[14:15] Priya: That's reasonable for a conservative start. we'll include that as a sample config.
[14:22] Marcus: About pilot ops — what's required from our infra team?
[14:27] Priya: We'll need access to anonymized traces, a staging API key, and for VPC pilot either peering info or a private deploy. for hosted pilot, we just need sample traffic replay.
[14:40] Ethan: For replay we'll scrub PII; are there any formats you prefer?
[14:45] Priya: JSONL with fields: timestamp, tenant_id (anonymized), system_prompt_hash, user_prompt_text (scrubbed), response_length. the bench sheet has a template.
[14:58] Lina: One more on compliance: we require audit trails for key promotions and revocations, and an exportable ledger for a 90-day window.
[15:08] Jordan: Redwood can provide audit exports. note that storage retention for logs is an add-on but included in enterprise packages.
[15:18] Sofia: Can we simulate an attack where someone tries to poison promotion by injecting many repeated prompts across synthetic tenants?
[15:27] Priya: Yes. recommended mitigations: rate-limit promotions per tenant, require promotions to come from distinct real tenant accounts (we can exclude suspect accounts), and add anomaly detection on promotion traffic. we'll run such a fuzz test in the pilot.
[15:46] Marcus: Good. can we talk briefly about eviction storm behavior when a large number of keys expire at once?
[15:53] Avery: Eviction storms are handled with staggered expiration (randomized TTL jitter) and async lazy deletion. we also use write-behind removal to avoid thundering herd.
[16:06] Ethan: If we run in private mode with our KMS, does key rotation require downtime?
[16:12] Priya: No downtime. we support KMS-backed HMAC with seamless rotation. old HMACs can be accepted for a grace period while you re-hash entries if you choose to.
[16:25] Marcus: Any recommendations on fingerprint length? shorter=smaller metadata vs collision risk.
[16:31] Priya: 128-bit fingerprint is a good balance; collision risk negligible for your scale. if you need extra safety we can do 256-bit at higher storage cost.
[16:42] Sofia: A question on debugging: if a reply is wrong due to stale prefix in cache, how do we detect and remediate?
[16:50] Priya: We recommend a TTL and a freshness score — if model quality metrics degrade we can trigger a cache flush policy for affected keys and backfill with replays. also we store sampling of served prompts to run automated evals.
[17:05] Marcus: How to handle tenant migrations? if a tenant moves from hosted to private, how to migrate cache entries?
[17:14] Avery: We treat cache as ephemeral — migration typically replays warmup traffic. cross-deployment replication is possible but adds complexity and must preserve keys securely; we usually recommend warmup replay.
[17:30] Sofia: About metrics: what dashboards do you provide out of the box?
[17:35] Jordan: per-route hit-rate, per-model token savings, per-tenant memory and eviction counters, promotion logs, and a promotion audit stream.
[17:46] Lina: Will those dashboards leak prompt content?
[17:49] Priya: No, by default dashboards show only hashed fingerprints and aggregate stats; we can enable redacted snippets under strict RBAC.
[18:00] Marcus: Let's get tactical on pilot timeline. We want a 2-week test with replay and live traffic shadowing.
[18:07] Priya: We recommend a 3-phase pilot: 1) replay (1 week) to validate hit-rates and safety checks, 2) shadow live traffic (few days), 3) limited canary with real responses for a small percent.
[18:21] Marcus: Good. What resources from Redwood do we need?
[18:24] Jordan: AE to coordinate, SE support for config and bench, Solutions Eng for pilot infra, and access to security docs.
[18:33] Sofia: We'll set internal review with legal and security before sharing traces. expect that early next week.
[18:40] Lina: We'll request the security packet and review KMS integration notes.
[18:45] Jordan: We'll send the eviction policy doc and sample config after this call.
[18:50] Priya: Also including a sample promote-quarantine config and sample replay script.
[18:57] Marcus: Anything else to cover?
[19:00] Sofia: One micro question — do you support per-route custom TTLs?
[19:04] Avery: Yes. routes have policy layers: default, per-model override, and explicit per-route TTLs.
[19:13] Ethan: If we want strict tenant enforcement for some routes, can we lock down promotion for that route?
[19:17] Priya: Absolutely — route-level flags to disable promotion and force tenant-siloed writes.
[19:25] Marcus: Sounds good. let's recap next steps.
[19:28] Jordan: Recap: Jordan share eviction policy + sample config; Priya prepare pilot config and cost estimate; Sofia to upload prompt families and scrubbed traces; schedule pilot week of 2026-03-02.
[19:42] Marcus: Confirmed. thanks everyone. that was super helpful.
[19:47] Lina: thanks. we'll review security doc and revert.
[19:50] Jordan: Thanks all. we'll follow up via email with links and config.

End of meeting.

title: Edge silo deployment intake - Ventana Medical - Redwood intro
preview:
Meeting header: 
Date: 2026-02-18 16:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes (recording)
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Liam Ortiz (Redwood SE), Jordan Blake (Redwood Solutions), Dr. Priya Rao (CTO, Ventana Medical), Miguel Santos (IT Ops Manager), Elena Park (Compliance), Noah Kim (ML Engineer)

Summary: quick intro for Ventana's requirements for an isolated 'edge-silo' deployment in a regulated lab environment. Focus areas: offline update workflow, hardware constraints (no H100s available at site, NVMe limits), approval path for airgapped installs, KMS/HSM handling, audit logs, and pilot success metrics.

[00:00] Maya Chen: hey everyone thanks for joining, i'm Maya with Redwood, i'm the a-e on this and i'll kick us off. we just wanted to learn about what you folks need for a private onprem install in your lab environment.
[00:18] Dr. Priya Rao: thanks Maya. yeah, we run clinical imaging pipelines and increasingly want an in-house llm for report drafting and triage. the main constraint is we are in a regulated facility, fully isolated no internet egress from the compute zone, so airgap is required.
[00:31] Miguel Santos: and just to call out, our ops team only allows approved hardware lists. currently we have a couple of A100 80GB nodes in the data center but nothing newer, and our NVMe footprint per host is 8 TB, not huge.
[00:46] Liam Ortiz: thanks, miguel. quick clarifying question, when you say airgap, is that fully no external network, or do you have a jumpbox in a DMZ for controlled updates?
[00:57] Elena Park: currently fully locked down. updates go through our compliance gate and are pushed by physical media or via a secure handoff using an approved external vendor. so yes, physical transfer is the expected pattern.
[01:11] Jordan Blake: that aligns with what we've done for other regulated labs — we call it an \"edge silo\" pattern — everything packaged as signed artifacts, customer performs an ingest and we provide verification tools.
[01:23] Dr. Priya Rao: can you explain the verification? we need to show auditors checksums and provenance.
[01:29] Liam Ortiz: sure. for airgapped customers we provide a signed bundle: container images (OCI) and model artifacts with SHA256 checksums and a signature from Redwood's release key. on the host side we provide a verifier that validates signatures and checksums before unpacking. that produces an audit record you can ship to your compliance log.
[01:47] Miguel Santos: is the key material stored in an H-S-M? we can't have private keys lying in plain files.
[01:52] Liam Ortiz: yep, we support HSM integration and the package verifier can be configured to validate via your HSM or a K-M-S interface. if the HSM is unreachable we also support an offline attestation flow where the verifier emits a signed statement you can bring to the KMS admin.
[02:10] Elena Park: puzzled transcription note: earlier we heard \"K-M-S\" — yes that's KMS. margin - auditors will want to confirm rotation policy and access controls.
[02:20] Maya Chen: for compliance we can share our SOC 2 (we sometimes say S-O-C two in decks), the release signing cert chain, and our supplied verifier's exec hash. we also have RBAC and SSO integrations for the control plane, but obviously the private control plane in an airgapped setup is customer-hosted.
[02:37] Dr. Priya Rao: control plane — do you mean a local control plane we run, or something we run behind a manager? how much host-level access do you need?
[02:45] Jordan Blake: good question. for fully private deployments we ship a control plane image you run inside your VPC/vlan. Redwood does not get access to it unless you opt in. typically we ask for a bootstrap step where your admin runs an installer, captures logs for troubleshooting (which you can redact), and shares those logs via secure transfer. no persistent outbound connection required.
[03:04] Miguel Santos: so debug access is manual only — good. what about patching? we can't reboot the cluster randomly; we need scheduled windows and signed patch bundles.
[03:14] Liam Ortiz: agreed. our update model supports staged bundles and a canary on a single node. for airgapped sites that canary step is manual — you bring the bundle to the canary node first, validate, then roll. we can provide scripts to automate verification and rollback helpers.
[03:31] Dr. Priya Rao: performance question — our prompts are around 512 tokens, often with clinical history pre-pended so effective context is often like 1k tokens. what throughput/latency should we expect on an A100 80GB with your runtime?
[03:44] Liam Ortiz: for a 1k context using a ~7B model quantized to int8, median p95 latency for a single request should be in the 400-600 ms ballpark on an A100 if KV caching is warm. if you use larger models or H100s we can push lower latencies. caveat — NVMe IO and host memory footprint matter for long contexts.
[04:04] Noah Kim: uh, small correction — our team plans to use a 13B distilled model initially, not 7B. how does that change numbers?
[04:10] Liam Ortiz: 13B quantized gives you roughly 2x the token compute. expect p95 around 700 ms to 1.2 s depending on batching. we can run benchmarks with your actual prompt set; that will give more reliable numbers.
[04:23] Miguel Santos: about quantization — we have concerns about determinism and output fidelity for clinical language. do you have evaluation reports on quality regressions after int8 quant?
[04:32] Jordan Blake: yes we provide quality regression evals tied to your prompt set. we run A/B style comparisons and surface token-level diffs, BLEU-like metrics and human-sample reviews. for clinical text we recommend a small human-in-the-loop acceptance step before rolling quantization across the fleet.
[04:49] Elena Park: auditors will want to see that step documented. also data retention — transcripts of inference may contain PHI. can Redwood guarantee inference logs can be disabled or purged?
[05:00] Maya Chen: absolutely. in private on-prem installs you control telemetry. by default we write audit logs locally to a configured directory; you can disable remote telemetry and configure retention/rotation. we also support a strict no-logging mode where model inputs/outputs are not persisted beyond in-memory processing.
[05:16] Dr. Priya Rao: good. one sticky point is secure transport for model updates — our vendor policy requires using a sealed USB device that is scanned and then manually mounted. can your update artifacts be applied from removable media?
[05:29] Liam Ortiz: yes, everything's file-based. the installer supports applying bundles from a local mount path. we also sign every artifact so the verifier will reject tampered media.
[05:38] Miguel Santos: any special requirements for the host OS? we run RHEL 8.6 with kpatch live updates; no docker allowed, only runc or CRI-O containers.
[05:49] Jordan Blake: we offer both container images and a bare-metal tarball if containers are not permitted. for CRI-O we supply OCI-compliant images and a lightweight runtime shim that manages the kv cache and gpus.
[06:01] Noah Kim: what's the recommended GPU memory footprint for a 13B quantized model? we have 80GB A100s but want to know usable concurrency.
[06:09] Liam Ortiz: for int8 13B you'll need about 20-28 GB of GPU memory for the model state, plus working memory for kv cache depending on sequence length. with 80GB you'd likely run 2-3 concurrent 1k context requests comfortably, maybe more with batching and token streaming offload.
[06:25] Dr. Priya Rao: latency targets for our product team are 1.2 s p95. seems feasible. what about fallback if the node capacity is exhausted — can you fall back to a smaller model on the same node?
[06:36] Maya Chen: yes we support model variants and runtime fallbacks. routing policies are configurable in the local control plane; you can prefer a high-precision 13B then fall back to a 7B distilled variant for overloaded conditions. the fallback is local, no internet required.
[06:49] Miguel Santos: encryption at rest — we need keys controlled by our KMS; Redwood cannot hold keys.
[06:55] Liam Ortiz: that's standard — all model files and artifacts can be encrypted with your key material. we only store unencrypted payloads transiently during install if you explicitly allow. otherwise use pre-encrypted artifacts and HSM-unseal during runtime.
[07:08] Elena Park: about identity and access — we want RBAC so only certain user roles can request inference logs. does the control plane integrate with our SAML directory?
[07:17] Jordan Blake: yes, the local control plane supports SAML 2.0 or LDAP integrations for enterprise RBAC. for completely isolated environments we ship a SAML stub that you can map to your internal IdP.
[07:28] Dr. Priya Rao: curious on operational expectations — how many people from Redwood do we expect to interact with during onboarding?
[07:34] Maya Chen: typically an AE, an SE for install and a Solutions engineer for initial tuning — so 2-3 folks. for pilot we often do a remote install assist, and if you want we can do an on-site engineer for the first week (that becomes part of procurement and scope).
[07:47] Miguel Santos: wondering about licensing and cost — do you charge per-token for private? we have to show opex vs capex numbers to finance.
[07:56] Maya Chen: for private deployments it's a subscription + optional usage metering. there's a platform fee for the control plane and then a per-node or per-GPU capacity price. we can provide a cost sheet tailored to your node mix.
[08:08] Noah Kim: competitor question — we've evaluated BentoML and some internal solutions. what differentiates Redwood here?
[08:15] Liam Ortiz: main differences: 1) our runtime optimizations for continuous batching and kv cache; 2) rollout tooling and signed artifact flows for airgapped installs; and 3) the unified experience across hosted/dedicated/private so you can move between modes without rewriting integration.
[08:31] Elena Park: one more compliance question — can we get archiveable audit logs that prove who approved a model update and when?
[08:38] Liam Ortiz: yes, the verifier emits a signed provenance record on apply that includes approver id (from your SAML/LDAP mapping), timestamp, artifact SHA and operator comments. that file can be exported and retained in your GRC system.
[08:52] Dr. Priya Rao: let's talk timeline — our target is to have a pilot running inside 3-4 weeks with a small dataset. is that realistic?
[08:59] Maya Chen: yes that's within our typical pilot cadence assuming hardware is available. critical path: you confirm hardware inventory and approve the security questionnaire, we ship signed artifacts, you schedule the install window.
[09:13] Miguel Santos: hardware inventory: i'll attach a list but initial note — no H100s, only two A100 80GB hosts, one spare 40G NIC and limited NVMe. also we have a policy that any removable media has to be scanned offsite first.
[09:29] Maya Chen: thanks miguel. we'll need that inventory and the approved change window. we'll also send a sample installer and the checksum list.
[09:36] Dr. Priya Rao: what about metrics we should collect during the pilot to evaluate success?
[09:41] Jordan Blake: recommended pilot success metrics: p95 latency for your 512-1k prompts, token cost per inference (we can estimate the compute cost), qualitative human review of outputs (clinician acceptance rate), and operational stability (uptime during the pilot window).
[09:56] Noah Kim: also add model drift detection — tracking quality over time; we may want alerts if regression crosses a threshold.
[10:01] Liam Ortiz: we have a quality monitoring hook in the control plane that can run evals against a baseline set and emit regression alerts. that can run locally and push only summaries if you permit.
[10:12] Elena Park: any required third-party vendors for the install? some vendors demand an approved list for the USB supplier.
[10:19] Maya Chen: no required vendors; we ship artifacts and you can use any approved transfer medium. we can also work with your hardware partner if you want Redwood to sign off on the install steps.
[10:29] Miguel Santos: curious about rollback speed — if an update causes issues, how long to revert to prior model and get the node back to service?
[10:36] Liam Ortiz: rollback is typically minutes to tens of minutes depending on NVMe speed because we stream the model from local storage. we provide a fast switch-over script that changes symlinks and restarts the runtime. full restore that includes cache warmup is longer — cache warming takes some tokens to reach steady state.
[10:50] Dr. Priya Rao: clarifying question: do you keep a cached copy of previous artifacts on disk or do we need to maintain our own archive?
[10:57] Jordan Blake: by default we keep previous versions in a configured retention directory, but we can configure zero-retention policy so only one version exists and you maintain separate archive.
[11:07] Miguel Santos: any special networking ports? remember we have a locked-down firewall.
[11:11] Liam Ortiz: for a fully isolated run, no outbound ports are required. for optional remote debug we use a single outbound TLS port to a bootstrap endpoint, but that's optional. otherwise all runtime traffic is local.
[11:24] Elena Park: auditors will want a runbook for incident response. do you provide templates?
[11:29] Maya Chen: yes, we provide runbook templates covering CVE response, rollback, and forensic collection steps. we'll include them in the checklist.
[11:38] Dr. Priya Rao: okay, let's surface the immediate risks: hardware constraints, update transport timeline, and operational training.
[11:48] Maya Chen: agreed. proposed immediate next steps — we'll email the private-deploy checklist and security pack, liam will draft a quick perf estimate for 13B quant on your two A100s, and noah will share the prompt set and sample artifacts for an initial run.
[12:02] Miguel Santos: does Redwood require network access for license activation? some vendors phone-home for license checks and we can't allow that.
[12:09] Liam Ortiz: no phone-home required. licenses are validated locally with signed tokens and don't require outbound connectivity.
[12:16] Dr. Priya Rao: great. can you also share references for other healthcare customers who have done similar isolated deployments?
[12:23] Maya Chen: yes we can share redacted case studies and two references; we'll include that in the follow-up email.
[12:29] Elena Park: and include SOC 2 docs, and the verifier code checksum if possible.
[12:33] Jordan Blake: we'll redact anything sensitive but include the public parts of our compliance pack.
[12:37] Noah Kim: small question on model provenance — do you instrument model weights with metadata like training dataset hashes?
[12:43] Liam Ortiz: we include model metadata: model name, version, training run id where available and artifact provenance. for open models provenance is limited to the source repo and hash.
[12:53] Maya Chen: we're almost out of time — last callouts?
[12:56] Miguel Santos: i'll push to get the inventory and the change window by end of week.
[13:01] Dr. Priya Rao: thanks all.
[13:03] Maya Chen: thanks, we'll follow up with the checklist and scheduling link.

Recap / Action items (spoken in call):
- Maya to send private deployment checklist, security pack, SOC2 docs, and references.
- Liam to produce perf estimate for 13B quant on two A10080GB hosts and propose quant options.
- Noah to share prompt set / sample artifacts for benchmark.
- Miguel to provide hardware inventory and confirm secure transport policy.

End of recording.

[09:13] Miguel Santos: hardware inventory: i'll attach a list but initial note — no H100s, only two A100 80GB hosts, one spare 40G NIC and limited NVMe. also we have a policy that any removable media has to be scanned offsite first.
[09:29] Maya Chen: thanks miguel. we'll need that inventory and the approved change window. we'll also send a sample installer and the checksum list.
[09:36] Dr. Priya Rao: what about metrics we should collect during the pilot to evaluate success?
[09:41] Jordan Blake: recommended pilot success metrics: p95 latency for your 512-1k prompts, token cost per inference (we can estimate the compute cost), qualitative human review of outputs (clinician acceptance rate), and operational stability (uptime during the pilot window).
[09:56] Noah Kim: also add model drift detection — tracking quality over time; we may want alerts if regression crosses a threshold.
[10:01] Liam Ortiz: we have a quality monitoring hook in the control plane that can run evals against a baseline set and emit regression alerts. that can run locally and push only summaries if you permit.
[10:12] Elena Park: any required third-party vendors for the install? some vendors demand an approved list for the USB supplier.
[10:19] Maya Chen: no required vendors; we ship artifacts and you can use any approved transfer medium. we can also work with your hardware partner if you want Redwood to sign off on the install steps.
[10:29] Miguel Santos: curious about rollback speed — if an update causes issues, how long to revert to prior model and get the node back to service?
[10:36] Liam Ortiz: rollback is typically minutes to tens of minutes depending on NVMe speed because we stream the model from local storage. we provide a fast switch-over script that changes symlinks and restarts the runtime. full restore that includes cache warmup is longer — cache warming takes some tokens to reach steady state.
[10:50] Dr. Priya Rao: clarifying question: do you keep a cached copy of previous artifacts on disk or do we need to maintain our own archive?
[10:57] Jordan Blake: by default we keep previous versions in a configured retention directory, but we can configure zero-retention policy so only one version exists and you maintain separate archive.
[11:07] Miguel Santos: any special networking ports? remember we have a locked-down firewall.
[11:11] Liam Ortiz: for a fully isolated run, no outbound ports are required. for optional remote debug we use a single outbound TLS port to a bootstrap endpoint, but that's optional. otherwise all runtime traffic is local.
[11:24] Elena Park: auditors will want a runbook for incident response. do you provide templates?
[11:29] Maya Chen: yes, we provide runbook templates covering CVE response, rollback, and forensic collection steps. we'll include them in the checklist.
[11:38] Dr. Priya Rao: okay, let's surface the immediate risks: hardware constraints, update transport timeline, and operational training.
[11:48] Maya Chen: agreed. proposed immediate next steps — we'll email the private-deploy checklist and security pack, liam will draft a quick perf estimate for 13B quant on your two A100s, and noah will share the prompt set and sample artifacts for an initial run.
[12:02] Miguel Santos: does Redwood require network access for license activation? some vendors phone-home for license checks and we can't allow that.
[12:09] Liam Ortiz: no phone-home required. licenses are validated locally with signed tokens and don't require outbound connectivity.
[12:16] Dr. Priya Rao: great. can you also share references for other healthcare customers who have done similar isolated deployments?
[12:23] Maya Chen: yes we can share redacted case studies and two references; we'll include that in the follow-up email.
[12:29] Elena Park: and include SOC 2 docs, and the verifier code checksum if possible.
[12:33] Jordan Blake: we'll redact anything sensitive but include the public parts of our compliance pack.
[12:37] Noah Kim: small question on model provenance — do you instrument model weights with metadata like training dataset hashes?
[12:43] Liam Ortiz: we include model metadata: model name, version, training run id where available and artifact provenance. for open models provenance is limited to the source repo and hash.
[12:53] Maya Chen: we're almost out of time — last callouts?
[12:56] Miguel Santos: i'll push to get the inventory and the change window by end of week.
[13:01] Dr. Priya Rao: thanks all.
[13:03] Maya Chen: thanks, we'll follow up with the checklist and scheduling link.

Recap / Action items (spoken in call):
- Maya to send private deployment checklist, security pack, SOC2 docs, and references.
- Liam to produce perf estimate for 13B quant on two A10080GB hosts and propose quant options.
- Noah to share prompt set / sample artifacts for benchmark.
- Miguel to provide hardware inventory and confirm secure transport policy.

End of recording.

title: Northwind Labs POC weekly sync - meters + usage dashboards
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Northwind Labs POC weekly sync - meters + usage dashboards
Date: 2026-02-14
Time: 9:02 AM PT
Duration: 35 minutes

Attendees
- Jordan Blake (Redwood Inference)
- Logan Wright (Redwood Inference)
- Nadia Rahman (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Northwind Labs)
- Ben Hartman (Northwind Labs)
- Sarah Kim (Northwind Labs)

Summary (auto-generated)
Northwind reviewed Redwood’s usage dashboard and asked for clarity on token counting, especially when cache hits occur and when requests are streamed. Redwood explained the difference between raw tokens and billed tokens, and how cache-hit attribution is represented in new usage API fields. The group discussed reconciliation between console numbers, usage exports, and invoice-like totals, plus minor concerns about timezone alignment for daily exports.

Topics
- Console usage dashboard filters (model, region, endpoint)
- Cache-hit attribution and how it affects billed tokens
- New usage API fields (billed_tokens vs raw_tokens, cache_hit indicators)
- Streaming semantics and partial completion behavior
- Reconciliation workflow and who should use which numbers

Questions (auto-generated)
- When prefix caching triggers, are “saved” tokens explicitly visible in the export?
- Are cached prompts billed at zero or reduced rate, and does that show up as billed_tokens?
- How are retries / fallbacks represented (one request_id or multiple)?
- Can we get daily exports aligned to a customer timezone?

Transcript

[00:00] Jordan Blake: Alright, cool. We’re live. Thanks everyone for hopping on. This is the Northwind POC weekly check-in, focus today on meters, the usage dashboard, and the cache hit stuff that came up in Slack.

[00:10] Priya Desai: Yep, thanks Jordan. We pulled the dashboard yesterday and… it’s good, but we have questions.

[00:16] Ben Hartman: Mostly around “why did billed tokens go down” on some days where our traffic was up.

[00:21] Jordan Blake: Love that problem to have. Logan and Nadia are here, they own a lot of the telemetry definitions. Sarah, good to meet you.

[00:27] Sarah Kim: Hi. I’m here because the numbers will end up in a spreadsheet on my side, so I need to understand what we’re looking at.

[00:34] Jordan Blake: Totally. Quick agenda: (1) walk the dashboard view you’re using, (2) talk cache-hit attribution and new usage API fields, (3) reconcile how you should interpret daily totals, and then next steps.

[00:49] Nadia Rahman: Sounds good.

[00:51] Logan Wright: Yep.

[00:53] Jordan Blake: Priya, do you want to share what you saw? Like which chart or which tab.

[00:58] Priya Desai: Sure. We’re in the Console, Usage tab, then “Hosted API”. We filtered to the llama model—uh, sorry, “Redwood Verified Llama 3.1 70B” I think. And region us-west.

[01:12] Ben Hartman: The panel shows Requests, Prompt tokens, Completion tokens, and then there’s “Billed tokens” now, which I don’t think was there last week.

[01:21] Jordan Blake: Yep, we added “billed tokens” as a first-class line item. It used to be implied.

[01:26] Priya Desai: Right. So the day we turned on caching on our gateway, we expected prompt tokens to go down, and they did. But we also see billed tokens drop more than prompt tokens, which is confusing.

[01:39] Logan Wright: Got it. So there are two things in play. One is your own caching, like if you’re caching upstream and not sending requests, then requests and tokens go down. The other is Redwood-side prefix/KV caching, which can still happen even if you’re still sending requests. In that case, you might send the same prefix and we can reuse parts of the compute path.

[01:57] Ben Hartman: But does that mean you bill fewer tokens? Or you bill the same tokens but just cheaper on your margin.

[02:04] Nadia Rahman: It depends on the product policy. For the POC you’re on, we’re tracking both raw_tokens and billed_tokens. Raw tokens are literally what the model processes in the tokenization sense. Billed tokens are the tokens that are eligible for billing after we apply rules like cache hit attribution, retries de-dupe, and certain streaming edge cases.

[02:25] Sarah Kim: Sorry—so billed tokens can be less than raw tokens even for the same request.

[02:29] Nadia Rahman: Yes. Example: if we have a cache hit on the prompt prefix, we can attribute some prompt tokens as cache_hit tokens. Depending on plan configuration, those may be billed at zero or at a reduced rate. Internally it’s represented as billed_tokens_prompt and billed_tokens_completion. In the UI we’re collapsing it into one “billed tokens” for now.

[02:49] Priya Desai: That’s the key thing. We need to know what is “saved” and how you represent it.

[02:54] Logan Wright: The new usage export fields include cache_hit boolean and cache_hit_tokens (or “cache_tokens_saved” depending on the endpoint). I’m going to say this carefully because naming changed once: we have raw_prompt_tokens, raw_completion_tokens, billed_prompt_tokens, billed_completion_tokens, and then cache_hit_prompt_tokens as a separate dimension.

[03:17] Ben Hartman: Wait, so it’s not actually “saved tokens” it’s “prompt tokens that were served from cache” basically.

[03:22] Logan Wright: Right. It’s attribution, not a claim that no compute happened at all, but in the common case it correlates with reduced compute.

[03:29] Priya Desai: Okay. On our side we’re doing deterministic prompt templates, so it should be pretty high hit rate.

[03:36] Jordan Blake: That’s consistent with what we saw; your workload is very cache friendly.

[03:40] Sarah Kim: So for finance, the number I should rely on is billed tokens, not prompt+completion.

[03:46] Jordan Blake: Correct. For forecasting and invoice alignment, billed tokens is the one.

[03:51] Ben Hartman: But in the UI, sometimes prompt+completion is higher, sometimes lower. I saw one hour where completion tokens were zero but the request succeeded.

[04:01] Logan Wright: That can happen on streaming if the client disconnects early. The model may have generated some tokens, but if the stream is cut at the transport layer, the event we get might mark the completion as partial. We should still have billed_completion_tokens if we emitted them, but there are edge cases where the final chunk isn’t recorded.

[04:20] Priya Desai: That’s the “partial chunk” thing we saw. Like we had a 200 response but our app timed out waiting for the last bytes.

[04:28] Nadia Rahman: Yep. There is also the retry/fallback path. If we retry server-side, we have to make sure we don’t double count. We’ve been hardening idempotency keys on usage events so the request_id is the unit of billing even if there were internal attempts.

[04:45] Ben Hartman: Good. Because we were worried about double charging. We saw that in another vendor.

[04:50] Jordan Blake: Totally fair. That’s one of the reasons we’re adding these fields explicitly and doing “shadow billing compare” before any meter changes affect invoices. For your POC, you’re basically getting the newest semantics.

[05:03] Sarah Kim: I’d like to understand what I can reconcile. Like if I pull daily usage exports, will it match what the UI shows.

[05:11] Logan Wright: It should within a small lag. UI is near-real-time aggregated. Exports are usually hourly to daily and can have late-arriving events. We typically recommend a freshness window of, say, 24 hours before you treat a day as final.

[05:27] Sarah Kim: That’s important for month end.

[05:30] Jordan Blake: We can talk about freeze windows later, but yes, we can do a “close” process.

[05:36] Priya Desai: On the cache hit attribution, does it apply to completion tokens too?

[05:41] Nadia Rahman: Generally cache hits apply to prompt tokens—prefix caching. For completions, we don’t “cache” generation the same way, unless you do your own application layer caching of outputs. So you’ll mostly see cache_hit_prompt_tokens and raw/billed completion tokens unchanged.

[06:00] Ben Hartman: So if billed tokens drop a lot, it’s mostly prompt side.

[06:03] Nadia Rahman: Correct.

[06:05] Priya Desai: One more nuance: we use tool calls sometimes. Does the tool call tokens show up in completion or prompt.

[06:12] Logan Wright: Great question. Tool/function call tokens typically land in completion tokens because they’re generated by the model, but depending on the API shape they can be separated. In the OpenAI compatible endpoints, it gets weird because the tool call is in the assistant message but it’s structured.

[06:29] Nadia Rahman: We normalize it as “completion” for token accounting, and then the usage event includes a flag like tool_call_present. Not sure the UI shows that yet.

[06:41] Priya Desai: Okay, fine.

[06:43] Jordan Blake: Let’s anchor on what you need for the POC decision. You want predictable spend and a way to explain to your leadership that caching is real and not just Redwood changing numbers.

[06:54] Priya Desai: Yes.

[06:55] Sarah Kim: And we need a daily report that we can compare to our internal counts.

[07:01] Ben Hartman: And also when we switch models, the tokens might change because tokenization differs.

[07:07] Nadia Rahman: Yep. Tokenization can vary by model family and tokenizer version. We use the tokenizer associated with the deployed model build. That’s why reconciling “our” tokens to “your” tokens only works if you use the same tokenizer implementation.

[07:24] Priya Desai: We do use a local tokenizer, but it’s probably not exactly the same.

[07:29] Logan Wright: That’s common. The best recon is request_id level: compare the usage export for a few known prompts and completions. If you can provide 2-3 request_ids where you see a difference, we can show you the raw_prompt_tokens/raw_completion_tokens from our side.

[07:47] Priya Desai: Okay, I can do that.

[07:50] Jordan Blake: Can we pivot to the dashboard for a second. Ben, you mentioned there’s a “cache hit rate” panel too?

[07:56] Ben Hartman: Yeah. It’s on the right, “Cache hit %” and “Cached prompt tokens”. It’s not super clear if that’s a rate of requests or tokens.

[08:06] Logan Wright: That’s a good callout. In v1, it’s token-based: cached prompt tokens divided by total prompt tokens. We’re adding a request-based hit rate as a separate metric, because it’s easier to reason about sometimes.

[08:22] Nadia Rahman: Also, the definition of “cache hit” can be partial. If the first 200 tokens hit but the rest is new, it still counts as a cache hit, but not 100% cached.

[08:33] Ben Hartman: That might be what we’re seeing, because our templates share the system prompt and first few messages, but the last message varies.

[08:42] Priya Desai: Exactly.

[08:44] Jordan Blake: So on the business side, you should see billed prompt tokens reduce proportional to cached prompt tokens. We can validate with a sample export.

[08:55] Sarah Kim: That’s what I want. Like, “show me a CSV with columns and I can do the math.”

[09:00] Jordan Blake: Great. We’ll do that.

[09:03] Priya Desai: One more thing. In the usage export we pulled last week, there was no cache field. Is that just not enabled.

[09:11] Logan Wright: Yeah. Those fields were behind a feature gate. We can enable it for your org. That will add columns; it won’t break existing parsing unless you’re strict about schema.

[09:24] Ben Hartman: We are strict.

[09:26] Jordan Blake: Okay, then we should version it. We can share a “v2 export schema” and you can switch when ready. In the API it’s additive fields, so older clients ignore them. But if you’re doing CSV ingestion with exact headers, we’ll coordinate.

[09:42] Sarah Kim: Please coordinate.

[09:45] Nadia Rahman: Also, there’s a distinction: usage API vs invoice. For POC you’re not getting invoices, but we’re aligning the usage API with what would be invoiced, meaning billed_tokens is what would map to charges.

[10:01] Priya Desai: Great.

[10:03] Ben Hartman: Can you confirm how region impacts pricing. Like if the dashboard says us-east sometimes, does that mean we got routed.

[10:11] Logan Wright: There is smart routing on capacity, but for POC we mostly keep you pinned to us-west unless we’re failing over. If you see another region, it might be either a fallback or a mis-tagged region dimension—there was a bug we fixed recently. But for your account, I can check.

[10:29] Jordan Blake: Logan, can you pull a seven-day report by region for them.

[10:33] Logan Wright: Yep.

[10:34] Priya Desai: We did see some “unknown” region values in export.

[10:38] Nadia Rahman: That’s exactly the missing dimension issue. It should be resolved, but there might be old events still.

[10:45] Sarah Kim: Old events as in last week.

[10:48] Logan Wright: Yeah, within the last couple weeks. We can backfill, but for your POC, we can just annotate it.

[10:56] Jordan Blake: Cool. Let’s talk about what you need to present internally. Priya, is it more performance or cost.

[11:03] Priya Desai: Both, but honestly the CFO question is “why is this line item unpredictable.” So the metering clarity is huge.

[11:12] Sarah Kim: Yes.

[11:13] Ben Hartman: We can handle the tokenization variance, but we need to understand what Redwood counts.

[11:19] Nadia Rahman: Then we should share a short dictionary:
- raw_prompt_tokens: tokenizer count for prompt
- raw_completion_tokens: tokenizer count for generated output
- billed_prompt_tokens: raw prompt tokens minus any cache-attributed tokens (or discounted, depending)
- billed_completion_tokens: typically equals raw completion tokens, minus de-duped retries
- cache_hit_prompt_tokens: portion of prompt tokens served from cache
- cache_hit: boolean if any portion of prompt was cache hit

[11:47] Priya Desai: Perfect. That’s what we need.

[11:50] Jordan Blake: Nadia, can you send that as an email after.

[11:53] Nadia Rahman: Yep.

[11:54] Ben Hartman: There’s still a question: do you ever bill for tokens generated during a server retry. Like if you generate 200 tokens, fail, retry, generate again.

[12:05] Logan Wright: We bill the successful request once, with de-dupe logic so internal attempts do not show up as multiple billed events. In the raw events, we may record attempts for debugging, but billing uses a stable idempotency key.

[12:20] Nadia Rahman: And for streaming, if the client disconnects, we’re working on making it consistent: do you bill only what was delivered, or what was generated. Current policy is bill what was delivered/confirmed, but it’s an area we’re tightening. That’s why you might see completion tokens 0 in some rare cases; we’d rather undercount than double count.

[12:40] Sarah Kim: Understood. That’s actually good.

[12:43] Priya Desai: We prefer that. The worst thing is arguing about phantom tokens.

[12:47] Jordan Blake: Exactly.

[12:49] Ben Hartman: Another small thing: the dashboard shows “Requests” but we have batched requests for embeddings in other systems. Not sure if that’s relevant here.

[12:58] Logan Wright: For chat/completions it’s 1 request equals 1 API call. For embeddings, if you send an array of inputs, we count it as 1 request but multiple “units” for embeddings. For your current POC you’re mostly chat, so not relevant.

[13:14] Priya Desai: Good.

[13:15] Jordan Blake: Let’s do a quick live readback. Ben, what’s the main concern you want closed by next week.

[13:21] Ben Hartman: I want a clear mapping between cache hit metrics and billed token reductions. If we can show one day’s worth of exports and compute it, we’re good.

[13:32] Priya Desai: And I want to confirm our internal “token per request” metrics aren’t wildly off because of tokenizer differences.

[13:40] Sarah Kim: And I want timezone alignment. The export day boundary is currently UTC, right.

[13:47] Logan Wright: Yes, it’s UTC by default.

[13:49] Sarah Kim: We operate on Pacific time. Otherwise daily charts look spiky.

[13:54] Jordan Blake: We’ve heard that before. We can either (a) provide timezone param in export, or (b) provide hourly exports and you roll up. Right now, easiest is hourly plus your rollup.

[14:09] Sarah Kim: Hourly is fine as long as it’s stable.

[14:12] Logan Wright: It’s stable, but there is still late arrival. We’ll note the freshness SLA.

[14:18] Nadia Rahman: And for month-end we can establish a “freeze” where after a certain time we stop backfilling without explicit approval. That’s more for paid contracts though.

[14:29] Sarah Kim: Good to know.

[14:31] Jordan Blake: We’ll keep it simple for the POC.

[14:34] Priya Desai: One more: are you planning to change these meter definitions soon. We don’t want the numbers shifting mid-test.

[14:43] Jordan Blake: Great question. The definitions are stabilizing. We’re adding fields, but we’re not changing how billed tokens are computed for you without notice. If there’s a meter fix, we’d run it in shadow mode first and tell you. Logan/Nadia can confirm.

[14:58] Logan Wright: Yep. Any change goes through change control, shadow compare, and a rollout window.

[15:05] Priya Desai: Great.

[15:07] Ben Hartman: Can you explain “shadow compare” quickly.

[15:10] Logan Wright: It means we compute both old and new meter outputs from the same raw request logs. We compare drift. If it’s within expected bounds, we can flip it. If it’s not, we treat it like a bug. But invoices and customer-facing totals stay on the old until approved.

[15:30] Ben Hartman: Nice.

[15:31] Jordan Blake: Okay. I think we covered the conceptual stuff. Let’s talk logistics: Nadia sends the dictionary, Logan pulls the last 7 days cache hit and region breakdown, Priya sends 3 request_ids.

[15:45] Priya Desai: Yep.

[15:46] Sarah Kim: And I’ll reply with timezone preferences.

[15:49] Jordan Blake: Perfect. Before we wrap, any competitor comparisons you’re doing. Just so we can help.

[15:56] Priya Desai: We’re also testing OpenAI and Bedrock. Mainly just price predictability.

[16:03] Ben Hartman: And Anthropic for one flow.

[16:05] Jordan Blake: Got it. We’ll keep the focus on predictability and auditability.

[16:10] (crosstalk)

[16:11] Logan Wright: One more thing—when we enable the new fields, you might see columns like billed_tokens_total. If your parser breaks, ping us. We can also give you JSON API instead of CSV.

[16:23] Ben Hartman: JSON is better.

[16:25] Sarah Kim: CSV is easier for me, but we can do both.

[16:29] Jordan Blake: We’ll do both.

[16:31] Priya Desai: Can we schedule that working session with Logan/Nadia. Like Tuesday.

[16:36] Jordan Blake: Yep, I’ll send a hold. Tuesday 10am PT.

[16:41] Nadia Rahman: Works.

[16:42] Logan Wright: Works.

[16:44] Jordan Blake: Great. Quick recap:
1) Redwood enables usage API fields for billed tokens + cache attribution.
2) Send a sample export and a field dictionary.
3) Northwind sends request_ids for token diffs.
4) We do a working session to validate cache hit math and answer streaming edge questions.

[17:05] Sarah Kim: Sounds good.

[17:07] Priya Desai: Thanks.

[17:08] Ben Hartman: Thanks, all.

[17:10] Jordan Blake: Cool, talk soon.

(end)

Notes
- Transcript may contain minor inaccuracies due to automated transcription.
- “Cache hit” discussed refers to Redwood-side prefix/KV caching attribution as represented in usage events and exports.

[03:17] Ben Hartman: Wait, so it’s not actually “saved tokens” it’s “prompt tokens that were served from cache” basically.

[03:22] Logan Wright: Right. It’s attribution, not a claim that no compute happened at all, but in the common case it correlates with reduced compute.

[03:29] Priya Desai: Okay. On our side we’re doing deterministic prompt templates, so it should be pretty high hit rate.

[03:36] Jordan Blake: That’s consistent with what we saw; your workload is very cache friendly.

[03:40] Sarah Kim: So for finance, the number I should rely on is billed tokens, not prompt+completion.

[03:46] Jordan Blake: Correct. For forecasting and invoice alignment, billed tokens is the one.

[03:51] Ben Hartman: But in the UI, sometimes prompt+completion is higher, sometimes lower. I saw one hour where completion tokens were zero but the request succeeded.

[04:01] Logan Wright: That can happen on streaming if the client disconnects early. The model may have generated some tokens, but if the stream is cut at the transport layer, the event we get might mark the completion as partial. We should still have billed_completion_tokens if we emitted them, but there are edge cases where the final chunk isn’t recorded.

[04:20] Priya Desai: That’s the “partial chunk” thing we saw. Like we had a 200 response but our app timed out waiting for the last bytes.

[04:28] Nadia Rahman: Yep. There is also the retry/fallback path. If we retry server-side, we have to make sure we don’t double count. We’ve been hardening idempotency keys on usage events so the request_id is the unit of billing even if there were internal attempts.

[04:45] Ben Hartman: Good. Because we were worried about double charging. We saw that in another vendor.

[04:50] Jordan Blake: Totally fair. That’s one of the reasons we’re adding these fields explicitly and doing “shadow billing compare” before any meter changes affect invoices. For your POC, you’re basically getting the newest semantics.

[05:03] Sarah Kim: I’d like to understand what I can reconcile. Like if I pull daily usage exports, will it match what the UI shows.

[05:11] Logan Wright: It should within a small lag. UI is near-real-time aggregated. Exports are usually hourly to daily and can have late-arriving events. We typically recommend a freshness window of, say, 24 hours before you treat a day as final.

[05:27] Sarah Kim: That’s important for month end.

[05:30] Jordan Blake: We can talk about freeze windows later, but yes, we can do a “close” process.

[05:36] Priya Desai: On the cache hit attribution, does it apply to completion tokens too?

[05:41] Nadia Rahman: Generally cache hits apply to prompt tokens—prefix caching. For completions, we don’t “cache” generation the same way, unless you do your own application layer caching of outputs. So you’ll mostly see cache_hit_prompt_tokens and raw/billed completion tokens unchanged.

[06:00] Ben Hartman: So if billed tokens drop a lot, it’s mostly prompt side.

[06:03] Nadia Rahman: Correct.

[06:05] Priya Desai: One more nuance: we use tool calls sometimes. Does the tool call tokens show up in completion or prompt.

[06:12] Logan Wright: Great question. Tool/function call tokens typically land in completion tokens because they’re generated by the model, but depending on the API shape they can be separated. In the OpenAI compatible endpoints, it gets weird because the tool call is in the assistant message but it’s structured.

[06:29] Nadia Rahman: We normalize it as “completion” for token accounting, and then the usage event includes a flag like tool_call_present. Not sure the UI shows that yet.

[06:41] Priya Desai: Okay, fine.

[06:43] Jordan Blake: Let’s anchor on what you need for the POC decision. You want predictable spend and a way to explain to your leadership that caching is real and not just Redwood changing numbers.

[06:54] Priya Desai: Yes.

[06:55] Sarah Kim: And we need a daily report that we can compare to our internal counts.

[07:01] Ben Hartman: And also when we switch models, the tokens might change because tokenization differs.

[07:07] Nadia Rahman: Yep. Tokenization can vary by model family and tokenizer version. We use the tokenizer associated with the deployed model build. That’s why reconciling “our” tokens to “your” tokens only works if you use the same tokenizer implementation.

[07:24] Priya Desai: We do use a local tokenizer, but it’s probably not exactly the same.

[07:29] Logan Wright: That’s common. The best recon is request_id level: compare the usage export for a few known prompts and completions. If you can provide 2-3 request_ids where you see a difference, we can show you the raw_prompt_tokens/raw_completion_tokens from our side.

[07:47] Priya Desai: Okay, I can do that.

[07:50] Jordan Blake: Can we pivot to the dashboard for a second. Ben, you mentioned there’s a “cache hit rate” panel too?

[07:56] Ben Hartman: Yeah. It’s on the right, “Cache hit %” and “Cached prompt tokens”. It’s not super clear if that’s a rate of requests or tokens.

[08:06] Logan Wright: That’s a good callout. In v1, it’s token-based: cached prompt tokens divided by total prompt tokens. We’re adding a request-based hit rate as a separate metric, because it’s easier to reason about sometimes.

[08:22] Nadia Rahman: Also, the definition of “cache hit” can be partial. If the first 200 tokens hit but the rest is new, it still counts as a cache hit, but not 100% cached.

[08:33] Ben Hartman: That might be what we’re seeing, because our templates share the system prompt and first few messages, but the last message varies.

[08:42] Priya Desai: Exactly.

[08:44] Jordan Blake: So on the business side, you should see billed prompt tokens reduce proportional to cached prompt tokens. We can validate with a sample export.

[08:55] Sarah Kim: That’s what I want. Like, “show me a CSV with columns and I can do the math.”

[09:00] Jordan Blake: Great. We’ll do that.

[09:03] Priya Desai: One more thing. In the usage export we pulled last week, there was no cache field. Is that just not enabled.

[09:11] Logan Wright: Yeah. Those fields were behind a feature gate. We can enable it for your org. That will add columns; it won’t break existing parsing unless you’re strict about schema.

[09:24] Ben Hartman: We are strict.

[09:26] Jordan Blake: Okay, then we should version it. We can share a “v2 export schema” and you can switch when ready. In the API it’s additive fields, so older clients ignore them. But if you’re doing CSV ingestion with exact headers, we’ll coordinate.

[09:42] Sarah Kim: Please coordinate.

[09:45] Nadia Rahman: Also, there’s a distinction: usage API vs invoice. For POC you’re not getting invoices, but we’re aligning the usage API with what would be invoiced, meaning billed_tokens is what would map to charges.

[10:01] Priya Desai: Great.

[10:03] Ben Hartman: Can you confirm how region impacts pricing. Like if the dashboard says us-east sometimes, does that mean we got routed.

[10:11] Logan Wright: There is smart routing on capacity, but for POC we mostly keep you pinned to us-west unless we’re failing over. If you see another region, it might be either a fallback or a mis-tagged region dimension—there was a bug we fixed recently. But for your account, I can check.

[10:29] Jordan Blake: Logan, can you pull a seven-day report by region for them.

[10:33] Logan Wright: Yep.

[10:34] Priya Desai: We did see some “unknown” region values in export.

[10:38] Nadia Rahman: That’s exactly the missing dimension issue. It should be resolved, but there might be old events still.

[10:45] Sarah Kim: Old events as in last week.

[10:48] Logan Wright: Yeah, within the last couple weeks. We can backfill, but for your POC, we can just annotate it.

[10:56] Jordan Blake: Cool. Let’s talk about what you need to present internally. Priya, is it more performance or cost.

[11:03] Priya Desai: Both, but honestly the CFO question is “why is this line item unpredictable.” So the metering clarity is huge.

[11:12] Sarah Kim: Yes.

[11:13] Ben Hartman: We can handle the tokenization variance, but we need to understand what Redwood counts.

[11:19] Nadia Rahman: Then we should share a short dictionary:
- raw_prompt_tokens: tokenizer count for prompt
- raw_completion_tokens: tokenizer count for generated output
- billed_prompt_tokens: raw prompt tokens minus any cache-attributed tokens (or discounted, depending)
- billed_completion_tokens: typically equals raw completion tokens, minus de-duped retries
- cache_hit_prompt_tokens: portion of prompt tokens served from cache
- cache_hit: boolean if any portion of prompt was cache hit

[11:47] Priya Desai: Perfect. That’s what we need.

[11:50] Jordan Blake: Nadia, can you send that as an email after.

[11:53] Nadia Rahman: Yep.

[11:54] Ben Hartman: There’s still a question: do you ever bill for tokens generated during a server retry. Like if you generate 200 tokens, fail, retry, generate again.

[12:05] Logan Wright: We bill the successful request once, with de-dupe logic so internal attempts do not show up as multiple billed events. In the raw events, we may record attempts for debugging, but billing uses a stable idempotency key.

[12:20] Nadia Rahman: And for streaming, if the client disconnects, we’re working on making it consistent: do you bill only what was delivered, or what was generated. Current policy is bill what was delivered/confirmed, but it’s an area we’re tightening. That’s why you might see completion tokens 0 in some rare cases; we’d rather undercount than double count.

[12:40] Sarah Kim: Understood. That’s actually good.

[12:43] Priya Desai: We prefer that. The worst thing is arguing about phantom tokens.

[12:47] Jordan Blake: Exactly.

[12:49] Ben Hartman: Another small thing: the dashboard shows “Requests” but we have batched requests for embeddings in other systems. Not sure if that’s relevant here.

[12:58] Logan Wright: For chat/completions it’s 1 request equals 1 API call. For embeddings, if you send an array of inputs, we count it as 1 request but multiple “units” for embeddings. For your current POC you’re mostly chat, so not relevant.

[13:14] Priya Desai: Good.

[13:15] Jordan Blake: Let’s do a quick live readback. Ben, what’s the main concern you want closed by next week.

[13:21] Ben Hartman: I want a clear mapping between cache hit metrics and billed token reductions. If we can show one day’s worth of exports and compute it, we’re good.

[13:32] Priya Desai: And I want to confirm our internal “token per request” metrics aren’t wildly off because of tokenizer differences.

[13:40] Sarah Kim: And I want timezone alignment. The export day boundary is currently UTC, right.

[13:47] Logan Wright: Yes, it’s UTC by default.

[13:49] Sarah Kim: We operate on Pacific time. Otherwise daily charts look spiky.

[13:54] Jordan Blake: We’ve heard that before. We can either (a) provide timezone param in export, or (b) provide hourly exports and you roll up. Right now, easiest is hourly plus your rollup.

[14:09] Sarah Kim: Hourly is fine as long as it’s stable.

[14:12] Logan Wright: It’s stable, but there is still late arrival. We’ll note the freshness SLA.

[14:18] Nadia Rahman: And for month-end we can establish a “freeze” where after a certain time we stop backfilling without explicit approval. That’s more for paid contracts though.

[14:29] Sarah Kim: Good to know.

[14:31] Jordan Blake: We’ll keep it simple for the POC.

[14:34] Priya Desai: One more: are you planning to change these meter definitions soon. We don’t want the numbers shifting mid-test.

[14:43] Jordan Blake: Great question. The definitions are stabilizing. We’re adding fields, but we’re not changing how billed tokens are computed for you without notice. If there’s a meter fix, we’d run it in shadow mode first and tell you. Logan/Nadia can confirm.

[14:58] Logan Wright: Yep. Any change goes through change control, shadow compare, and a rollout window.

[15:05] Priya Desai: Great.

[15:07] Ben Hartman: Can you explain “shadow compare” quickly.

[15:10] Logan Wright: It means we compute both old and new meter outputs from the same raw request logs. We compare drift. If it’s within expected bounds, we can flip it. If it’s not, we treat it like a bug. But invoices and customer-facing totals stay on the old until approved.

[15:30] Ben Hartman: Nice.

[15:31] Jordan Blake: Okay. I think we covered the conceptual stuff. Let’s talk logistics: Nadia sends the dictionary, Logan pulls the last 7 days cache hit and region breakdown, Priya sends 3 request_ids.

[15:45] Priya Desai: Yep.

[15:46] Sarah Kim: And I’ll reply with timezone preferences.

[15:49] Jordan Blake: Perfect. Before we wrap, any competitor comparisons you’re doing. Just so we can help.

[15:56] Priya Desai: We’re also testing OpenAI and Bedrock. Mainly just price predictability.

[16:03] Ben Hartman: And Anthropic for one flow.

[16:05] Jordan Blake: Got it. We’ll keep the focus on predictability and auditability.

[16:10] (crosstalk)

[16:11] Logan Wright: One more thing—when we enable the new fields, you might see columns like billed_tokens_total. If your parser breaks, ping us. We can also give you JSON API instead of CSV.

[16:23] Ben Hartman: JSON is better.

[16:25] Sarah Kim: CSV is easier for me, but we can do both.

[16:29] Jordan Blake: We’ll do both.

[16:31] Priya Desai: Can we schedule that working session with Logan/Nadia. Like Tuesday.

[16:36] Jordan Blake: Yep, I’ll send a hold. Tuesday 10am PT.

[16:41] Nadia Rahman: Works.

[16:42] Logan Wright: Works.

[16:44] Jordan Blake: Great. Quick recap:
1) Redwood enables usage API fields for billed tokens + cache attribution.
2) Send a sample export and a field dictionary.
3) Northwind sends request_ids for token diffs.
4) We do a working session to validate cache hit math and answer streaming edge questions.

[17:05] Sarah Kim: Sounds good.

[17:07] Priya Desai: Thanks.

[17:08] Ben Hartman: Thanks, all.

[17:10] Jordan Blake: Cool, talk soon.

(end)

Notes
- Transcript may contain minor inaccuracies due to automated transcription.
- “Cache hit” discussed refers to Redwood-side prefix/KV caching attribution as represented in usage events and exports.

title: Zenith Health — Proof-of-Concept: Traffic & Token Profile Scoping
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-02-12 16:00 PST
Duration: 52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Shah (Redwood SE), Alicia Moreno (Zenith Head of Platform), Devon Park (Zenith ML Eng), Rita Singh (Zenith Security), Samir Patel (Zenith PM)

Summary:
Quick POC scoping to align on workload characterization (QPS, concurrency), token distributions, target SLAs for latency and cost, and an initial benchmark plan and timeline. Action items captured; Redwood to supply workload template and run initial analysis on logs.

Topics discussed:
- Workload mix and request shapes (short chat prompts vs long summarization)
- Concurrency and burst patterns (daily peaks, seasonality)
- Token input/output distributions and outlier handling
- Success criteria for POC: latency SLO, throughput target, quality/regression thresholds
- Security constraints: VPC, data retention, KMS
- Benchmark approach: dry run, controlled QPS ramp, measurement metrics

[00:00] Jordan: all right hey everyone thanks for joining, I think we have about 50 minutes, if folks can see the agenda in the calendar invite let me know.
[00:10] Alicia: yep, thanks Jordan, we got it. I'm Alicia, I run platform at Zenith. quick context — we're looking to validate a hosted vs dedicated tradeoff for our new patient-triage assistant. main concerns: latency under 200ms 90pct for short-turn prompts, and cost per thousand tokens.
[00:27] Jordan: got it. Priya from our infra/bench team is with me — she'll take the token/QPS stuff. Priya?
[00:33] Priya: hi everyone, thanks. so high level we want to map your actual request distribution — input token length, expected output length, QPS and concurrency — then propose a POC test that mimics production traffic. any sample logs you can share will speed this up.
[00:48] Devon: quick detour — our traffic is a mix. ~70% short intents (user asks 1-2 line question), 25% medium (200–600 char), 5% long (summaries / chart generation). Burstiness is real: clinic hours 8am–5pm PST, big jumps at appointment change windows. Typical sustained QPS is around 12 rps (requests per second) but bursts to 80 rps for 90–120s are possible.
[01:18] Priya: helpful. when you say 12 rps sustained is that per region or global?
[01:22] Devon: that's US-west only. we have another 3–4 rps in europe. all of this goes through an nginx proxy and then our API layer — we can export the upstream logs.
[01:31] Samir: we also have an offline batch job that runs nightly that spikes token output sizes for summarization, not latency sensitive but affects total token counts.
[01:40] Jordan: great intel. let's make sure the POC captures both the latency-sensitive path and the batch-heavy token volume.
[01:48] Rita: on security: we need SOC 2 evidence, SSO with SAML, and that the logs stay in our VPC — we prefer private deployment, but we can consider dedicated hosted with VPC peering if it meets encryption-at-rest requirements.
[02:05] Priya: understood. for the benching itself we'll need a staging environment that mirrors network egress; for private variants we can run the benchmark inside your VPC to avoid egress differences.
[02:15] Devon: question — how do you handle KV cache and prefix caching? sometimes users repeat prompts with minor edits, we'd like caching to kick in.
[02:25] Priya: we have automatic prefix and KV caching, but the effectiveness depends on prompt normalization. in our tests caching reduced token compute by 18–35% for sessioned chat patterns. sorry, the transcript might show "kay-vuh cache" — that's the audio.
[02:41] [laughter] Alicia: noted.
[02:45] Jordan: let's talk numbers. what distribution do you have for input tokens and output tokens? Devon?
[02:51] Devon: rough numbers — input median 12 tokens, 75th perc 48, 95th 320. outputs: median 40 tokens, 75th 160, 95th 900 (the summarizations).
[03:10] Priya: perfect — that spread means we need a mixed workload generator. we'll model three buckets: short (0–64 out), medium (65–300), long (>300). the POC should run a controlled QPS ramp per bucket.
[03:26] Alicia: and success criteria? we want: 90th percentile latency < 250ms for short paths; 95th < 800ms for medium; long ops are allowed to be slower up to 3s if they are non-blocking. plus cost per 1M tokens target — we can give cost constraints after we see Redwood's estimate.
[03:46] Jordan: I'll draft a set of measurable success criteria and share — e.g., percentiles, error rate < 0.5%, cost per token baseline, and quality checks.
[03:54] Devon: on quality checks — we want BLEU/ROUGE-ish for summarization? we typically have human-labeled prompts, 500 samples. can Redwood hook up evals?
[04:05] Priya: yes, we can run automated evaluations against your golden set. we'll also monitor hallucination rate via a keyword check and flag regressions relative to your baseline.
[04:18] [05:00] Jordan: timeline thoughts? customer provides logs and golden set by Feb 18, Redwood runs preliminary analysis and returns config + cost by Feb 19, schedule dry-run benchmark the week of Feb 23, full POC 1 week after sign-off. does that match expectations?
[05:18] Alicia: that sounds reasonable.
[05:22] Rita: re compliance — can you provide TTL for logs? we need log retention controls and proof that data in the bench is encrypted.
[05:30] Priya: yes — for dedicated/private we support customer-managed KMS and audit log exports. I'll attach the security checklist.
[05:38] Devon: for the workload template what columns do you need in the CSV? timestamp, input_tokens, output_tokens, response_ms, user_session_id?
[05:46] Priya: that's perfect. add endpoint, status_code, and whether prompt contains PII (we expect you to redact PII before sharing). if you can't add PII flags, at minimum indicate redaction was applied.
[05:58] Jordan: ok, sample CSV format will be in the template. also send a short runbook for how to run the generator locally so you can validate.
[06:06] Samir: benchmarks — will you do concurrency tests like 1,2,5,10,20 concurrent connections or QPS ramp?
[06:14] Priya: both. we do connection-level concurrency and QPS ramps. e.g., for your profile we'll simulate base 12 qps sustained for 30 mins, then bursts to 80 qps with 100ms jitter for 2 mins, repeated 6 times. we'll measure tail latencies, token throughput, GPU utilization, and cost projection.
[06:34] Devon: and if we want burst protection, how does Redwood autoscaling behave?
[06:38] Jordan: we have policy options — aggressive autoscale for latency SLO, or cost-conservative scaling with graceful fallback to a cheaper model variant. we'll show model routing options in the config.
[06:48] Rita: will the fallback models have the same safety/PII redaction? we can't accept degraded safety.
[06:54] Priya: safety filters are separate from model variant; fallback should never bypass safety checks. any fallback that changes redaction behavior is a no-go.
[07:02] Alicia: good. on measurement — what telemetry will you capture? we want token-level cost accounting and per-route dashboards.
[07:09] Priya: we'll capture per-request input/output tokens, response time, model variant used, GPU node id, and a cost estimate per sample that ties back to token compute and GPU occupancy. dashboards and traces will be provided in the Redwood Console.
[07:22] [07:50] Jordan: let's capture some edge cases — repeated retries, 429s, and partial responses. we recommend circuit-breaker policies and retry budgets. Devon, do you have retry behavior in your client?
[07:33] Devon: currently it's exponential backoff with 3 retries, but we can adjust per guidance.
[07:37] Priya: for the POC we suggest disabling client retries and letting the test harness handle retries to avoid skewing latency metrics.
[07:45] Samir: are you also measuring tokenization differences? we use a custom tokenization pipeline in some flows.
[07:51] Priya: we'll run tokenization parity tests — give us examples and we'll validate token counts against Redwood's tokenizer. differences will be flagged.
[07:58] Rita: security doc ask — can you share SOC 2 summary, encryption details, and the audit log format?
[08:04] Jordan: yes, we'll attach the security pack and the SOC 2 letter.
[08:09] Alicia: last question — success criteria on cost. do you have cost modeling we can look at?
[08:14] Jordan: we'll produce a per-token cost projection using your token distribution. sample output will include cost per 1M tokens and projected monthly cost given your traffic profile.
[08:22] Devon: ok. for the POC we want to hit 95% of the target throughput for short paths for at least 2 hours during the run with error rate < 0.5%.
[08:31] Priya: we can aim for that.
[08:34] Jordan: recap and next steps: Redwood sends template and security docs by Feb 16; Zenith provides logs and golden set by Feb 18; Redwood returns config/cost estimate by Feb 19; schedule dry run week of Feb 23.
[08:48] Alicia: agreed. thanks everyone.
[08:51] Jordan: thanks all — we'll follow up in email and Slack.

Notes/Artifacts referenced:
- POC template spreadsheet (will be shared): https://redwood.app/templates/zenith-poc-template.csv
- Benchmark config example deck: https://redwood.app/decks/zenith-benchmark-kickoff.pdf
- Security checklist: https://redwood.app/security/zenith-checklist.pdf

Action items (verbatim):
- Priya to run initial token distribution analysis after logs arrive (due 2026-02-19)
- Devon to export and redact upstream proxy logs and confirm redaction (due 2026-02-18)
- Jordan to draft POC success criteria and cost estimate (due 2026-02-16)

Transcript notes: occasional mis-transcriptions noted (e.g., "kay-vuh cache" for KV cache, "vee pee see" for VPC). Speaker attribution is best-effort; some short crossings and interruptions may be present.
