[05:38] Devon: for the workload template what columns do you need in the CSV? timestamp, input_tokens, output_tokens, response_ms, user_session_id?
[05:46] Priya: that's perfect. add endpoint, status_code, and whether prompt contains PII (we expect you to redact PII before sharing). if you can't add PII flags, at minimum indicate redaction was applied.
[05:58] Jordan: ok, sample CSV format will be in the template. also send a short runbook for how to run the generator locally so you can validate.
[06:06] Samir: benchmarks — will you do concurrency tests like 1,2,5,10,20 concurrent connections or QPS ramp?
[06:14] Priya: both. we do connection-level concurrency and QPS ramps. e.g., for your profile we'll simulate base 12 qps sustained for 30 mins, then bursts to 80 qps with 100ms jitter for 2 mins, repeated 6 times. we'll measure tail latencies, token throughput, GPU utilization, and cost projection.
[06:34] Devon: and if we want burst protection, how does Redwood autoscaling behave?
[06:38] Jordan: we have policy options — aggressive autoscale for latency SLO, or cost-conservative scaling with graceful fallback to a cheaper model variant. we'll show model routing options in the config.
[06:48] Rita: will the fallback models have the same safety/PII redaction? we can't accept degraded safety.
[06:54] Priya: safety filters are separate from model variant; fallback should never bypass safety checks. any fallback that changes redaction behavior is a no-go.
[07:02] Alicia: good. on measurement — what telemetry will you capture? we want token-level cost accounting and per-route dashboards.
[07:09] Priya: we'll capture per-request input/output tokens, response time, model variant used, GPU node id, and a cost estimate per sample that ties back to token compute and GPU occupancy. dashboards and traces will be provided in the Redwood Console.
[07:22] [07:50] Jordan: let's capture some edge cases — repeated retries, 429s, and partial responses. we recommend circuit-breaker policies and retry budgets. Devon, do you have retry behavior in your client?
[07:33] Devon: currently it's exponential backoff with 3 retries, but we can adjust per guidance.
[07:37] Priya: for the POC we suggest disabling client retries and letting the test harness handle retries to avoid skewing latency metrics.
[07:45] Samir: are you also measuring tokenization differences? we use a custom tokenization pipeline in some flows.
[07:51] Priya: we'll run tokenization parity tests — give us examples and we'll validate token counts against Redwood's tokenizer. differences will be flagged.
[07:58] Rita: security doc ask — can you share SOC 2 summary, encryption details, and the audit log format?
[08:04] Jordan: yes, we'll attach the security pack and the SOC 2 letter.
[08:09] Alicia: last question — success criteria on cost. do you have cost modeling we can look at?
[08:14] Jordan: we'll produce a per-token cost projection using your token distribution. sample output will include cost per 1M tokens and projected monthly cost given your traffic profile.
[08:22] Devon: ok. for the POC we want to hit 95% of the target throughput for short paths for at least 2 hours during the run with error rate < 0.5%.
[08:31] Priya: we can aim for that.
[08:34] Jordan: recap and next steps: Redwood sends template and security docs by Feb 16; Zenith provides logs and golden set by Feb 18; Redwood returns config/cost estimate by Feb 19; schedule dry run week of Feb 23.
[08:48] Alicia: agreed. thanks everyone.
[08:51] Jordan: thanks all — we'll follow up in email and Slack.

Notes/Artifacts referenced:
- POC template spreadsheet (will be shared): https://redwood.app/templates/zenith-poc-template.csv
- Benchmark config example deck: https://redwood.app/decks/zenith-benchmark-kickoff.pdf
- Security checklist: https://redwood.app/security/zenith-checklist.pdf

Action items (verbatim):
- Priya to run initial token distribution analysis after logs arrive (due 2026-02-19)
- Devon to export and redact upstream proxy logs and confirm redaction (due 2026-02-18)
- Jordan to draft POC success criteria and cost estimate (due 2026-02-16)

Transcript notes: occasional mis-transcriptions noted (e.g., "kay-vuh cache" for KV cache, "vee pee see" for VPC). Speaker attribution is best-effort; some short crossings and interruptions may be present.

title: AWS private offer terms and process (Redwood Inference)
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript

Title: AWS private offer terms and process (Redwood Inference)
Date: Feb 12, 2026
Start time: 4:00 PM UTC
Duration: 46 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
- Stephanie Nguyen (Redwood Inference)
- Jordan Blake (Redwood Inference)
- Laura Bennett (Redwood Inference)
- Sofia Mendes (Redwood Inference)
- Cole Summers (Redwood Inference)
- Ryan Patel (AWS Marketplace)
- Meghan O'Connor (AWS Marketplace)
- Ankit Rao (AWS)

---
Auto-summary (generated)
- Reviewed AWS Marketplace private offer flow and typical approval timeline.
- Discussed standard terms: effective date, acceptance window, refunds/credits, termination, and what can/can't be customized in Marketplace.
- Confirmed required inputs: buyer AWS account ID, product/offer type, dimensions, pricing schedule, contract duration, and legal docs.
- Flagged open items: discount guardrails, how to handle credits/refunds, and how Redwood should represent Dedicated/Private SKUs.

Topics
- Private offer mechanics (portal fields, acceptance, start date)
- Contract and consumption dimensions / metering
- Legal structure (EULA, addenda, order of precedence)
- Finance approvals and internal controls
- Timeline expectations and common failure modes

Questions captured
- Can Redwood include a separate MSA/DPA in a private offer, or only reference it?
- How should Redwood represent "Dedicated capacity" in Marketplace (contract dimension vs professional services vs usage)?
- What is the best practice for refund handling when usage is billed through AWS?

---
Transcript

[00:00] Stephanie Nguyen: Cool. All right, I think we're recording. Thanks everybody for making the time. This is Stephanie from Redwood Inference. Goal today is to get super crisp on the AWS private offer process—what we can standardize, what needs approvals, and honestly, where finance and legal get pulled in.

[00:18] Ryan Patel: Sounds good. Ryan here, AWS Marketplace partner ops. I have Meghan from deals desk and Ankit who covers SaaS contract mechanics.

[00:26] Jordan Blake: Jordan Blake, CRO at Redwood. Also on the line we have Laura from finance and Sofia from legal. Cole is here from our billing side.

[00:35] Meghan O'Connor: Hi everyone.

[00:36] Ankit Rao: Hey.

[00:40] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. Quick context: We’re refreshing our listing and SKUs. We have Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private deployment, plus compliance add-ons. We’re seeing more enterprise prospects where procurement says "must be AWS Marketplace" and then we end up scrambling on private offer terms.

[01:04] Ryan Patel: Yep, common.

[01:05] Stephanie Nguyen: So, first question: can we just walk the end-to-end? Like, what are the steps and typical timeline.

[01:12] Meghan O'Connor: Sure. For a private offer, you’ll need the buyer’s AWS account ID, the product, the offer type, pricing and dimensions, and then you submit through the management portal. Deals desk review varies—anywhere from 24 hours to, say, 5 business days depending on complexity and whether there are custom EULA changes.

[01:32] Stephanie Nguyen: When you say offer type, you're referring to…

[01:34] Ankit Rao: It’s basically whether you are doing SaaS Contract, SaaS Contract with Consumption, or pure consumption. For most SaaS vendors that want a committed spend plus overage, SaaS Contract with Consumption is the pattern.

[01:49] Cole Summers: Just to sanity check—"contract with consumption" allows a committed amount that draws down, and then metered overage billed monthly, right?

[01:58] Ankit Rao: Yes. There are nuances in how you represent the commit—some folks do it as an upfront fee, some do it as a monthly schedule. But the key is: contract portion is billed per the schedule, consumption portion is metered through the Marketplace metering service.

[02:14] Laura Bennett: And from a buyer standpoint, they see one line item on AWS invoice?

[02:19] Meghan O'Connor: They typically see the contract charges and usage charges, but yes, on the AWS invoice. Depending on their consolidated billing, it can show per linked account.

[02:30] Jordan Blake: Our internal worry is mismatches between how we meter and how AWS expects the dimension. We had an incident recently where a dimension name drifted.

[02:39] Cole Summers: Yeah, we found "token_units" versus "tokens" in one environment. Not proud.

[02:45] Ryan Patel: That’s a common reason for delays. If dimension names don’t line up with the listing configuration, it gets flagged.

[02:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Ok. On the private offer specifics—what fields cause the most back-and-forth?

[03:01] Meghan O'Connor: Buyer account ID missing or wrong. Start date and end date confusion. And then any custom language in the seller legal terms or EULA.

[03:12] Sofia Mendes: Can you clarify what "custom language" means here? Are we allowed to add, like, an addendum with DPA terms?

[03:20] Ankit Rao: In the Marketplace flow, the primary legal document is the EULA associated with the product. For private offers, you can attach a custom EULA for that offer, but it needs to be reviewed. Separate external agreements—some sellers do it, but AWS doesn't enforce it. It's between you and the buyer.

[03:40] Sofia Mendes: Right, so the offer can reference "Customer is also subject to Redwood MSA" but AWS isn't guaranteeing signature.

[03:46] Ankit Rao: Correct.

[03:49] Jordan Blake: For enterprise deals we usually have an order form plus an MSA. Marketplace makes that messy.

[03:55] Meghan O'Connor: The clean pattern is: keep the Marketplace legal terms as the controlling document for the transaction, and then handle DPA/security addenda off-platform if needed. But don’t put conflicting terms.

[04:10] Stephanie Nguyen: So how do vendors usually handle termination, refunds, credits? Our finance team will ask.

[04:18] Laura Bennett: I am asking. (laughs)

[04:21] Ankit Rao: Generally AWS Marketplace transactions are non-cancelable and non-refundable unless you explicitly allow it. For private offers, you can include custom refund policies in the legal terms, but again—be cautious. Also, for consumption charges, refunds are complicated; it becomes a credit memo process through AWS.

[04:44] Laura Bennett: When you say credit memo, is that us issuing it or AWS?

[04:48] Meghan O'Connor: It's an AWS process but initiated by the seller through support. You’d provide justification, and it routes for approval.

[04:56] Laura Bennett: That’s important. We need to know internal approval steps on our side.

[05:02] Stephanie Nguyen: Another one: acceptance window. If we send a private offer, how long is it valid?

[05:08] Ryan Patel: You set the expiration. Commonly 30 days, sometimes 60. But shorter is better to avoid pricing being stale.

[05:17] Jordan Blake: Okay. We’re trying to standardize. Like, "30-day acceptance window, start date on acceptance".

[05:24] Ankit Rao: Start date can be immediate or set. For SaaS contract with consumption, folks often do "effective upon acceptance" but if you need time to provision, you can set start date a week out.

[05:39] Cole Summers: Provisioning is pretty instant for Hosted, but Dedicated and Private might need scheduling.

[05:45] Stephanie Nguyen: That’s the crux. Dedicated capacity is not just flipping a bit.

[05:49] Ryan Patel: Yep. For capacity-style products, some sellers keep it off Marketplace and only bill through contract. But if you want it in Marketplace, you represent it as a contract dimension, like "capacity units" per month.

[06:05] Jordan Blake: We want buyers to procure Dedicated and Private through marketplace. So we likely need contract dimensions that map to internal reservation SKUs.

[06:15] Cole Summers: Our internal meter is "gpu_reservation_hour" for Dedicated, and "private_platform_fee" monthly for Private. For Hosted it’s tokens.

[06:24] Ankit Rao: That can work. AWS doesn’t care what you call it as long as it’s clear and consistent. But the buyer needs to understand what a "reservation hour" means.

[06:35] Stephanie Nguyen: Right, we’ll translate to something like "Dedicated GPU capacity (monthly)".

[06:39] Meghan O'Connor: And avoid too many dimensions. Deals desk gets nervous when there’s like 12.

[06:47] Sofia Mendes: On legal, I want to confirm: order of precedence. If we have Marketplace terms plus our support policy, what wins?

[06:56] Ankit Rao: Typically the Marketplace EULA + private offer terms win for that transaction. If you reference external docs, make sure you state whether they are incorporated by reference and the precedence.

[07:12] Sofia Mendes: Okay, so we can include language like "In the event of conflict, private offer terms control".

[07:18] Meghan O'Connor: Yes, but please keep it readable. Too much legalese triggers review loops.

[07:25] Jordan Blake: We'll try. (laughs)

[07:27] Stephanie Nguyen: Switching to approval workflow: internally at AWS, who approves? Is it automated or manual?

[07:33] Ryan Patel: For standard pricing with standard EULA, it can be pretty quick. Once you start customizing the EULA, it’s manual review. Also, large discounts sometimes trigger additional checks.

[07:47] Laura Bennett: What counts as "large"?

[07:49] Ryan Patel: There's no published threshold, but if it's extreme relative to public price, it gets questions.

[07:56] Laura Bennett: Got it. We'll set guardrails.

[08:01] Stephanie Nguyen: For private offers, do we need to upload attachments? Like, can we attach a security packet PDF?

[08:09] Meghan O'Connor: In the portal you can add notes and link out. Attachments are limited. Some sellers host docs and reference them.

[08:18] Jordan Blake: We're publishing a marketplace-ready security bundle on our docs site, so linking should be fine.

[08:23] Sofia Mendes: As long as it's stable URLs.

[08:27] Cole Summers: We can do stable URLs. (hopefully)

[08:31] Stephanie Nguyen: Let’s talk about standard terms we want across offers. Ryan/Meghan, what's "normal"?

[08:38] Ryan Patel: Common patterns:
- 12-month term
- Billing monthly or upfront
- Non-cancelable
- Support included at a defined tier
- Offer expires in 30 days
- Usage measured via defined dimensions

[08:54] Stephanie Nguyen: And for contract with consumption, can the contract portion be a commit that draws down against usage? Or is it like a platform fee separate from usage?

[09:04] Ankit Rao: AWS doesn't natively do "drawdown" like a bank. The contract portion is its own charge. Some vendors conceptually treat it as commit and then set usage price to $0 until commit is reached, but that’s complicated and not typical.

[09:20] Cole Summers: Yeah, we can’t do $0 tokens until commit. Our billing expects per-token always.

[09:25] Jordan Blake: So simplest is: contract is a platform fee or reserved capacity fee, and consumption is overage or standard usage.

[09:34] Ankit Rao: Exactly.

[09:36] Laura Bennett: From rev rec perspective, we prefer monthly schedule with fixed fees and then variable usage.

[09:43] Stephanie Nguyen: So we’ll standardize monthly.

[09:46] Meghan O'Connor: Monthly is fine.

[09:49] Stephanie Nguyen: Another question we've had in deals: buyer asks "can we start the contract, but delay usage until we finish security review". Is that normal?

[09:59] Ryan Patel: It happens. You can set a future start date. But be careful; if you set it too far out, the offer might expire or get stale.

[10:10] Jordan Blake: Also, we need to provision entitlements. If they accept, our system should recognize it.

[10:17] Cole Summers: Our entitlement sync job runs every few minutes, but sometimes AWS propagation is delayed. We’re hardening retries.

[10:27] Ryan Patel: Good. If you’re seeing delays, you can also instruct the buyer to wait up to an hour. But that’s… not great.

[10:34] Stephanie Nguyen: Yeah, we're trying to remove that from the FAQ.

[10:38] Sofia Mendes: Coming back to credits. If we want to offer "migration credits" or "trial credits" via private offer, how is that reflected?

[10:46] Meghan O'Connor: Usually it's just discounted pricing for a period, like first month discounted. Credits as a concept are not a standard Marketplace mechanism.

[10:56] Laura Bennett: So no "$10k credit" line.

[10:58] Meghan O'Connor: Not as a credit line item. But you can structure pricing to net out similarly.

[11:04] Jordan Blake: Makes sense.

[11:07] Stephanie Nguyen: For Dedicated and Private, we anticipate private offers that include both contract and usage. Example: "Private deployment platform fee" plus "Hosted tokens for overflow". Is mixing dimensions okay?

[11:21] Ankit Rao: Yes, but keep in mind the buyer’s procurement team needs a clear narrative. Too many moving pieces and they get confused.

[11:31] Cole Summers: Also we need to ensure the metering integration is correct per dimension.

[11:36] Stephanie Nguyen: Cole, can you send final dimension names? We’re locking SKU catalog v2.

[11:42] Cole Summers: Yes. I'll send the list today. It’s basically: hosted_tokens, embedding_units, dedicated_capacity_month, private_platform_month, and then compliance_addon_month.

[11:55] Laura Bennett: Are we charging compliance add-on through Marketplace too? That’s the idea?

[11:59] Jordan Blake: Buyers ask for it as part of procurement, yes.

[12:02] Ryan Patel: You can. But, again, if it’s an add-on, make sure it’s clearly described.

[12:09] Stephanie Nguyen: Cool. Now, internal approval workflow on our side: We want a process so AE isn't inventing terms. What do you see other sellers do?

[12:18] Meghan O'Connor: Usually: deal desk or finance has a template, AEs fill in variables (buyer ID, price, term), legal only reviews if there are exceptions.

[12:29] Laura Bennett: That’s what I want. A template with allowed ranges.

[12:33] Sofia Mendes: And a clause library: refunds/no refunds, termination, SLA reference.

[12:40] Jordan Blake: We'll do that.

[12:42] Stephanie Nguyen: Ryan, question: If we submit a private offer with standard terms and pricing, and then buyer requests a small change, do we edit the offer or create a new one?

[12:53] Ryan Patel: Generally you create a new private offer. Edits after submission can be messy.

[12:59] Stephanie Nguyen: So versioning is new offer each time.

[13:02] Ryan Patel: Exactly.

[13:04] Cole Summers: Is there an API? Or only portal?

[13:08] Ankit Rao: Mostly portal. Some partners have automation, but I wouldn’t rely on it.

[13:15] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay.

[13:17] Jordan Blake: Let’s talk about the "standard" private offer notes. We got feedback from AWS on wording—no unsupported security claims, no "guaranteed" language.

[13:28] Ryan Patel: Yep. Also avoid comparing to other vendors in the offer text.

[13:33] Stephanie Nguyen: Understood.

[13:35] Sofia Mendes: On SLAs: can we reference an SLA doc and say "SLA applies"?

[13:40] Meghan O'Connor: You can reference your support policy. But AWS doesn’t enforce it; it’s your obligation. Just don’t imply AWS is providing the SLA.

[13:51] Jordan Blake: Right.

[13:52] Laura Bennett: One more finance thing: payment terms. In Marketplace it's basically AWS terms, correct? Net 30, etc.

[14:00] Meghan O'Connor: Correct. Buyer pays AWS; AWS pays you on the standard seller disbursement schedule.

[14:06] Laura Bennett: So we can't do net 60 as a concession.

[14:09] Meghan O'Connor: Not really. Marketplace is standardized.

[14:12] Laura Bennett: That's good actually.

[14:15] Stephanie Nguyen: How about co-terming? Buyers sometimes want co-term with an existing Marketplace contract.

[14:22] Ankit Rao: You can set a custom end date to align with their other contracts, but it can get tricky if you’re also changing price schedules.

[14:33] Ryan Patel: Deals desk can help but it may extend review.

[14:36] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay.

[14:38] Jordan Blake: We also hear "We have EDP with AWS, does this count?".

[14:43] Ankit Rao: Many customers prefer Marketplace because it counts toward their AWS spend commitment. It depends on their EDP terms, but generally Marketplace purchases do count.

[14:53] Jordan Blake: Great. That’s a selling point.

[14:56] Stephanie Nguyen: Now, open items: (1) refund/credit policy, (2) early termination for non-performance, (3) data processing and security docs references.

[15:06] Sofia Mendes: For early termination, we usually allow termination for material breach. But Marketplace says non-cancelable, right?

[15:14] Ankit Rao: "Non-cancelable" refers to convenience cancellation. Material breach is separate; you can still have legal remedies. But don’t create a term that suggests the buyer can cancel anytime and get refunded.

[15:28] Sofia Mendes: Understood.

[15:31] Laura Bennett: And refunds—our default is no refunds, but we sometimes offer credits.

[15:37] Meghan O'Connor: Credits are easier than refunds, but still involve AWS support tickets.

[15:44] Cole Summers: Also, our internal system can apply service credits directly, but if AWS is billing, it's misaligned.

[15:51] Jordan Blake: Right. We need one consistent story.

[15:55] Stephanie Nguyen: Ryan, can you confirm what support channel we use to request a credit, and how long it takes?

[16:02] Ryan Patel: It’s through AWS Marketplace seller support. Typical turnaround is a few business days, but depends.

[16:10] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay.

[16:11] Jordan Blake: Another thing: for Private deployments, we might have a separate SOW for implementation. Can that be included?

[16:19] Ankit Rao: Professional services can be sold through Marketplace in some cases, but it's a different product type. Many sellers keep SOW separate.

[16:28] Sofia Mendes: We prefer separate SOW anyway.

[16:31] Stephanie Nguyen: So private offer covers subscription/platform fee, and implementation is off-platform.

[16:37] Ryan Patel: That’s common.

[16:40] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. Let's get tactical: if we want to send a private offer next week, what do you need from us?

[16:48] Meghan O'Connor: Buyer account ID, which product listing, the dimensions and pricing, start date, term length, and whether you want custom EULA.

[17:00] Stephanie Nguyen: We'll do standard EULA wherever possible.

[17:03] Sofia Mendes: We'll provide a standard private offer note block too.

[17:07] Jordan Blake: And finance guardrails.

[17:10] Laura Bennett: Yep.

[17:12] Cole Summers: And I’ll provide dimension IDs.

[17:15] Ryan Patel: Perfect.

[17:17] Stephanie Nguyen: Can we talk about typical review gotchas so we avoid resubmissions?

[17:24] Ryan Patel: Biggest ones:
- Pricing doesn’t match the dimension definitions
- Ambiguous product description (what is included)
- Using "unlimited" language
- Stating security certifications incorrectly

[17:38] Jordan Blake: We have SOC 2 Type II, but we don’t want to over-claim.

[17:42] Ryan Patel: Exactly—say what you have and keep it factual.

[17:47] Stephanie Nguyen: For Dedicated, we sometimes say "SLA-backed". Is that okay?

[17:53] Meghan O'Connor: It’s okay if you have an SLA doc and it’s your SLA, not AWS.

[17:58] Stephanie Nguyen: Got it.

[18:01] Laura Bennett: What about price changes mid-term? Like if token price changes in public listing.

[18:07] Ankit Rao: Private offer pricing is locked for that buyer for the term, for the dimensions in the offer.

[18:14] Laura Bennett: Good.

[18:16] Jordan Blake: That’s why buyers like private offers.

[18:18] Stephanie Nguyen: We should also cover channel partner private offers—CPPO. Do we need that now?

[18:26] Ryan Patel: Only if a partner/reseller wants to extend the offer. If you’re selling direct to the end customer on Marketplace, standard private offer is fine.

[18:38] Stephanie Nguyen: Ok, not now.

[18:40] Jordan Blake: Let’s land the process in a checklist we can use internally.

[18:45] Stephanie Nguyen: Yep. We'll do a template.

[18:48] Sofia Mendes: Last question from legal: governing law. In our MSA we specify Delaware or California. In Marketplace, what happens?

[18:58] Ankit Rao: Your EULA governs. AWS doesn't impose a governing law, but your document might. Just keep consistent.

[19:06] Sofia Mendes: Okay.

[19:08] Stephanie Nguyen: We’re at time, but I want to recap next steps.

[19:12] Jordan Blake: Before recap—AWS, any recommendation for how we name the offers? We have naming constraints across portals.

[19:20] Meghan O'Connor: Keep it simple: "Redwood Inference - Private Offer - [Customer Name] - [Date]".

[19:28] Stephanie Nguyen: Great.

[19:30] Stephanie Nguyen: Recap:
1) We should use SaaS Contract with Consumption for most enterprise deals with commit + usage.
2) Keep EULA standard; minimize custom legal.
3) Avoid refunds language; prefer discounts; if credits are needed, understand AWS credit memo workflow.
4) Provide buyer account ID, dimensions, pricing schedule, term, start date, and expiration.

[19:55] Stephanie Nguyen: Action items—Laura will confirm discount guardrails and min commits. Sofia will draft standardized private offer legal notes and get sign-off. Cole will send final dimension list/IDs. Ryan will confirm whether we can attach or only link to security packet and SLA docs.

[20:13] Ryan Patel: Yep, I’ll check and send.

[20:15] Meghan O'Connor: Works.

[20:17] Ankit Rao: Sounds good.

[20:18] Jordan Blake: Thanks everyone.

[20:20] Stephanie Nguyen: Thanks. We'll schedule a quick follow-up once we have the template drafted.

[20:26] Meeting ended.

(Note: Fireflies transcript may omit some portions after 20:26 due to early stop / calendar disconnect. Total recording length shown in header includes pre-join audio and post-call buffer.)

[00:00] Stephanie Nguyen: Cool. All right, I think we're recording. Thanks everybody for making the time. This is Stephanie from Redwood Inference. Goal today is to get super crisp on the AWS private offer process—what we can standardize, what needs approvals, and honestly, where finance and legal get pulled in.

[00:26] Jordan Blake: Jordan Blake, CRO at Redwood. Also on the line we have Laura from finance and Sofia from legal. Cole is here from our billing side.

[00:35] Meghan O'Connor: Hi everyone.

[00:36] Ankit Rao: Hey.

[00:40] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. Quick context: We’re refreshing our listing and SKUs. We have Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private deployment, plus compliance add-ons. We’re seeing more enterprise prospects where procurement says "must be AWS Marketplace" and then we end up scrambling on private offer terms.

[01:04] Ryan Patel: Yep, common.

[01:05] Stephanie Nguyen: So, first question: can we just walk the end-to-end? Like, what are the steps and typical timeline.

[01:12] Meghan O'Connor: Sure. For a private offer, you’ll need the buyer’s AWS account ID, the product, the offer type, pricing and dimensions, and then you submit through the management portal. Deals desk review varies—anywhere from 24 hours to, say, 5 business days depending on complexity and whether there are custom EULA changes.

[01:32] Stephanie Nguyen: When you say offer type, you're referring to…

[01:34] Ankit Rao: It’s basically whether you are doing SaaS Contract, SaaS Contract with Consumption, or pure consumption. For most SaaS vendors that want a committed spend plus overage, SaaS Contract with Consumption is the pattern.

[01:49] Cole Summers: Just to sanity check—"contract with consumption" allows a committed amount that draws down, and then metered overage billed monthly, right?

[01:58] Ankit Rao: Yes. There are nuances in how you represent the commit—some folks do it as an upfront fee, some do it as a monthly schedule. But the key is: contract portion is billed per the schedule, consumption portion is metered through the Marketplace metering service.

[02:14] Laura Bennett: And from a buyer standpoint, they see one line item on AWS invoice?

[02:19] Meghan O'Connor: They typically see the contract charges and usage charges, but yes, on the AWS invoice. Depending on their consolidated billing, it can show per linked account.

[02:30] Jordan Blake: Our internal worry is mismatches between how we meter and how AWS expects the dimension. We had an incident recently where a dimension name drifted.

[02:39] Cole Summers: Yeah, we found "token_units" versus "tokens" in one environment. Not proud.

[02:45] Ryan Patel: That’s a common reason for delays. If dimension names don’t line up with the listing configuration, it gets flagged.

[02:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Ok. On the private offer specifics—what fields cause the most back-and-forth?

[03:01] Meghan O'Connor: Buyer account ID missing or wrong. Start date and end date confusion. And then any custom language in the seller legal terms or EULA.

[03:12] Sofia Mendes: Can you clarify what "custom language" means here? Are we allowed to add, like, an addendum with DPA terms?

[03:20] Ankit Rao: In the Marketplace flow, the primary legal document is the EULA associated with the product. For private offers, you can attach a custom EULA for that offer, but it needs to be reviewed. Separate external agreements—some sellers do it, but AWS doesn't enforce it. It's between you and the buyer.

[03:40] Sofia Mendes: Right, so the offer can reference "Customer is also subject to Redwood MSA" but AWS isn't guaranteeing signature.

[03:46] Ankit Rao: Correct.

[03:49] Jordan Blake: For enterprise deals we usually have an order form plus an MSA. Marketplace makes that messy.

[03:55] Meghan O'Connor: The clean pattern is: keep the Marketplace legal terms as the controlling document for the transaction, and then handle DPA/security addenda off-platform if needed. But don’t put conflicting terms.

[04:10] Stephanie Nguyen: So how do vendors usually handle termination, refunds, credits? Our finance team will ask.

[04:18] Laura Bennett: I am asking. (laughs)

[04:21] Ankit Rao: Generally AWS Marketplace transactions are non-cancelable and non-refundable unless you explicitly allow it. For private offers, you can include custom refund policies in the legal terms, but again—be cautious. Also, for consumption charges, refunds are complicated; it becomes a credit memo process through AWS.

[04:44] Laura Bennett: When you say credit memo, is that us issuing it or AWS?

[04:48] Meghan O'Connor: It's an AWS process but initiated by the seller through support. You’d provide justification, and it routes for approval.

[04:56] Laura Bennett: That’s important. We need to know internal approval steps on our side.

[05:02] Stephanie Nguyen: Another one: acceptance window. If we send a private offer, how long is it valid?

[05:08] Ryan Patel: You set the expiration. Commonly 30 days, sometimes 60. But shorter is better to avoid pricing being stale.

[05:17] Jordan Blake: Okay. We’re trying to standardize. Like, "30-day acceptance window, start date on acceptance".

[05:24] Ankit Rao: Start date can be immediate or set. For SaaS contract with consumption, folks often do "effective upon acceptance" but if you need time to provision, you can set start date a week out.

[05:39] Cole Summers: Provisioning is pretty instant for Hosted, but Dedicated and Private might need scheduling.

[05:45] Stephanie Nguyen: That’s the crux. Dedicated capacity is not just flipping a bit.

[05:49] Ryan Patel: Yep. For capacity-style products, some sellers keep it off Marketplace and only bill through contract. But if you want it in Marketplace, you represent it as a contract dimension, like "capacity units" per month.

[06:05] Jordan Blake: We want buyers to procure Dedicated and Private through marketplace. So we likely need contract dimensions that map to internal reservation SKUs.

[06:15] Cole Summers: Our internal meter is "gpu_reservation_hour" for Dedicated, and "private_platform_fee" monthly for Private. For Hosted it’s tokens.

[06:24] Ankit Rao: That can work. AWS doesn’t care what you call it as long as it’s clear and consistent. But the buyer needs to understand what a "reservation hour" means.

[06:35] Stephanie Nguyen: Right, we’ll translate to something like "Dedicated GPU capacity (monthly)".

[06:39] Meghan O'Connor: And avoid too many dimensions. Deals desk gets nervous when there’s like 12.

[06:47] Sofia Mendes: On legal, I want to confirm: order of precedence. If we have Marketplace terms plus our support policy, what wins?

[06:56] Ankit Rao: Typically the Marketplace EULA + private offer terms win for that transaction. If you reference external docs, make sure you state whether they are incorporated by reference and the precedence.

[07:12] Sofia Mendes: Okay, so we can include language like "In the event of conflict, private offer terms control".

[07:18] Meghan O'Connor: Yes, but please keep it readable. Too much legalese triggers review loops.

[07:25] Jordan Blake: We'll try. (laughs)

[07:27] Stephanie Nguyen: Switching to approval workflow: internally at AWS, who approves? Is it automated or manual?

[07:33] Ryan Patel: For standard pricing with standard EULA, it can be pretty quick. Once you start customizing the EULA, it’s manual review. Also, large discounts sometimes trigger additional checks.

[07:47] Laura Bennett: What counts as "large"?

[07:49] Ryan Patel: There's no published threshold, but if it's extreme relative to public price, it gets questions.

[07:56] Laura Bennett: Got it. We'll set guardrails.

[08:01] Stephanie Nguyen: For private offers, do we need to upload attachments? Like, can we attach a security packet PDF?

[08:09] Meghan O'Connor: In the portal you can add notes and link out. Attachments are limited. Some sellers host docs and reference them.

[08:18] Jordan Blake: We're publishing a marketplace-ready security bundle on our docs site, so linking should be fine.

[08:23] Sofia Mendes: As long as it's stable URLs.

[08:27] Cole Summers: We can do stable URLs. (hopefully)

[08:31] Stephanie Nguyen: Let’s talk about standard terms we want across offers. Ryan/Meghan, what's "normal"?

[08:38] Ryan Patel: Common patterns:
- 12-month term
- Billing monthly or upfront
- Non-cancelable
- Support included at a defined tier
- Offer expires in 30 days
- Usage measured via defined dimensions

[08:54] Stephanie Nguyen: And for contract with consumption, can the contract portion be a commit that draws down against usage? Or is it like a platform fee separate from usage?

[09:04] Ankit Rao: AWS doesn't natively do "drawdown" like a bank. The contract portion is its own charge. Some vendors conceptually treat it as commit and then set usage price to $0 until commit is reached, but that’s complicated and not typical.

[09:20] Cole Summers: Yeah, we can’t do $0 tokens until commit. Our billing expects per-token always.

[09:25] Jordan Blake: So simplest is: contract is a platform fee or reserved capacity fee, and consumption is overage or standard usage.

[09:34] Ankit Rao: Exactly.

[09:36] Laura Bennett: From rev rec perspective, we prefer monthly schedule with fixed fees and then variable usage.

[09:43] Stephanie Nguyen: So we’ll standardize monthly.

[09:46] Meghan O'Connor: Monthly is fine.

[09:49] Stephanie Nguyen: Another question we've had in deals: buyer asks "can we start the contract, but delay usage until we finish security review". Is that normal?

[09:59] Ryan Patel: It happens. You can set a future start date. But be careful; if you set it too far out, the offer might expire or get stale.

[10:10] Jordan Blake: Also, we need to provision entitlements. If they accept, our system should recognize it.

[10:17] Cole Summers: Our entitlement sync job runs every few minutes, but sometimes AWS propagation is delayed. We’re hardening retries.

[10:27] Ryan Patel: Good. If you’re seeing delays, you can also instruct the buyer to wait up to an hour. But that’s… not great.

[10:34] Stephanie Nguyen: Yeah, we're trying to remove that from the FAQ.

[10:38] Sofia Mendes: Coming back to credits. If we want to offer "migration credits" or "trial credits" via private offer, how is that reflected?

[10:46] Meghan O'Connor: Usually it's just discounted pricing for a period, like first month discounted. Credits as a concept are not a standard Marketplace mechanism.

[10:56] Laura Bennett: So no "$10k credit" line.

[10:58] Meghan O'Connor: Not as a credit line item. But you can structure pricing to net out similarly.

[11:04] Jordan Blake: Makes sense.

[11:07] Stephanie Nguyen: For Dedicated and Private, we anticipate private offers that include both contract and usage. Example: "Private deployment platform fee" plus "Hosted tokens for overflow". Is mixing dimensions okay?

[11:21] Ankit Rao: Yes, but keep in mind the buyer’s procurement team needs a clear narrative. Too many moving pieces and they get confused.

[11:31] Cole Summers: Also we need to ensure the metering integration is correct per dimension.

[11:36] Stephanie Nguyen: Cole, can you send final dimension names? We’re locking SKU catalog v2.

[11:42] Cole Summers: Yes. I'll send the list today. It’s basically: hosted_tokens, embedding_units, dedicated_capacity_month, private_platform_month, and then compliance_addon_month.

[11:55] Laura Bennett: Are we charging compliance add-on through Marketplace too? That’s the idea?

[11:59] Jordan Blake: Buyers ask for it as part of procurement, yes.

[12:02] Ryan Patel: You can. But, again, if it’s an add-on, make sure it’s clearly described.

[12:09] Stephanie Nguyen: Cool. Now, internal approval workflow on our side: We want a process so AE isn't inventing terms. What do you see other sellers do?

[12:18] Meghan O'Connor: Usually: deal desk or finance has a template, AEs fill in variables (buyer ID, price, term), legal only reviews if there are exceptions.

[12:29] Laura Bennett: That’s what I want. A template with allowed ranges.

[12:33] Sofia Mendes: And a clause library: refunds/no refunds, termination, SLA reference.

[12:40] Jordan Blake: We'll do that.

[12:42] Stephanie Nguyen: Ryan, question: If we submit a private offer with standard terms and pricing, and then buyer requests a small change, do we edit the offer or create a new one?

[12:53] Ryan Patel: Generally you create a new private offer. Edits after submission can be messy.

[12:59] Stephanie Nguyen: So versioning is new offer each time.

[13:02] Ryan Patel: Exactly.

[13:04] Cole Summers: Is there an API? Or only portal?

[13:08] Ankit Rao: Mostly portal. Some partners have automation, but I wouldn’t rely on it.

[13:15] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay.

[13:17] Jordan Blake: Let’s talk about the "standard" private offer notes. We got feedback from AWS on wording—no unsupported security claims, no "guaranteed" language.

[13:28] Ryan Patel: Yep. Also avoid comparing to other vendors in the offer text.

[13:33] Stephanie Nguyen: Understood.

[13:35] Sofia Mendes: On SLAs: can we reference an SLA doc and say "SLA applies"?

[13:40] Meghan O'Connor: You can reference your support policy. But AWS doesn’t enforce it; it’s your obligation. Just don’t imply AWS is providing the SLA.

[13:51] Jordan Blake: Right.

[13:52] Laura Bennett: One more finance thing: payment terms. In Marketplace it's basically AWS terms, correct? Net 30, etc.

[14:00] Meghan O'Connor: Correct. Buyer pays AWS; AWS pays you on the standard seller disbursement schedule.

[14:06] Laura Bennett: So we can't do net 60 as a concession.

[14:09] Meghan O'Connor: Not really. Marketplace is standardized.

[14:12] Laura Bennett: That's good actually.

[14:15] Stephanie Nguyen: How about co-terming? Buyers sometimes want co-term with an existing Marketplace contract.

[14:22] Ankit Rao: You can set a custom end date to align with their other contracts, but it can get tricky if you’re also changing price schedules.

[14:33] Ryan Patel: Deals desk can help but it may extend review.

[14:36] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay.

[14:38] Jordan Blake: We also hear "We have EDP with AWS, does this count?".

[14:43] Ankit Rao: Many customers prefer Marketplace because it counts toward their AWS spend commitment. It depends on their EDP terms, but generally Marketplace purchases do count.

[14:53] Jordan Blake: Great. That’s a selling point.

[14:56] Stephanie Nguyen: Now, open items: (1) refund/credit policy, (2) early termination for non-performance, (3) data processing and security docs references.

[15:06] Sofia Mendes: For early termination, we usually allow termination for material breach. But Marketplace says non-cancelable, right?

[15:14] Ankit Rao: "Non-cancelable" refers to convenience cancellation. Material breach is separate; you can still have legal remedies. But don’t create a term that suggests the buyer can cancel anytime and get refunded.

[15:28] Sofia Mendes: Understood.

[15:31] Laura Bennett: And refunds—our default is no refunds, but we sometimes offer credits.

[15:37] Meghan O'Connor: Credits are easier than refunds, but still involve AWS support tickets.

[15:44] Cole Summers: Also, our internal system can apply service credits directly, but if AWS is billing, it's misaligned.

[15:51] Jordan Blake: Right. We need one consistent story.

[15:55] Stephanie Nguyen: Ryan, can you confirm what support channel we use to request a credit, and how long it takes?

[16:02] Ryan Patel: It’s through AWS Marketplace seller support. Typical turnaround is a few business days, but depends.

[16:10] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay.

[16:11] Jordan Blake: Another thing: for Private deployments, we might have a separate SOW for implementation. Can that be included?

[16:19] Ankit Rao: Professional services can be sold through Marketplace in some cases, but it's a different product type. Many sellers keep SOW separate.

[16:28] Sofia Mendes: We prefer separate SOW anyway.

[16:31] Stephanie Nguyen: So private offer covers subscription/platform fee, and implementation is off-platform.

[16:37] Ryan Patel: That’s common.

[16:40] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. Let's get tactical: if we want to send a private offer next week, what do you need from us?

[16:48] Meghan O'Connor: Buyer account ID, which product listing, the dimensions and pricing, start date, term length, and whether you want custom EULA.

[17:00] Stephanie Nguyen: We'll do standard EULA wherever possible.

[17:03] Sofia Mendes: We'll provide a standard private offer note block too.

[17:07] Jordan Blake: And finance guardrails.

[17:10] Laura Bennett: Yep.

[17:12] Cole Summers: And I’ll provide dimension IDs.

[17:15] Ryan Patel: Perfect.

[17:17] Stephanie Nguyen: Can we talk about typical review gotchas so we avoid resubmissions?

[17:24] Ryan Patel: Biggest ones:
- Pricing doesn’t match the dimension definitions
- Ambiguous product description (what is included)
- Using "unlimited" language
- Stating security certifications incorrectly

[17:38] Jordan Blake: We have SOC 2 Type II, but we don’t want to over-claim.

[17:42] Ryan Patel: Exactly—say what you have and keep it factual.

[17:47] Stephanie Nguyen: For Dedicated, we sometimes say "SLA-backed". Is that okay?

[17:53] Meghan O'Connor: It’s okay if you have an SLA doc and it’s your SLA, not AWS.

[17:58] Stephanie Nguyen: Got it.

[18:01] Laura Bennett: What about price changes mid-term? Like if token price changes in public listing.

[18:07] Ankit Rao: Private offer pricing is locked for that buyer for the term, for the dimensions in the offer.

[18:14] Laura Bennett: Good.

[18:16] Jordan Blake: That’s why buyers like private offers.

[18:18] Stephanie Nguyen: We should also cover channel partner private offers—CPPO. Do we need that now?

[18:26] Ryan Patel: Only if a partner/reseller wants to extend the offer. If you’re selling direct to the end customer on Marketplace, standard private offer is fine.

[18:38] Stephanie Nguyen: Ok, not now.

[18:40] Jordan Blake: Let’s land the process in a checklist we can use internally.

[18:45] Stephanie Nguyen: Yep. We'll do a template.

[18:48] Sofia Mendes: Last question from legal: governing law. In our MSA we specify Delaware or California. In Marketplace, what happens?

[18:58] Ankit Rao: Your EULA governs. AWS doesn't impose a governing law, but your document might. Just keep consistent.

[19:06] Sofia Mendes: Okay.

[19:08] Stephanie Nguyen: We’re at time, but I want to recap next steps.

[19:12] Jordan Blake: Before recap—AWS, any recommendation for how we name the offers? We have naming constraints across portals.

[19:20] Meghan O'Connor: Keep it simple: "Redwood Inference - Private Offer - [Customer Name] - [Date]".

[19:28] Stephanie Nguyen: Great.

[19:30] Stephanie Nguyen: Recap:
1) We should use SaaS Contract with Consumption for most enterprise deals with commit + usage.
2) Keep EULA standard; minimize custom legal.
3) Avoid refunds language; prefer discounts; if credits are needed, understand AWS credit memo workflow.
4) Provide buyer account ID, dimensions, pricing schedule, term, start date, and expiration.

[19:55] Stephanie Nguyen: Action items—Laura will confirm discount guardrails and min commits. Sofia will draft standardized private offer legal notes and get sign-off. Cole will send final dimension list/IDs. Ryan will confirm whether we can attach or only link to security packet and SLA docs.

[20:13] Ryan Patel: Yep, I’ll check and send.

[20:15] Meghan O'Connor: Works.

[20:17] Ankit Rao: Sounds good.

[20:18] Jordan Blake: Thanks everyone.

[20:20] Stephanie Nguyen: Thanks. We'll schedule a quick follow-up once we have the template drafted.

[20:26] Meeting ended.

(Note: Fireflies transcript may omit some portions after 20:26 due to early stop / calendar disconnect. Total recording length shown in header includes pre-join audio and post-call buffer.)

title: Procurement / legal - usage definition redlines (ZenChat)
preview: ZenChat procurement/legal walked through redlines focused on (1) defining usage units (tokens/requests/embeddings), (2) retry/fallback behavior and double-charging concerns, (3) caching and whether cache hits are billed, (4) audit logs and retention/access controls for usage data, (5) invoice dispute window and process, and (6) credits/true-ups and how/when credits apply. Redwood agreed to send updated clause language options (token definition + retries + caching), a short billing/metering FAQ, and a proposed dispute window compromise tied to invoice preview + usage exports. ZenChat asked for evidence of audit logging and clarity on whether they can export daily usage in their preferred timezone.

title: Pricing + usage review with Acme AI
preview: Acme AI flagged a discrepancy between Feb invoice preview and their internal counts/usage exports (invoice billed tokens higher). Redwood explained the current Hosted API metering semantics (billed_tokens derived from prompt+completion tokens, model tokenizer-specific; streaming bills for tokens actually generated; retries/fallback should not double-bill but there is a known edge case under certain server retry paths). Nadia confirmed they are rolling out improved dedupe/idempotency and cache_hit attribution fields, plus a "shadow billing compare" to validate changes before they affect invoices. The group agreed on a standard daily export schema (UTC) including request_id, idempotency_key, model, region, raw vs billed tokens, cache_hit fields, retry/fallback indicators, and status so Acme can reconcile per-request. Redwood to run a targeted reconciliation for 2026-02-01 through 2026-02-05 and follow up with findings and any credit recommendation if warranted.

title: StackSage onboarding feedback / activation + early retention
preview: StackSage shared candid feedback on Redwood Hosted API onboarding. They reached first value quickly using the TypeScript SDK + a minimal “hello world” snippet and streaming, but paused after initial tests because they didn’t have a clear path from “it works” to “production confidence”: evals/regression checks, cost predictability, and a simple checklist for going from first request → 100k tokens → first feature shipped. They also noted mild friction around API key handling across environments and wanted clearer guidance on rate limits and what “good” looks like for TTFT/latency. Email onboarding was mostly helpful, but one teammate never received the second email (possible deliverability/suppression). Console nudges were useful when contextual (API key creation / first request), but they asked for less repetition after dismissal. Redwood will follow up with a short activation checklist, recommended model + settings for their use case, and a 2-week pilot plan with success metrics and instrumentation.

title: Lifecycle growth sprint — retargeting launch checklist
preview: Internal working session to finalize retargeting launch details for the lifecycle growth experiments sprint: audience definitions and suppression rules (active opps + enterprise domains), UTM naming conventions, landing page mapping, creative variant set, spend caps, and measurement expectations. Team aligned on a conservative ramp, a hard exclusion list refresh cadence, and how to interpret activation vs retention signals for retargeting.

title: DevFlow AI x Redwood - onboarding feedback / first request friction
preview: DevFlow AI shared onboarding friction from a recent self-serve trial of Redwood Hosted API. Main blockers were: confusion on where to create an API key + which org/project it belongs to, docs/quickstart mismatch with current SDK methods, unclear model name selection, and lack of in-console “next step” guidance after signup. They also hit a 401 due to using a revoked key and didn’t notice the error details; wanted a clearer error surface and copyable curl/SDK snippets in the console. Team uses Node/TypeScript and Vercel; they care about low-latency streaming for an “AI pair programmer” feature and want predictable spend. Requested: onboarding checklist in console, a single minimal “first request” path, clearer rate limit/quotas visibility, and a debugging page for common errors (401/429/model not found).

title: KV layering, lazy kernel-tiling and quantization tradeoffs — Stratus Analytics deep technical review
preview: Redwood presented layered KV strategies and a new lazy kernel-tiling path that reduces working memory for 128k+ context windows. Discussion covered latency vs throughput tradeoffs, quantization impacts on perf/quality, and integration patterns for Stratus' VPC/on-prem hardware (HSM/KMS, air-gapped). Agreed next steps: Redwood to deliver benchmark scripts and security docs; Stratus to provision test VPC for 128k/256k runs. Action items assigned.

title: OrbitFlow x Redwood - Infrastructure & Partner Onboarding Discovery
preview: OrbitFlow wants a hybrid deployment: hosted for non-sensitive workloads and Private/VPC for PII-adjacent workloads. Main blockers: KMS/HSM integration, SOC2 artifacts, VPC/PrivateLink connectivity, and quantized model compatibility. Agreed PoC: ~2-week bench with deliverables (latency/throughput/costs). Redwood to share partner deck, security pack, benchmark harness and pricing. OrbitFlow to share network diagram, anonymized traffic replay, and model artifacts.

title: AWS Marketplace listing review sync - Redwood Inference (SaaS offer)
preview: AWS Marketplace team walked Redwood through SaaS listing requirements and common rejection reasons. Focus was on security documentation expectations (SOC 2/ISO posture wording, data handling, encryption/KMS, audit logs, retention) and how to present shared responsibility in the listing + attached docs. AWS recommended tightening claims (avoid absolute compliance statements), ensuring support and refund language is explicit, and providing a clean architecture/data flow diagram. Timeline: AWS said first-pass review is typically ~5–10 business days after submission if artifacts are complete; security review can add a few days. Redwood targeted end-of-month submission to support a February launch window, pending final copy, screenshots, and attachment bundle.

Focus was on security documentation expectations (SOC 2/ISO posture wording, data handling, encryption/KMS, audit logs, retention) and how to present shared responsibility in the listing + attached docs. AWS recommended tightening claims (avoid absolute compliance statements), ensuring support and refund language is explicit, and providing a clean architecture/data flow diagram. Timeline: AWS said first-pass review is typically ~5–10 business days after submission if artifacts are complete; security review can add a few days. Redwood targeted end-of-month submission to support a February launch window, pending final copy, screenshots, and attachment bundle.

title: Solutions Eng: Demo Scenarios & Objection Sparring (Field Roleplay)
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-12-16
Start: 17:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Samir, Maya, Jordan, Priya, Nina, Tom, Lena
Shared: demo script deck (link: https://redwood.share/demo-scripts/v2), perf sheet (https://redwood.share/bench/perf-q4)

Summary (auto): quick roleplay for field-facing demo flows, work through three objection bundles (latency/cost, private deployment, security), refine canned rebuttals and callouts. 
Topics: demo scripting, competitive posture, bench numbers to show, security bullets, next steps/ownership.

Transcript:
[00:00] Samir (AE): "Okay folks — good to see everybody. We'll start with a very short frame: three roleplay scenes, each ~15 minutes, then 10 minutes at the end to capture updates to the deck and action items. Maya, you want to kick us off with the scenario prompts?"
[00:18] Maya (SE): "Yep. Scene one is 'mid-market healthcare app' — customer worried about PII residency and SOC 2. Scene two is 'B2B search' worried about latency and cost, they compare to Anthropic and OpenAI. Scene three is 'platform team' asking about private deployment, VPC, and KMS. We'll rotate roles so someone plays customer."
[00:42] Nina (AE - East): "I'll take scene one as customer — healthcare, 500 requests/min at peak. My challenge: we have PHI and can't send data to a multi-tenant API. What do you say?"
[00:55] Priya (Enablement Manager): "Quick note: try to keep rebuttals under 30s — we want short soundbites for AEs to memorize."
[01:12] Samir: "Right. Maya, go."
[01:15] Maya: "Thanks. Nina, great question. We do two things: first, Redwood Private — we can run in your VPC or on-prem with customer-managed KMS so tokens and data never leave your network. Second, we have audit logs and SOC2 plus quarterly attestation we can share. For the PHI flow we recommend dedicated capacity model to avoid noisy neighbors and allow predictable throughput."
[01:44] Nina (customer): "Okay, but what's the delta vs saying 'we host on AWS and encrypt' — they push back that it's just incentive language."
[01:53] Jordan (Head SE): "Good push. Add the operational piece: we run a private control plane with optional air-gapped bundles and we provide automated key rotation hooks for your HSM/KMS. Also mention the audit artifacts — not just SOC2 but the raw access logs you can pull for e-discovery. That's concrete."
[02:21] Tom (SE - East, playing customer in scene two): "Scene two: latency/cost. Customer says 'OpenAI gives similar quality and we can cache in their layer'. How would you position Redwood?"
[02:34] Maya: "Short: Redwood gives predictable latency through dedicated capacity and our batching + KV cache options reduce per-token cost. When you need low tail latency we recommend smaller batch windows and a compatible quantized model — we can show tradeoff numbers."
[02:50] Nina: "When they ask 'but can't we just use GPT' — what about model freshness and cost?"
[02:56] Samir: "Point them to the unit economics slide: we compare per-inference token cost on hosted OpenAI vs Redwood Dedicated with prefix caching and reduced context windows. Also call out smart routing: you can route dev traffic to cheaper model variants and route high-criticality traffic to a higher-tier/low-latency variant."
[03:21] Lena (AE - West): "There's often a question about who owns cost if we do dedicated vs hosted — finance will ask."
[03:28] Jordan: "Yes, push the committed capacity model: committed reserved capacity has a flat monthly commitment which makes forecasting easier for finance teams. For pilot we suggest time-boxed burst credits."
[03:42] Nina: "Competitor playbook — customers will bring up Anthropic safety messaging or replication with Replicate. What's the 30-second callout?"
[03:51] Maya: "Anthropic: strong on safety, but less flexible deployment; for regulated workloads we lean Redwood Private. Replicate/Open infra: faster experimentation but less stability for production SLAs. For customers who care about self-hosting, show our private deployment options and rolling upgrades story."
[04:15] Samir: "Remember to avoid badmouthing, instead highlight where Redwood is differentiated: unified runtime across hosted and private, observability, and cost levers."
[04:30] Nina: "Cool. Now scene three — platform team. I'll be the platform eng. I ask: how do you integrate with SSO and enterprise KMS? Also what's the escalation path if a node fails?"
[04:45] Tom: "We hand to Maya for the tech bits."
[04:47] Maya: "SSO: we support SAML/SSO mapped to RBAC, and can integrate with Okta or Azure AD. For keys we integrate with customer KMS and HSM — we provide docs for rotation. For node failures we have automated failover and can route traffic to a warm pool. We also provide tracing hooks so you can pin a request to a model version and see token-level cost breakdowns."
[05:12] Jordan: "Add sentence about our fallbacks: automatic model variant fallback and cross-region routing so service stays up even during capacity squeezes. That's often the 'why Redwood' story for platform owners."
[05:27] Nina: "One real question they asked at Acme last week: can you demonstrate 99th percentile latency under 100ms for short prompts?"
[05:36] Maya: "We can show a bench but caveat: depends on model size, request size, and batching. For tiny prompts with the right model variant and dedicated capacity you can hit sub-100ms p99, but we should be explicit with the customers about conditions. We'll run a quick sketch for the deck."
[05:53] Samir: "Okay that's the first rotation. Quick feedback round: Jordan?"
[06:00] Jordan: "Strong answers overall. Two suggestions: 1) add a canned story for SOC2 vs HIPAA (two sentences), 2) shorten the KMS answer into bullet points for the deck."
[06:15] Priya: "Also, tag which slides to update: security slide, unit economics slide, and the demo flow doc. Samir, add note." 
[06:26] Samir: "Noted. We'll move into roleplay two where we simulate a competitive objection directly: customer says 'We already use OpenAI and Anthropic, why change?' Nina you're on."
[06:40] Nina: "Alright. 'We're happy with current latency and models, switching is risky.'"
[06:44] Samir: "Acknowledge risk, then land on value: Redwood reduces variance in latency and gives cost controls. Give a concrete example: we had a customer cut per-token cost by 35% using caching and routing."
[07:02] Maya: "If they press on integration effort, highlight our SDKs and drop-in API compatibility and offer a short POC with a canary route to prove behavior."
[07:18] Tom: "Script note: add an explicit line to offer sandbox credentials for quick perf checks — customers respond well to hands-on numbers."
[07:28] Lena: "One objection we saw: 'If we go private, we lose access to new model improvements.' How do we answer?"
[07:35] Jordan: "We say: private deployments can be configured to pull vetted model updates on your schedule; you can pin versions and roll gradually. Also mention Redwood Optimize recommendations for model/quantization choices."
[07:56] Maya: "There's some sloppiness in the script where we jam too many technical terms — compute-optimized, quantized, KV cache — we should have parenthetical short explainer lines."
[08:12] Priya: "Actionable: each jargon term gets a one-line footnote in the deck. Samir you're updating the script to include those."
[08:20] Samir: "Yep. I'll do that. Next split: quick live demo runthrough — Maya, can you demo the flow in 90 seconds? We'll do a dry run for cadence."
[08:30] Maya: "I'll do a simple prompt demo: multistep assistant with retrieval + function call mock. Starting now.

[08:40] Maya (demo running): 'Okay, sending prompt to model... receiving partial stream... function call trigger detected: retrieve-customer-profile... done. Final response: \"Hi Nina, here's the update...\"'

[08:57] Nina: "Nice. Could you call out the streams and function calling explicitly when narrating to customers? It helps non-technical AEs."
[09:03] Samir: "Good point — add a 10-second narration script for AEs to say 'this is streamed, we can show token-level tracing and function calls'."
[09:12] Jordan: "Also remind them to mention 'structured output' for deterministic downstream parsing in product use-cases."
[09:22] Tom: "We should run one more full roleplay later with a more hostile customer — helps reps get comfortable."
[09:28] Priya: "We'll schedule a 'hostile objection' clinic in January. We'll note that as follow-up."
[09:34] Samir: "Okay last 20 minutes: fill the deck updates and assign owners. We'll also collect one-liners for the battlecards. Nina, please give 3 oneliners you want to see."
[09:44] Nina: "1) 'Private deployment with KMS: no data leaves your VPC.' 2) 'Predictable economics with reserved capacity' 3) 'Transparent observability and per-request cost breakdowns.'"
[09:58] Samir: "Perfect. Jordan — any final technical caveats we should jot down?"
[10:05] Jordan: "Yes: p99 numbers are conditional on prompt size and model quant; for the deck show two scenarios — short prompt p99 and longer prompt p99. Also note which models support KV caching and what prefix caching gains look like."
[10:22] Priya: "I'll collate these into a battlecard doc and share link in the follow-up. Also will create a 'rebuttal cheat-sheet' from today's roleplays."
[10:33] Samir: "Nice. Before we close, quick housekeeping — who owns the updated deck?"
[10:37] Maya: "I'll take slide edits for the tech slides. Samir keep the demo script. Jordan will do the security slide. Priya will own the battlecard doc."
[10:46] Samir: "Great. Any other competitor calls out?"
[10:49] Nina: "One AE asked about AWS endpoints — they want to know if Redwood can run on top of SageMaker for easier procurement."
[10:58] Maya: "We can integrate in some setups but typically our runtime is opinionated for performance; we'll add a 'works with cloud infra' note but avoid promising full SageMaker endpoint parity."
[11:12] Samir: "Okay, wrap-up. I'll send the updated script and a link to the perf sketch. Thanks everyone."

[End of meeting]

Recap (auto):
- Update demo script to include short narrations and jargon footnotes (Owner: Samir; due 12/18).
- Maya to run perf sketches for p99 under two configs and post numbers (due 12/19).
- Jordan to update security slide with KMS/SSO bullets (due 12/20).
- Priya to publish battlecard and hostile objection clinic invite (due 12/22).

References:
- Demo script deck: https://redwood.share/demo-scripts/v2
- Perf sheet (initial): https://redwood.share/bench/perf-q4
- Follow-up calendar invite: internal calendar (sent)

title: Activation playbook kickoff - Elm Harbor Logistics
preview:
--- Meeting header ---
Date: 2025-11-20
Start time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Evan Miles (Redwood CSM), Aisha Tariq (Elm Harbor - Head of AI Platform), Liam O'Neil (Elm Harbor - CTO), Rosa Gutierrez (Elm Harbor - Sr DevOps), Miguel Santos (Elm Harbor - PM)
Recording id: fhf-20251120-elm-activation-001

Summary: quick kickoff to align on onboarding/playbook, security checklist, staging needs, pilot scope (2 prompt sets), timeline for activation and who's owning what. Discussed dedicated vs hosted for pilot, network/VPC constraints, SSO/KMS + SOC2 expectations.

Topics: activation timeline; sandbox and benchmark; security & network; success metrics and pilot scope; pricing/commits; next steps

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, uh, I think we have about 50 minutes set aside, so we'll run through the activation playbook, who's doing what, and get the first pilot scoped out. Can folks do quick intros?
[00:18] Aisha Tariq: Yeah hi, I'm Aisha, Head of AI Platform at Elm Harbor. We're building a dockside scheduling assistant and an internal SLA alerting assistant. Putting Redwood in as inference layer for both.
[00:28] Liam O'Neil: Liam here, CTO. High level I'll lean on Rosa and Miguel to drive technical and product decisions.
[00:34] Rosa Gutierrez: Rosa — I manage infra and networking. We'll need to validate VPC peering and outbound rules, and our security team will want KMS integration and SSO.
[00:41] Miguel Santos: Miguel, PM for the dockside product — I'll own pilot acceptance criteria and prompt corpus with Aisha.
[00:48] Priya Nair: Priya from Redwood SE, I'll handle the sandbox, initial benchmarks, and the deploy/run steps, plus the KV cache and batching tuning.
[00:55] Evan Miles: Evan, CSM, I'll be your main contact for onboarding cadence and success tracking.
[01:02] Jordan Blake: Great. Quick agenda: 1) timeline and milestones, 2) network/security checklist, 3) pilot scope & metrics, 4) pricing and dedicated vs hosted decision for pilot, 5) next steps. Any additions?
[01:14] Aisha Tariq: No that covers it. Wanted to note we have a compliance review window two weeks from now, so timing's tight.
[01:21] Jordan Blake: Got it. We'll align timeline to that.

[05:00] Priya Nair: Okay timeline — our suggested activation playbook is eight weeks: week 0-1 sandbox + creds, week 2 pilot data and prompts, week 3 performance benchmarks and tuning, week 4-6 rollouts/canary, week 7 run acceptance evaluation and handoff. We can compress if you need.
[05:18] Rosa Gutierrez: Two quick questions — network: does Redwood need inbound VPN or is egress to our VPC e.g., private link supported?
[05:27] Priya Nair: For hosted it's egress only; we can do Private deployment for full VPC peering or Private with a customer-controlled plane. For the pilot we usually recommend hosted sandbox with strict retention and dedicated capacity so you get predictable latency.
[05:39] Rosa Gutierrez: And SAML for SSO? We use Okta.
[05:43] Jordan Blake: Yes SAML/Okta supported, plus RBAC in the Console. We'll send a SAML metadata file and an SSO integration doc.

[08:00] Miguel Santos: Pilot scope — we want two prompt sets: 1) dock scheduling (real-time vendor interactions, low latency requirement) 2) SLA incident triage (longer context, embeddings/rerank heavy). Success metrics: P95 latency under 200ms for the scheduling flow at 95th percentile of simulated load; and classification precision/recall thresholds for triage.
[08:24] Priya Nair: For the scheduling flow, we'll recommend a smaller low-latency model and a dedicated pool (x4 GPUs) to keep warm KV cache. For triage we can run larger model variants with embeddings + reranking; caching helps too to cut costs.
[08:38] Liam O'Neil: Cost question — if we do dedicated GPU pools for pilot, what's the commit and can we autoscale down?
[08:45] Jordan Blake: You can reserve for minimum capacity with autoscale up for bursts. We'll send a couple of commit tiers; there's monthly reserved pricing and usage overage.

[12:00] Aisha Tariq: SOC2 compliance — our auditors will want to see audit logs and key management. Do you expose audit logs and integrate with third-party KMS?
[12:10] Evan Miles: Yes, Redwood's Private and Dedicated offerings include audit logs and integrations with KMS/HSM. For hosted we offer audit export and encrypted at rest with customer-managed keys available for Private only — we'll clarify in the security pack.
[12:23] Rosa Gutierrez: Also need the egress IP ranges for firewall rules; can you provide that?
[12:28] Priya Nair: We'll share a CIDR list and a short lived token process for the sandbox.

Any infra blockers?
[15:23] Rosa Gutierrez: We'll need a scoped service account and we require SAML SSO for access. Also need to confirm log retention policies — legal requires 90 days for some logs.
[15:33] Evan Miles: We can set default retention and discuss extended retention as an add-on; we will note that as part of the security packet.

[18:00] Priya Nair: Quick note about model variants — we recommend starting with an open LLM that's Redwood-verified for latency, then we can test quantized variants to reduce cost. Some transcriptions in our docs say KV cash — that's obviously KV cache — sorry, autocorrect.
[18:20] Miguel Santos: Haha, KV cash, we like that.

[22:00] Jordan Blake: Onboarding milestones — I'm going to propose these dates: sandbox creds by 2025-11-22, first benchmark run by 2025-11-26, network & SSO tests by 2025-11-28, pilot acceptance by 2025-12-12. Does that align with your compliance window, Aisha?
[22:20] Aisha Tariq: Compliance review is on 2025-12-04, so if acceptance can be by 12/12 that's okay but we need the security docs earlier.
[22:30] Jordan Blake: We'll prioritize security docs by 2025-11-21.

[25:00] Rosa Gutierrez: A technical detail — we need to understand KV cache eviction semantics and batching behavior when you have mixed latency workloads.
[25:10] Priya Nair: Good call. Short answer: cache is per-token prefix; eviction is LRU with a tunable TTL; batching is adaptive — we can set strict latency SLOs and Redwood will bias scheduling to meet those while still batching opportunistically. We'll share a short doc and a schematic, maybe a whiteboard capture.
[25:28] Liam O'Neil: What about on-prem option? We have some workloads that cannot leave the datacenter.
[25:34] Jordan Blake: For regulated workloads we can do Redwood Private with a customer-managed control plane — on-prem package is available but that's a longer procurement and install path. For the pilot, hosted with strict retention or Dedicated in your VPC is usually faster.

[30:00] Miguel Santos: Another question — how do rollouts work? We want canaries and automatic fallback if latency/regression detected.
[30:10] Priya Nair: Console supports route-level rollouts, canary percents, automatic fallback policies if model error rates or latency cross thresholds. We'll demo this in the next session.

[33:00] Jordan Blake: Let's surface a quick risk register — top risks: network/firewall delays, security sign-off time, model quality regressions, quota surprises. Mitigations: early sandbox, security packet, baseline evaluations, and a small reserved commit to cap cost surprises.
[33:18] Aisha Tariq: Agree. Also need to confirm data residency — some of our records are EU-bound.
[33:25] Jordan Blake: We have region controls — will note EU region for those routes. We'll include that in configuration in the playbook.

[36:00] Rosa Gutierrez: For the sandbox, we prefer ephemeral test credentials and want logs forwarded to our SIEM. Is log forwarding supported?
[36:08] Evan Miles: Yes, we support webhook and syslog-style forwarding for events and can arrange a connector during pilot. We'll include a sample in the security pack.

[40:00] Priya Nair: On the benchmark plan — we'll create two harnesses: 1) low-latency burst harness tuned for scheduling and 2) throughput/embedding harness for triage. We'll run both during the first week and share CSV/plots.

[40:28] Priya Nair: We have output metadata and token-level logs for debugging; for production GRPC streams we can include structured outputs and tool calls to trace decisions. Success metrics: P95 latency under 200ms for the scheduling flow at 95th percentile of simulated load; and classification precision/recall thresholds for triage.

[08:38] Liam O'Neil: Cost question — if we do dedicated GPU pools for pilot, what's the commit and can we autoscale down?
[08:45] Jordan Blake: You can reserve for minimum capacity with autoscale up for bursts. We'll send a couple of commit tiers; there's monthly reserved pricing and usage overage.

title: Ultra-long-context KV/batching/quantization deep dive - Nimbus DX
preview: Deep technical session about supporting ultra long-context workloads (100k-200k tokens). Covered KV cache strategies, hierarchical chunking, batching tradeoffs, quantization approaches for 4-bit/8-bit, runtime kernel choices, and on-prem/VPC constraints. Agreed to run two bench sets (hosted + on-prem) and to share quant profile + kernel logs.
text: Deep technical session about supporting ultra long-context workloads (100k-200k tokens).
