title: Avalon Health - SecOps pen test walkthrough + SDLC & patch cadence
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-11-05
Start: 14:30 PT
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Evan (Redwood CSM), Maya, Luis, Hannah, Omar (Avalon)

Auto-summary (auto-gen; may be fuzzy): walkthrough of recent 3rd-party pen test results; discussion of medium/high findings around auth and container images; alignment on patch cadence and secure SDLC gating for PRs; agree to action items and retest timeline.

00:00 Jordan: hey everyone, thanks for joining. can you all hear me?
00:04 Maya: yes loud and clear, thanks.
00:06 Jordan: cool, quick agenda — priya will run through the pen test findings we saw, we'll talk mitigation options, then go over SDLC/patch cadence recommendations and close with next steps. sound good?
00:18 Luis: yep fine, and we also wanted to confirm how Redwood handles KMS and audit logs in Private mode.
00:25 Priya: absolutely, I'll surface that as we go. quick note, transcript might have some mis-hears — apologies, this was recorded by the flybot.

02:12 Priya: ok opening with the high-level from the vendor report — 42 total findings, 3 high, 11 medium, rest low/info. the high ones were: an auth bypass in an internal admin endpoint exposed via staging, a container image with a known CVE (CVE-2024-27812) in a sidecar, and an S3 misconfig that could expose metadata. some of the medium are outdated libs and insufficient RBAC on some service accounts.
02:39 Hannah: that CVE — is that in our base image or something in one of the sidecar helpers?
02:45 Priya: vendor called it in the observability sidecar image — it's an older base layer we picked up from upstream. we flagged it for immediate patch. note: shorthand in the report says K-V cash issues, they meant KV cache, that's a transcription/typo.

04:05 Maya: thanks. for the auth bypass, vendor provided PoC?
04:09 Priya: yes, limited PoC — they showed an unauthenticated call to /admin/metrics that returned partial payloads. we think it's due to a missing middleware in the internal route config; likely introduced in a canary rollout two months ago.
04:26 Luis: that aligns with a routing change our platform team merged around 2025-09-18. we'll need to check the PR history.

05:40 Evan: quick Q — how do you typically recommend handling rollbacks vs patching for an auth regression like that?
05:45 Priya: if it's a config/regression you can roll back to the last known-good commit and then patch the middleware in a follow-up. for customers on Redwood Dedicated or Private we also recommend applying per-route RBAC and using the console to enforce canary policies so this can't reach prod wide without the middleware.

07:01 Omar: on audit logs — we need full event retention for 12 months for compliance, is that standard in Private?
07:09 Jordan: Private supports configurable retention; default retention is 90 days but enterprise customers can configure archive to S3 with encryption for long-term retention. that ties into KMS for key rotation.
07:21 Omar: do you support customer-managed keys/HSM?
07:24 Priya: yes, KMS/HSM integrations are available — the Private control plane can be configured with customer KMS; we documented steps for AWS KMS and Azure Key Vault in the security whitepaper. Jordan will share the link.

09:00 Maya: great. on the container SBOM side, we run nightly builds — our CI does dependency scanning but maybe not to a high enough threshold. what do you suggest?
09:09 Priya: two things — one, include SBOM generation on each image build and fail builds for critical CVEs; two, set up an automated alerting feed from dependency scanner into the incident queue. also pin base images and run image hardening scans. Redwood's recommended config for Private has an example GitHub Actions flow.

11:32 Hannah: regarding patch cadence, our current internal SLA is 30 days for high, 90 days for medium, 180 for low. the vendor flagged several medium items where the transitive dep was > 90 days.
11:44 Priya: that's sensible. for customers in regulated health orgs we usually advise tightening to 14 days for critical, 30 for high, 60 for medium given attack surface. but we recognize operational constraints.

13:20 Luis: if we tighten to 30 for high, what's the impact operationally on our release pipeline? Hannah: we'll need to increase triage and patch automation. could be more builds per week.
13:36 Priya: Redwood Optimize can recommend batch/quantization changes unrelatedly, but the relevant part here is we can help with image scanning hooks and provide a small runbook for hotfix branching strategy so you don't block normal feature rollouts.

15:05 Maya: on SOC 2 mapping — the pen test noted gaps in SSO enforcement for internal apps. we want to map each finding back to SOC 2 control and see risk posture.
15:15 Jordan: understood. have you scoped SOC 2 Type II already?
15:19 Omar: we're mid-audit prep; scope largely covers core services and employee access. we'll share the scope.
15:28 Priya: we can create a simple mapping table: finding -> control ID -> mitigation -> evidence artifact. it helps auditors and drives actionable remediation.

17:02 Priya: diving into a medium example — service account tokens were overly permissive in the staging cluster; vendor showed a role binding that allowed cluster wide list/get on secrets. remediation is to tighten to namespace-level role and rotate tokens. evidence: revoke and re-issue.
17:20 Hannah: is there an easy script to rotate tokens and update deployments without downtime?
17:24 Priya: yes we provide a k8s job template and a zero-downtime rollout pattern in the docs. we'll attach that.

19:40 Luis: question about RBAC mistakes in config — is there static analysis for Terraform or k8s manifests?
19:46 Priya: strongly recommend terraform sentinel or policy-as-code, and for k8s OPA/Gatekeeper policies in CI to catch broad role bindings. Redwood's private deployment examples include Gatekeeper snippets.

21:20 Maya: vendor recommended retest once remediations are in. typical turnaround?
21:25 Priya: usually 2-3 weeks after remediation to schedule a focused retest; depends on vendor calendar. if Avalon wants priority we can coordinate.

22:48 Hannah: can Redwood do a dark-lane sort of internal scan or is that strictly third-party?
22:53 Jordan: Redwood doesn't perform external pen tests for customers, but our SE org can run an internal config audit and you can add Redwood as an observer for the vendor retest. Priya: we can also share an infra checklist.

25:12 Priya: a couple of minor transcription artifacts in the vendor doc — they wrote 'RBAC denylist' which likely meant 'allowlist', small wording difference but important.
25:24 Maya: ha ok noted.

27:02 Omar: on encryption-at-rest, are database backups encrypted with customer keys?
27:07 Priya: for Private yes, backups can be encrypted with customer-managed KMS; for Hosted we use service-managed keys but support envelope encryption for certain customers.

28:55 Luis: one of the medium items flagged was incomplete logging for a scheduled job — logs were rotating out under 30 days. we need to retain some of that longer for audit.
29:03 Jordan: that's configurable — you can push logs to S3 with lifecycle rules and object locks if required. we can share a sample lifecycle policy.

30:40 Hannah: SDLC question — we want to block PRs that introduce dependencies with critical CVEs. recommended approach?
30:46 Priya: integrate the dependency scanner into the PR check, fail the pipeline for criticals, and for medium allow a skip only with a documented approver and a JIRA ticket. capture SBOM and CVE metadata in the ticket.

33:10 Maya: vendor also gave a medium finding on disclosure of internal service names in error pages. we have an edge error handling layer, can we rewrite responses?
33:18 Priya: yes mask errors at the edge; Redwood's console route-level filters can strip headers and normalise error bodies for hosted. for private, do it at your ingress with the same pattern.

35:05 Luis: timeline question — if we accept Redwood's mitigations and fix in 2 weeks, what's the retest schedule? we want this closed before end of month.
35:13 Priya: if you start remediation immediately, vendor retest can be scheduled for week 3; Jordan: we'll coordinate with the vendor to aim for 2025-11-24 retest window.

36:40 Hannah: on long-lived credentials in CI — we have a few personal tokens in some jobs. vendor called that out as medium.
36:48 Priya: rotate to ephemeral credentials and use short-lived OIDC tokens from your CI to the cloud provider; also use KMS to wrap secrets so no static tokens land in logs.

38:55 Omar: compliance asked about proof of vulnerability triage — auditors want a paper trail.
39:00 Priya: create a JIRA workflow where findings are tickets with severity, owners, and fix/retest links. Redwood's recommended template includes evidence attachments and test run IDs.

40:30 Jordan: quick re-iterate on KMS: Redwood supports bringing your own key, rotating every X days is possible, and you can configure key access policies per environment.

42:02 Maya: we will probably request a dedicated VPC deployment for the sensitive env — what's the difference for patching/ops?
42:09 Priya: Dedicated/VPC gives you network isolation and more control over patch windows; you still push images but you control patch orchestration and can run in a maintenance window. Redwood offers assisted patch runs as a managed service for Dedicated contracts.

44:10 Luis: any risks with automated rollback and canary policies when applying patches? we don't want a bad patch to cascade.
44:16 Priya: use progressive canary with automatic rollback triggers based on health checks and tail-latency thresholds. we also suggest a blue/green approach for DB migrations around security patches.

46:45 Hannah: vendor asked for proof that we've patched CVE-2024-27812 across all clusters — will a single CVE ticket with image hashes suffice?
46:52 Priya: yes, provide SBOM + image digests + deployment manifests and a short runbook showing where the images were updated. that's standard audit evidence.

48:10 Maya: any guidance on communicating this to execs? short risk summary?
48:16 Jordan: suggest a 1-page risk summary: number of findings, number high/medium/low, key mitigations, business impact, timeline to closure, and sign-off. keep it non-technical for execs and append the technical annex.

50:00 Priya: ok wrapping — quick recap: we'll share per-finding remediation notes (Priya), link to Private KMS + audit docs (Jordan), Avalon to share SBOM and patch SLA (Hannah/Maya), schedule retest week of 2025-11-24.
50:18 Maya: yep that works. thanks everyone.
50:24 Luis: thanks.
50:26 Jordan: thanks all. end.

04:05 Maya: thanks. for the auth bypass, vendor provided PoC?
04:09 Priya: yes, limited PoC — they showed an unauthenticated call to /admin/metrics that returned partial payloads. we think it's due to a missing middleware in the internal route config; likely introduced in a canary rollout two months ago.
04:26 Luis: that aligns with a routing change our platform team merged around 2025-09-18. we'll need to check the PR history.

05:40 Evan: quick Q — how do you typically recommend handling rollbacks vs patching for an auth regression like that?
05:45 Priya: if it's a config/regression you can roll back to the last known-good commit and then patch the middleware in a follow-up. for customers on Redwood Dedicated or Private we also recommend applying per-route RBAC and using the console to enforce canary policies so this can't reach prod wide without the middleware.

07:01 Omar: on audit logs — we need full event retention for 12 months for compliance, is that standard in Private?
07:09 Jordan: Private supports configurable retention; default retention is 90 days but enterprise customers can configure archive to S3 with encryption for long-term retention. that ties into KMS for key rotation.
07:21 Omar: do you support customer-managed keys/HSM?
07:24 Priya: yes, KMS/HSM integrations are available — the Private control plane can be configured with customer KMS; we documented steps for AWS KMS and Azure Key Vault in the security whitepaper. Jordan will share the link.

09:00 Maya: great. on the container SBOM side, we run nightly builds — our CI does dependency scanning but maybe not to a high enough threshold. what do you suggest?
09:09 Priya: two things — one, include SBOM generation on each image build and fail builds for critical CVEs; two, set up an automated alerting feed from dependency scanner into the incident queue. also pin base images and run image hardening scans. Redwood's recommended config for Private has an example GitHub Actions flow.

11:32 Hannah: regarding patch cadence, our current internal SLA is 30 days for high, 90 days for medium, 180 for low. the vendor flagged several medium items where the transitive dep was > 90 days.
11:44 Priya: that's sensible. for customers in regulated health orgs we usually advise tightening to 14 days for critical, 30 for high, 60 for medium given attack surface. but we recognize operational constraints.

13:20 Luis: if we tighten to 30 for high, what's the impact operationally on our release pipeline? Hannah: we'll need to increase triage and patch automation. could be more builds per week.
13:36 Priya: Redwood Optimize can recommend batch/quantization changes unrelatedly, but the relevant part here is we can help with image scanning hooks and provide a small runbook for hotfix branching strategy so you don't block normal feature rollouts.

15:05 Maya: on SOC 2 mapping — the pen test noted gaps in SSO enforcement for internal apps. we want to map each finding back to SOC 2 control and see risk posture.
15:15 Jordan: understood. have you scoped SOC 2 Type II already?
15:19 Omar: we're mid-audit prep; scope largely covers core services and employee access. we'll share the scope.
15:28 Priya: we can create a simple mapping table: finding -> control ID -> mitigation -> evidence artifact. it helps auditors and drives actionable remediation.

17:02 Priya: diving into a medium example — service account tokens were overly permissive in the staging cluster; vendor showed a role binding that allowed cluster wide list/get on secrets. remediation is to tighten to namespace-level role and rotate tokens. evidence: revoke and re-issue.
17:20 Hannah: is there an easy script to rotate tokens and update deployments without downtime?
17:24 Priya: yes we provide a k8s job template and a zero-downtime rollout pattern in the docs. we'll attach that.

19:40 Luis: question about RBAC mistakes in config — is there static analysis for Terraform or k8s manifests?
19:46 Priya: strongly recommend terraform sentinel or policy-as-code, and for k8s OPA/Gatekeeper policies in CI to catch broad role bindings. Redwood's private deployment examples include Gatekeeper snippets.

21:20 Maya: vendor recommended retest once remediations are in. typical turnaround?
21:25 Priya: usually 2-3 weeks after remediation to schedule a focused retest; depends on vendor calendar. if Avalon wants priority we can coordinate.

22:48 Hannah: can Redwood do a dark-lane sort of internal scan or is that strictly third-party?
22:53 Jordan: Redwood doesn't perform external pen tests for customers, but our SE org can run an internal config audit and you can add Redwood as an observer for the vendor retest. Priya: we can also share an infra checklist.

25:12 Priya: a couple of minor transcription artifacts in the vendor doc — they wrote 'RBAC denylist' which likely meant 'allowlist', small wording difference but important.
25:24 Maya: ha ok noted.

27:02 Omar: on encryption-at-rest, are database backups encrypted with customer keys?
27:07 Priya: for Private yes, backups can be encrypted with customer-managed KMS; for Hosted we use service-managed keys but support envelope encryption for certain customers.

28:55 Luis: one of the medium items flagged was incomplete logging for a scheduled job — logs were rotating out under 30 days. we need to retain some of that longer for audit.
29:03 Jordan: that's configurable — you can push logs to S3 with lifecycle rules and object locks if required. we can share a sample lifecycle policy.

30:40 Hannah: SDLC question — we want to block PRs that introduce dependencies with critical CVEs. recommended approach?
30:46 Priya: integrate the dependency scanner into the PR check, fail the pipeline for criticals, and for medium allow a skip only with a documented approver and a JIRA ticket. capture SBOM and CVE metadata in the ticket.

33:10 Maya: vendor also gave a medium finding on disclosure of internal service names in error pages. we have an edge error handling layer, can we rewrite responses?
33:18 Priya: yes mask errors at the edge; Redwood's console route-level filters can strip headers and normalise error bodies for hosted. for private, do it at your ingress with the same pattern.

35:05 Luis: timeline question — if we accept Redwood's mitigations and fix in 2 weeks, what's the retest schedule? we want this closed before end of month.
35:13 Priya: if you start remediation immediately, vendor retest can be scheduled for week 3; Jordan: we'll coordinate with the vendor to aim for 2025-11-24 retest window.

36:40 Hannah: on long-lived credentials in CI — we have a few personal tokens in some jobs. vendor called that out as medium.
36:48 Priya: rotate to ephemeral credentials and use short-lived OIDC tokens from your CI to the cloud provider; also use KMS to wrap secrets so no static tokens land in logs.

38:55 Omar: compliance asked about proof of vulnerability triage — auditors want a paper trail.
39:00 Priya: create a JIRA workflow where findings are tickets with severity, owners, and fix/retest links. Redwood's recommended template includes evidence attachments and test run IDs.

40:30 Jordan: quick re-iterate on KMS: Redwood supports bringing your own key, rotating every X days is possible, and you can configure key access policies per environment.

42:02 Maya: we will probably request a dedicated VPC deployment for the sensitive env — what's the difference for patching/ops?
42:09 Priya: Dedicated/VPC gives you network isolation and more control over patch windows; you still push images but you control patch orchestration and can run in a maintenance window. Redwood offers assisted patch runs as a managed service for Dedicated contracts.

44:10 Luis: any risks with automated rollback and canary policies when applying patches? we don't want a bad patch to cascade.
44:16 Priya: use progressive canary with automatic rollback triggers based on health checks and tail-latency thresholds. we also suggest a blue/green approach for DB migrations around security patches.

46:45 Hannah: vendor asked for proof that we've patched CVE-2024-27812 across all clusters — will a single CVE ticket with image hashes suffice?
46:52 Priya: yes, provide SBOM + image digests + deployment manifests and a short runbook showing where the images were updated. that's standard audit evidence.

48:10 Maya: any guidance on communicating this to execs? short risk summary?
48:16 Jordan: suggest a 1-page risk summary: number of findings, number high/medium/low, key mitigations, business impact, timeline to closure, and sign-off. keep it non-technical for execs and append the technical annex.

50:00 Priya: ok wrapping — quick recap: we'll share per-finding remediation notes (Priya), link to Private KMS + audit docs (Jordan), Avalon to share SBOM and patch SLA (Hannah/Maya), schedule retest week of 2025-11-24.
50:18 Maya: yep that works. thanks everyone.
50:24 Luis: thanks.
50:26 Jordan: thanks all. end.

title: NovaFerry renewal + regional uptick sync
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-10-30 16:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Samira Patel (Redwood SE), Ethan Riley (NovaFerry VP Eng), Priya Nair (NovaFerry Security Lead), Liam O'Connor (NovaFerry Platform)

Auto-generated summary (brief):
Customer wants to renew with a 2x capacity uplift and add EU region. Discussed hosted vs dedicated vs private options; compliance gaps (KMS, audit logs), latency targets in EU, and runbook/benchmarks. Agreed next steps: pricing, benchmark window, compliance doc exchange.

Topics: renewal timeline; capacity bump (2x); EU geocluster onboarding; security/KMS and audit logging; performance benchmark plan; pricing/contract amendments

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: hey everyone, thanks for joining — let's do quick intros then we'll jump into the renewal and region ask, um, and Samira will cover the perf side.
[00:12] Ethan: hi all, Ethan here. We're doing fine, short on time so appreciate it.
[00:16] Priya: Priya, security at NovaFerry. I have a few questions on KMS and audit logs.
[00:22] Liam: Liam — platform eng, hi.
[00:25] Jordan: great. Quick agenda: 1) confirm renewal terms and desired capacity 2) map EU region onboarding steps and timing 3) surface any security or compliance blockers and 4) schedule benchmark/test window. Does that work?
[00:36] Ethan: yes, that's right. To be explicit, we want to double our committed throughput starting Jan, add an EU region for lower latency to EU customers, and keep data residency for EU requests.
[00:47] Samira: cool. when you say double, what's your baseline commit today?
[00:51] Ethan: current commit is 40M tokens/month, so target is 80M. Peak concurrency today is around 120 concurrent requests, 95th percentile token latency target for EU ideally under 180ms per token for short prompts.
[01:08] Samira: okay that helps. two main paths: hosted with regional routing (fastest), or Dedicated pools in EU (predictable throughput, isolation). There's also Private/VPC if you need control over key management. each has tradeoffs on cost/latency.
[01:26] Priya: can we pause — about KMS — we need envelope encryption using our HSM (Thales) for any EU-resident keys and logs to go to our audit pipeline. does Redwood support customer-managed keys or HSM integrations in Private or Dedicated?
[01:40] Jordan: short answer: yes for Private; Dedicated can integrate with KMS/HSM via our KMS connectors but we need to scope the Thales integration as a custom item — Samira can unpack. For hosted, we provide managed KMS and BYOK options but HSM-level control typically requires Private.
[01:58] Samira: yeah, with Dedicated we can offer a KMS connector — it uses the customer's key wrapping and we rotate, but if you require FIPS 140-2 HSM attestation with on-prem HSM, Private deployment is the path. there's some extra onboarding time.
[02:14] Liam: what's the lead time for Private? Our timeline is to have EU region live by mid-Jan. Is that feasible?
[02:20] Samira: mid-Jan is tight. Hosted regional routing we can have in weeks, Dedicated EU pool is about 4-6 weeks if capacity slots open, Private with HSM is more like 8-12+ weeks because of network and install window.
[02:34] Ethan: hmm, okay. so maybe we do Dedicated for Jan then evaluate Private after. Our security team prefers Private long term, but the timing is the deal.
[02:44] Priya: that sounds reasonable, but we need to be clear on audit logs and access controls in Dedicated. Does Dedicated provide per-tenant audit logs with SSO/RBAC tie-ins?
[02:54] Jordan: yes, Dedicated includes per-tenant audit logging, SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, and fine-grained RBAC. We'll share the exact schema for logs — it's in the security pack but I'll attach the doc.
[03:06] Priya: please do. also need retention controls — we might need 7 years for certain artifacts.
[03:12] Jordan: noted. retention policy in Dedicated is configurable; for hosted we'll note standard retention and add-on options.
[03:18] [00:03:20] Ethan: ok on latency — Samira, you mentioned batching/caching, can we keep per-token latency under that 180ms target in EU with Dedicated? We're concerned about throughput vs tail latency during spikes.
[03:34] Samira: right — there's two levers. Dedicated with smaller batching windows and pre-warmed KV cache for frequent prompts keeps tail latency low, but increases cost per token. We can also do prefix caching for common flows. For spikes, autoscaling policy plus burst capacity helps. We should run a dedicated benchmark using your traffic pattern.
[03:50] Liam: one more on billing — if we commit to 2x, do we get discounted rate tiers? and how do we account for EU region capacity — is that included or a separate commitment?
[04:00] Jordan: yes to discounts — our reserved capacity pricing tiers scale with committed tokens. Regions can be added to the same commitment or be billed separately; many customers roll EU as part of their global commit for a blended rate. I'll include both model options in the quote.
[04:13] Ethan: about fallbacks — if EU pool is saturated, can requests failover to US with acceptable latency? We want graceful degradation, not hard failures.
[04:22] Samira: yes, routing policies can be set for failover to a compatible model in a different region. the tradeoff is higher latency for those requests. we can set automatic fallback to a cheaper variant too if you want cost-based fallback.
[04:36] Priya: on compliance docs, can you re-share SOC2 report, ISO certs, and the KMS connector spec? also your breach notification SLA?
[04:44] Jordan: absolutely. I'll attach SOC2+ISO snapshots and the KMS connector spec. breach notification is 72 hours for critical incidents by default; we can tighten in an enterprise SLA.
[04:54] [00:05:00] Ethan: cool. Samira, for the benchmark run, what do you need from us?
[05:00] Samira: a few things: 1) representative prompt set and weightings, 2) token size distribution (p50/p95), 3) concurrency profile, 4) example latency SLOs. Ideally we also get a small dataset and can run a 4-hour stress test plus a 24-hour soak. we'll capture tail-latency, throughput, and token-cost.
[05:18] Ethan: I can export the token histogram, but we might need to sanitize logs first. I'll get that ready.
[05:22] Liam: what's the expected spike handling in the Dedicated plan — do you pre-allocate burst capacity or autoscale dynamically?
[05:28] Samira: Dedicated supports autoscaling and we offer burst pools reserved for short-term spikes; both are configurable. autoscaling has warm-up times which we account for in the runbook.
[05:36] Jordan: in terms of contract, to meet Jan go-live we recommend signing amendment for capacity by mid-November to lock capacity and pricing. that aligns with infra provisioning timelines.
[05:48] Priya: legal — we'll need an SOW amendment clarifying data residency, KMS binding, and audit access. Noted.
[05:54] Ethan: competitor check: we've had a few calls with OtherCloudAI and InferaServe; cost is similar, but Redwood's observability is a positive. Our architects liked the KV cache idea.
[06:05] Samira: noted. we also have Redwood Optimize recommendations that will suggest quantization profiles and batching windows for your workload to balance cost/latency.
[06:13] [00:06:30] Priya: quick question — what's the minimum token commit for adding a regional Dedicated pool?
[06:19] Jordan: for EU Dedicated pools we typically ask for a minimum of 20M tokens/month per region in a commercial uplift, but we can flex depending on account history. I'll put clear numbers in the quote.
[06:30] Ethan: ok. regarding SSO — our IdP is Okta; do you support SAML + SCIM?
[06:35] Jordan: yes, SAML for SSO and SCIM for provisioning. SCIM supports group sync for RBAC mapping.
[06:42] Liam: there's one more ops ask — can we get token-level cost and latency dashboards for specific routes? We have attention-heavy flows that we'd like to watch separately.
[06:51] Samira: yes, per-route dashboards are available in the console — we can create a shared dashboard for NovaFerry and give view access to your SREs.
[06:59] [00:07:10] Ethan: great. So let's confirm immediate next steps — Jordan to send revised pricing and security pack, Samira to schedule benchmark, Ethan to share sanitized token histogram, Priya to confirm KMS/HSM constraints. Timeline target: sign amendment by ~2025-11-14 to hit Jan activation.
[07:20] Jordan: correct. I'll aim for 2025-11-03 to send the pricing and attachments. Samira, can you propose 2-3 windows for the benchmark week of 2025-11-10?
[07:28] Samira: yes I'll propose two 4-hour windows and one 24-hour soak.
[07:33] Priya: one more legal thought — for EU data residency, we need clarity on where ephemeral caches/KV persist. do they stay in region or replicate to the control plane?
[07:44] Samira: ephem caches stay in-region for Dedicated; control plane telemetry may be centralized but we can configure what telemetry leaves region and provide pseudonymized metrics if required. that's a security note we'll include.
[07:57] Ethan: sounds good. Anything else on billing or discounts?
[08:00] Jordan: I'll include two pricing ladders: blended global commit and region-specific add-on. Also note early-pay discounts if you want annual prepay.
[08:10] [00:08:20] Liam: by the way, do you have an SLA for 99.9% availability for Dedicated?
[08:14] Jordan: our Dedicated SLA is typically 99.95 for provisioned capacity, we'll include the specific SLA language in the amendment and outline credit structure.
[08:22] Priya: and incident runbooks? we need on-call escalation paths.
[08:27] Samira: yes, we'll share runbooks. for Dedicated you get dedicated on-call with priority routing.
[08:33] Ethan: ok, good. We're comfortable proceeding on the plan of signing amendment and starting Dedicated EU with the intent to migrate to Private HSM later in the year.
[08:41] Jordan: excellent. quick recap and owners then close.
[08:44] Jordan: recap: 1) Jordan send pricing + SOC2/ISO + KMS connector doc by 2025-11-03 2) Samira schedule perf windows week of 2025-11-10 and run benchmark 3) Ethan share sanitized token histogram by 2025-11-05 4) Priya confirm KMS/HSM specifics and retention by 2025-11-07 5) legal signs amendment target 2025-11-14.
[09:09] Priya: yep, all good from security side as long as docs show KMS options and audit schema.
[09:15] Ethan: thanks all.
[09:17] Jordan: thanks everyone, we'll follow up in email with attachments and calendar invites. Bye.

Notes on transcript quality: occasional mis-heard acronyms and filler words; some speaker labels are paraphrased.

Referenced artifacts / links: 
- Security pack (SOC2/ISO snapshots) — link to be attached by Jordan
- KMS connector spec (Thales integration note) — Jordan to attach
- Pricing template and amendment draft — Jordan to attach
- Benchmark runbook + sample scripts — Samira to share

End of transcript.

title: Cloud Marketplace Partner – Security collateral alignment (SOC 2, shared responsibility, listing requirements)

title: Marigold Health - Dedicated capacity intake
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-08-27 15:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Evan Park (Marigold Head of Platform), Maya Singh (ML Engineer), Carlos Ortega (Security Lead)

Summary:
Short intake call to understand Marigold Health's inference needs for deploying a mix of hosted + dedicated capacity. Main goals: reserved GPU sizing, strict tenant isolation for PHI-adjacent workloads, burst patterns (marketing spikes & nightly batch), and compliance/KMS requirements. Agreed to run benchmarks and exchange traffic profiles.

Transcript:
[00:00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone thanks for joining, I'm Jordan from Redwood, and Priya's our systems engineer. Quick roll call?
[00:00:08] Evan: Evan here, Maya and Carlos with me. Thanks for setting this up.
[00:00:12] Priya: Hi all, Priya - SE. Today we'll try to scope reserved nodes and what isolation means for you.
[00:00:21] Maya: Cool thanks. We're trying to decide between the hosted API for protos vs going dedicated for production because of latency and data rules.
[00:00:32] Jordan: Totally. Can you give us a baseline on traffic and model types? Sequence lengths, average tokens per request, that kind of thing?
[00:00:40] Evan: Sure. Most calls are short-ish clinical summarization prompts, so average 120 tokens out, 40 tokens in. But we have some longer patient-note generation at night pushing 1k tokens. Concurrency during peak is around 180 reqs/sec, sustained spiky events to 500 rps for a few minutes.
[00:01:01] Priya: 500 for a few minutes - ok. Are those bursts predictable (e.g., cron) or user-driven?
[00:01:07] Maya: Mostly user-driven (consumer app), but we do have batch jobs at 2am for nightly ingestion which cause sustained higher token throughput for 20-30 minutes.
[00:01:16] Carlos: And top concern for us is isolation for PHI adjacency. We need tenant fencing and VPC peering, plus key management.
[00:01:24] Jordan: Understood. For isolation, Dedicated (reserved) gives you node-level tenancy. You can have private VPC networking and customer KMS—Priya can speak to specifics.
[00:01:33] Priya: Yeah, on Redwood Private or Dedicated we support VPC peering and bring-your-own-KMS flow. We also provide audit logs and per-request tracing. Our dedicated pools can be sharded so a tenant maps to a subset of GPUs, that helps with cold-start and noisy neighbor.
[00:01:47] Evan: When you say sharded, do we get hard allocation? We can't afford another tenant sharing our GPUs for compliance.
[00:01:54] Priya: You can get exclusive nodes. We offer fully isolated node pools as a configuration—there's a lift in cost vs shared reserved but meets compliance.
[00:02:02] Maya: What's a typical GPU profile you recommend for 180 rps and those 1k token nightly jobs?
[00:02:09] Priya: Rough ballpark: depends on model. If you're using a 13B dense FP16-ish model, maybe 4-6 A100-type GPUs to hit p95 under 300ms for short requests; for the 1k token jobs you need batch windows or bigger sequence-optimized configs—could be fewer GPUs but longer wall-clock time per request. We can run a sizing benchmark.
[00:02:28] Jordan: Also worth noting quantization and compilation choices affect memory footprint. For larger batches we typically recommend 8xA100 for predictable throughput for enterprise.
[00:02:36] Evan: Any option to auto-burst into hosted capacity if our reserved pool is saturated?
[00:02:41] Priya: Yes, we support automatic fallback to compatible hosted variants. You define policy: failover, degrade to smaller model, or queue. That way you get soft SLOs preserved.
[00:02:52] Carlos: But that hosted fallback has to be encrypted and logs separated, that's non-negotiable.
[00:02:56] Jordan: Absolutely - hosted fallback will still use encrypted transit and we can limit what metadata is sent. We'll capture audit trails for any fallback calls. We'll include that in the security doc.
[00:03:06] Evan: Pricing question — how do you charge for burst traffic that goes to hosted?
[00:03:11] Jordan: It's usage-based for hosted token consumption; reserved capacity is a flat monthly commit. For mixed mode we can do blended rates for overflow. We'll put tiers in the quote.
[00:03:20] Maya: We need to model cost per 1M requests across our mix.
[00:03:24] Priya: We'll include a cost model with assumptions and also show how batching reduces per-token cost. For example, prefix caching and KV cache can avoid re-computation for repeated context.
[00:03:34] Evan: Speaking of cache, does Redwood do prefix caching across requests for similar patient summaries?
[00:03:39] Priya: Yes, automatic prefix/KV caching is part of the runtime. It helps a lot where requests share history. Note that for PHI you can disable shared cache and use per-tenant cache on dedicated nodes.
[00:03:50] Carlos: KMS integration - we use AWS KMS with an HSM-backed key policy. Does that work?
[00:03:55] Priya: We support AWS KMS and external HSM via BYOK flows. That will be in the compliance pack we share.
[00:04:01] Evan: Can you describe a realistic benchmark plan? We want to validate p95 < 350ms for interactive and confirm nightly batch completes in 30 minutes.
[00:04:09] Priya: Our plan: we prepare harness that replays your anonymized tokenized traffic at production QPS. Step 1: small smoke tests; Step 2: 1-hour warm-up to populate KV cache and prewarm; Step 3: scale up to peak bursts for controlled intervals; Step 4: long-tail latency analysis. We'll record token counts, GPU utilization, KV hit rates, latency percentiles.
[00:04:26] Maya: Sounds good — do you need model checkpoints or can we point to a model in S3?
[00:04:31] Priya: Either works. For private deployment we can pull from your S3 behind a VPC endpoint. For hosted we can accept a secured model upload.
[00:04:39] Evan: Any constraints on custom layers or LoRA? We do some lightweight fine-tuning.
[00:04:44] Priya: Fine-tuned models are fine; we recommend exporting as standard transformer weights. We test quantization safety; sometimes LoRA layers need special handling but we can incorporate that in the benchmark.
[00:04:55] Carlos: Audit logs - retention and export options? We need 12 months retention and SAML SSO with SCIM provisioning.
[00:05:02] Jordan: We can meet 12 months retention as a paid add-on, and SSO/SCIM is supported for enterprise accounts. We'll share the exact table for log schema.
[00:05:10] Evan: What about prewarm / warm pool strategies? Do you pre-warm models or maintain a hot-pool?
[00:05:15] Priya: We offer warm pools that keep models loaded on GPUs for fast cold-starts. For dedicated you can reserve a 'hotpool' of nodes that are always loaded for latency-sensitive routes. There's a cost tradeoff but it dramatically improves p99 cold-starts.
[00:05:28] Maya: How quickly can a warm pool scale? If we suddenly go from 100 to 500 rps, what's practical?
[00:05:33] Priya: Depends on prewarmed capacity. If you have hotpool headroom, scale is instant up to that headroom. For beyond that we have burst policies that bring up more nodes; startup can be 60-120 seconds with optimized prewarm images. That's why a small hotpool plus fallback to hosted covers the spike window.
[00:05:49] Evan: Is preemption possible? We don't want our nodes preempted by lower-tier customers.
[00:05:53] Priya: For dedicated reserved you get eviction protection; we will not preempt your exclusive nodes. For shared reservations there can be soft preemption per policy, but we can arrange priority.
[00:06:03] Jordan: Quick note - we also have instrumentation in Redwood Console for token-level cost breakdown so you can attribute spend by route. That helps in modeling.
[00:06:11] Maya: Security review: will you sign a DPA and allow penetration testing?
[00:06:16] Carlos: And we may need to review network diagrams for private deployment.
[00:06:20] Jordan: Yes to DPA and pen testing under standard terms. We'll provide architecture docs and a redacted network diagram for the intake.
[00:06:27] Priya: One operational note: for that nightly 1k token batch, we often recommend a separate batch-optimized queue with different concurrency and model variant to avoid impacting interactive routes.
[00:06:36] Evan: Good idea. And can we pin model versions and do canary deploys?
[00:06:40] Priya: Yes, rollouts with canaries and automatic fallback are supported. You can pin a model at the route level.
[00:06:46] Maya: For benchmarking, what success criteria should we set? p95 < 350ms interactive, nightly completion under 30 minutes, and less than 5% error rate?
[00:06:54] Priya: That's reasonable. We'll capture those metrics and propose adjustments to GPU count and batch sizes.
[00:06:59] Evan: Where do we add cost? We need an approximate monthly committed price for exclusive 8 GPUs vs 4 GPUs plus heavier hosted overflow.
[00:07:07] Jordan: I'll include two draft pricing scenarios in the proposal: 4xA100 exclusive, 8xA100 exclusive, and a hybrid 4x exclusive + hosted overflow model.
[00:07:16] (Brief cross talk)
[00:07:20] Carlos: We also need data residency — EU vs US. Some patients are EU-based.
[00:07:24] Jordan: We have regions in US and EU. You can restrict model serving regionally. For Private on-prem, that's obviously local.
[00:07:31] Maya: Any timeline commitments for ramp? If we sign next month.
[00:07:36] Priya: Time-to-first-traffic depends on model readiness and infra. Typical lead for dedicated isolated pool is 4-6 weeks after contract and access to model artifacts. Benchmarks can be scheduled in 1-2 weeks.
[00:07:47] Evan: Good — rough roadmap then: benchmark, then pilot, then full commit.
[00:07:51] Jordan: Sounds standard. We'll also add options for 6- and 12-month reserved terms with different discounts.
[00:07:58] Maya: One last non-tech question — who will be our onboarding PM on Redwood's side?
[00:08:02] Jordan: That'll be Lina Torres (Customer Success). I'll cc her on the next email.
[00:08:07] Evan: Ok thanks.
[00:08:10] Priya: A few more very quick details: our runtime supports dynamic kernel selection; in practice we profile your sequence length distributions to pick the fastest kernels and quantization route. That often changes GPU counts significantly.
[00:08:24] Maya: Can you share an example profile from similar customers?
[00:08:28] Priya: We have anonymized profiles and case studies; I'll include a short one that shows p95 improvement from 600ms to 280ms after tuning.
[00:08:37] Evan: Let's talk compliance specifics after this call. Carlos will be the point.
[00:08:42] Carlos: Yep.
[00:08:44] Jordan: Great. Recap and next steps: I'll send a proposal and pricing scenarios; Priya will share benchmark plan and prebuilt scripts; Evan will send tokenized traffic sample; Carlos will share compliance checklist. Schedule a live benchmark for week of 2025-09-08.
[00:08:58] Maya: Works for us.
[00:09:01] Jordan: Perfect, thanks everyone. We'll follow up with calendar invites.
[00:09:06] (Call ends)

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-hearings noted in audio, 'KV cache' sometimes transcribed as 'K V cash', and company internal acronyms were expanded where possible.
